title: CR4T: Rewrite-Based Guardrails for Adolescent LLM Safety
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in adolescent digital environments, mediating information seeking, advice, and emotionally sensitive interactions. Yet existing safety mechanisms remain largely grounded in adult-centric norms and operationalize safety through refusal-oriented suppression. While such approaches may reduce immediate policy violations, they can also create conversational dead-ends, limit constructive guidance, and fail to address the developmental vulnerabilities inherent in adolescent-AI interactions. We argue that adolescent LLM safety should be framed not solely as a filtering problem, but as a socio-technical, developmentally aligned transformation problem. To operationalize this perspective, we propose Critique-and-Revise-for-Teenagers (CR4T), a model-agnostic safeguarding framework that selectively reconstructs unsafe or refusal-style outputs into ageappropriate, guidance-oriented responses while preserving benign intent. CR4T combines lightweight risk detection with domain-conditioned rewriting to remove risk-amplifying content, reduce unnecessary conversational shutdown, and introduce developmentally appropriate guidance. Experimental results show that targeted rewriting substantially reduces unsafe and refusal-oriented outcomes while avoiding unnecessary intervention on acceptable interactions. These findings suggest that selective response reconstruction offers a more human-centered alternative to refusal-centric guardrails for adolescent-facing LLM systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new, developmentally aligned safety framework (CR4T) for LLMs used by adolescents, replacing pure refusal-based moderation with selective rewriting into age-appropriate, guidance-oriented responses.
AI.motivation: LLMs are now mediating many aspects of adolescents’ online lives, including information seeking, advice, and emotional support. Current safety systems are designed around adult norms and focus on refusal and suppression of potentially unsafe content. This can create conversational dead-ends, miss adolescents’ developmental needs, and fail to provide constructive support when youth are vulnerable and seeking help. The authors want a more nuanced, human-centered, and developmentally appropriate approach to safety for teen users.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize adolescent LLM safety as a socio-technical transformation problem rather than a filtering problem. They introduce CR4T (Critique-and-Revise-for-Teenagers), a model-agnostic safeguarding pipeline. CR4T first performs lightweight risk detection on model outputs, and when risk or over-refusal is detected, it applies domain-conditioned rewriting to transform the response. The rewriting aims to remove risk-amplifying content, avoid unnecessary refusals, and insert developmentally appropriate, guidance-oriented language, all while preserving the benign intent of the original answer. They then experimentally evaluate CR4T’s impact on safety, refusal rates, and over-intervention compared to baseline guardrails.
AI.result: Experiments show that CR4T’s targeted rewriting significantly reduces both unsafe responses and overly refusal-oriented outputs in adolescent-relevant interactions. At the same time, it avoids intervening when the original model output is already acceptable, indicating that the approach is selective rather than blunt. Overall, the framework improves the balance between safety, helpfulness, and conversational continuity.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that framing adolescent LLM safety as selective response reconstruction—via critique and revision—provides a more human-centered and developmentally aligned alternative to conventional refusal-centric guardrails. Instead of simply blocking or suppressing risky content, systems can transform it into age-appropriate, supportive guidance, better meeting adolescents’ needs while still mitigating risk.

title: Broadening Access to Transportation Safety Data with Generative AI: A Schema-Grounded Framework for Spatial Natural Language Queries
summary: Transportation safety analysis requires integrating crash records, roadway attributes, and geospatial data through GIS-based workflows, but access remains uneven across agencies and community stakeholders. Technical prerequisites create a gap between analytical tools central to safety planning and the practitioners able to use them. Local agencies, school committees, and residents may have safety concerns but limited capacity to retrieve, filter, map, and analyze relevant data. Generative AI offers a way to narrow this divide, but its public-sector use raises questions about reliability, reproducibility, and governance. This paper presents a schema-grounded natural language interface for transportation safety analysis, using a large language model (LLM) to interpret user intent while preserving deterministic, reviewable execution against an authoritative database. User queries are translated into structured semantic frames, validated by a rule-based layer, compiled into a typed directed acyclic graph of spatial operations, and executed against a PostGIS database. This bounded design separates language interpretation from deterministic execution, keeping results reproducible and schema-grounded while removing access barriers. The framework is evaluated using a statewide Massachusetts transportation safety database integrating crash records, roadway attributes, and geospatial layers including schools, bus stops, crosswalks, and municipal boundaries. All queries executed successfully; the validation layer corrects errors in 29% of evaluation queries, reflecting the gap between flexible natural language and strict schema-grounded requirements. The results suggest that combining natural language accessibility with deterministic execution is a practical direction for broadening access to transportation safety data, with implications for trustworthy AI in public-sector planning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a natural-language interface for transportation safety analysis that uses an LLM only for intent interpretation while keeping all data operations deterministic, schema-grounded, and reproducible in a GIS/PostGIS environment.
AI.motivation: Transportation safety planning depends on integrating crash, roadway, and geospatial data via GIS, but many local agencies and community stakeholders lack the technical capacity to retrieve, filter, and analyze these data. At the same time, directly exposing generative AI for analysis raises concerns about reliability and governance. The motivation is to lower the barrier to using safety data through natural language while maintaining rigor, transparency, and control over how analyses are executed.
AI.method: The authors design a schema-grounded natural language interface. User questions in natural language are first parsed by a large language model into structured semantic frames. These frames are then validated and, when possible, corrected by a rule-based validation layer based on the database schema and allowed operations. Validated frames are compiled into a typed directed acyclic graph (DAG) of spatial operations, which is then executed deterministically on a PostGIS database containing crash records, roadway attributes, and other geospatial layers. The system is evaluated on a statewide Massachusetts transportation safety database and a set of natural language queries.
AI.result: All evaluation queries can be executed successfully on the system. The rule-based validation layer detects and corrects schema or intent mismatches in about 29% of queries, demonstrating both the frequency of discrepancies between user language and strict database requirements and the effectiveness of the validation layer in bridging that gap. The system maintains deterministic, reproducible execution while allowing flexible natural language input.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid architecture that uses LLMs only for language understanding and delegates all analytics to a deterministic, schema-grounded execution engine can make transportation safety data more broadly accessible without sacrificing reproducibility and governance. This approach shows promise as a trustworthy AI pattern for public-sector planning applications, where transparency and control are essential but many stakeholders need easy, natural-language access to complex spatial data.

title: Sem-Detect: Semantic Level Detection of AI Generated Peer-Reviews
summary: How can we distinguish whether a peer review was written by a human or generated by an AI model? We argue that, in this setting, authorship should not be attributed solely from the textual features of a review, but also from the ideas, judgments, and claims it expresses. To this end, we propose Sem-Detect, an authorship detection method for peer reviews that operationalizes this principle by combining textual features with claim-level semantic analysis. Sem-Detect compares a target review against multiple AI-generated reviews of the same paper, leveraging the observation that different AI models tend to converge on similar points, while human reviewers introduce more unique and diverse ones. As a result, Sem-Detect is able to distinguish fully AI reviews from authentic human-written ones, including those that have been refined using an LLM but still reflect human judgment. Across a dataset of over 20,000 peer reviews from ICLR and NeurIPS conferences, Sem-Detect improves over the strongest baseline by 25.5% in TPR@0.1% FPR in the binary setting. Moreover, in the three-class scenario, we empirically show that LLM refinement preserves the semantic signals of human reviews, which remain distinct from the patterns exhibited by fully AI-generated text; as a result, fewer than 3.5% of LLM-refined human reviews are misclassified as AI-generated.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Sem-Detect, a method to distinguish AI-generated from human-written peer reviews by combining textual features with claim-level semantic analysis and comparison to multiple AI-generated reviews of the same paper.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-text detectors mainly rely on surface-level textual features and struggle when AI output is edited or when humans use LLMs for refinement. In peer review, authorship is tied not just to wording but also to the underlying ideas, judgments, and claims. The authors want a detection method that captures these semantic aspects and can tell apart fully AI-written reviews from human reviews, including those lightly refined with LLMs.
AI.method: They introduce Sem-Detect, which jointly uses textual features and claim-level semantic analysis. For a given target review, they generate multiple AI reviews of the same paper using different LLMs, then compare the target’s claims and ideas to those AI reviews. The method exploits that AI models tend to converge on similar points, whereas human reviewers contribute more unique and diverse claims. A classifier is trained on these semantic similarity patterns, along with standard textual features, to predict whether a review is human, AI-generated, or human with LLM refinement.
AI.result: On a dataset of over 20,000 ICLR and NeurIPS reviews, Sem-Detect substantially outperforms prior detectors. In the binary classification setting (human vs fully AI-generated), it improves TPR@0.1% FPR by 25.5% over the strongest baseline. In a three-class setup (human, fully AI, human+LLM-refined), it shows that LLM refinement largely preserves the semantic signature of human judgment: fewer than 3.5% of LLM-refined human reviews are misclassified as fully AI-generated.
AI.conclusion: Authorship of peer reviews should be inferred not only from text style but from the underlying ideas and claims. By leveraging claim-level semantic comparisons to multiple AI-generated reviews of the same paper, Sem-Detect can reliably distinguish fully AI-generated reviews from genuine human reviews, even when the latter are refined with LLMs, and significantly outperforms existing AI-text detection baselines.

title: Probabilistic Attribution For Large Language Models
summary: The generative nature of Large Language Models (LLMs) is reflected in the conditional probabilities they compute to sample each response token given the previous tokens. These probabilities encode the distributional structure that the model learns in training and exploits in inference. In this work, we use these probabilities to situate LLMs within the mathematical theory of stochastic processes. We use this framework to design a model-agnostic probabilistic token attribution measure, using Bayes rule to invert the next-token log-probabilities so as to capture the models internal representation of the distribution over token sequences. The representation is independent of the models computational structure. This representation yields the conditional probability of the response given the prompt, and of the response given the prompt with a token marginalized away. Our attribution score is the log of the ratio of these probabilities. We further compute the entropies of a single prompts token distributions, conditioned on the remaining context. The interplay between entropy and attribution score sheds light on LLM behavior. We evaluate 8 models across 7 prompts and investigate anomalies, token sensitivity, response stability, model stability, and training convergence, thereby improving interpretability and guiding users to focus on uncertain or unstable parts of the generation.

AI.tldr: They reinterpret LLM text generation as a stochastic process and define a probabilistic measure that attributes each output token to prompt tokens, using only model log-probabilities, to better understand uncertainty and instability in generations.
AI.motivation: Current LLM interpretability methods often depend on model internals (e.g., attention weights) or architecture-specific probes, and they do not fully leverage the probabilistic nature of token generation. There is a need for a principled, model-agnostic way to attribute output tokens to input tokens and to understand where the model is uncertain or unstable in its predictions.
AI.method: They formally cast LLM generation as a stochastic process defined by conditional next-token probabilities. Using Bayes rule, they invert next-token log-probabilities to recover the model’s internal distribution over sequences. For each prompt-response pair, they compute: (1) the conditional probability of the response given the full prompt; and (2) the conditional probability when a specific prompt token is marginalized out. The attribution score for a token is the log-ratio of these two probabilities. They also compute conditional entropies of token distributions within a prompt, given the rest of the context, and analyze how entropy interacts with attribution scores. Experiments involve 8 different LLMs and 7 prompts.
AI.result: They obtain a model-agnostic token-level attribution score that quantifies how much each prompt token contributes to a given response, based solely on probabilistic behavior, not architectural details. They also produce entropy measures that highlight where the model is uncertain. Across 8 models and 7 prompts, they systematically study anomalies, token sensitivity, response stability, model stability, and training convergence, showing the method can expose differences in behavior and highlight unstable or uncertain parts of generations.
AI.conclusion: Viewing LLMs as stochastic processes and inverting their next-token probabilities enables an architecture-independent probabilistic representation of token sequences. From this, one can derive a principled token attribution score and entropy-based uncertainty measures. These tools improve interpretability of LLM behavior and can guide users and practitioners to focus on uncertain or unstable regions of generated text and on model behaviors that may warrant further scrutiny or refinement.

title: Benchmarking and Improving Monitors for Out-Of-Distribution Alignment Failure in LLMs
summary: Many safety and alignment failures of large language models (LLMs) occur due to out-of-distribution (OOD) situations: unusual prompt or response patterns that are unforeseen by model developers. We systematically study whether LLM monitoring pipelines can detect these OOD alignment failures by introducing a benchmark called Misalignment Out Of Distribution (MOOD). It is difficult to find failures that are truly OOD for off-the-shelf models trained on vast safety datasets. We sidestep this by including a restricted training set in MOOD that we use to train our own monitors, as well as seven test sets with diverse alignment failures that are outside the training distribution. Using MOOD, we find that guard models (safety classifiers) often fail to generalize OOD. To fix this, we propose combining guard models with OOD detectors. We test four types of OOD detectors and find that a combination of a guard model with Mahalanobis distance and perplexity-based OOD detectors can improve recall from 39% to 45%. We also establish positive scaling trends across model scales for monitors that combine a guard model and OOD detector; we find that incorporating OOD detection into monitoring achieves a higher recall gain than using a guard model with 20 times more parameters. Our work suggests that OOD detection should be a crucial component of LLM monitoring and provides a foundation for further work on this important problem.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MOOD, a benchmark to test whether LLM safety monitors can detect out-of-distribution (OOD) alignment failures, and shows that combining guard models with OOD detectors significantly improves recall on such failures.
AI.motivation: Many safety and alignment failures in LLMs arise in out-of-distribution settings where prompts or responses differ from those seen during training, and existing guard models (safety classifiers) often miss these failures. There is a need for a systematic way to evaluate and improve the robustness of monitoring pipelines to such OOD alignment failures.
AI.method: The authors build a benchmark, MOOD, that includes a restricted training set for training custom guard models and seven diverse OOD test sets containing various alignment failures not represented in the training distribution. They then evaluate baseline guard models on MOOD, and explore augmenting them with four types of OOD detectors, including Mahalanobis distance and perplexity-based methods, to see how well these combined systems detect OOD misalignment. They also analyze scaling trends across model sizes for these combined monitors.
AI.result: They find that standalone guard models generalize poorly to OOD alignment failures. By combining guard models with OOD detectors—particularly Mahalanobis distance and perplexity-based detectors—they increase recall on OOD failures from 39% to 45%. They additionally show that scaling up model size improves performance, and that adding OOD detection yields a larger recall gain than simply increasing the guard model’s parameter count by 20x.
AI.conclusion: OOD conditions are a major source of safety failures for LLMs, and current guard models are insufficiently robust on their own. Integrating explicit OOD detection into monitoring pipelines meaningfully improves detection of misalignment, is more effective than large increases in model size, and should be treated as a core component of LLM safety monitoring. The MOOD benchmark offers a standardized testbed for future research on this problem.

title: RankJudge: A Multi-Turn LLM-as-a-Judge Synthetic Benchmark Generator
summary: As interactive LLM-based applications are created and refined, model developers need to evaluate the quality of generated text along many possible axes. For simpler systems, human evaluation may be practical, but in complicated systems like conversational chatbots, the amount of generated text can overwhelm human annotation resources. Model developers have begun to rely heavily on auto-evaluation, where LLMs are also used to judge generation quality. However, existing LLM-as-a-judge benchmarks largely focus on simple Q\&A tasks that do not match the complexity of multi-turn conversations. We introduce RankJudge, a benchmark generator for evaluating LLM-as-a-judge on multi-turn conversations grounded in reference documents. RankJudge creates pairs of conversations where one conversation has a single flaw injected into one turn. This construction allows paired conversations to be labeled unambiguously as better or worse, and precisely isolates failure categories to individual turns, enabling a strict joint correctness criterion for judging. We implement RankJudge across the domains of machine learning, biomedicine, and finance, evaluate 21 frontier LLM judges, and rank those judges via the Bradley-Terry model. Our formulation also allows ranking each conversation pair with difficulty ratings, which we use to dynamically curate the evaluation slice to reduce label noise, as confirmed via human annotation. We find that judge rankings are stable under partial observability, coarser correctness criteria, and an alternative random-walk rating algorithm.

AI.tldr: RankJudge is a benchmark framework that generates controlled pairs of multi-turn, document-grounded conversations with injected flaws to rigorously evaluate and rank LLMs used as automatic judges.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-as-a-judge benchmarks focus mostly on simple, single-turn Q&A and do not capture the complexity and failure modes in long, multi-turn conversations. Human evaluation becomes infeasible at scale for such settings, so there is a need for reliable automatic judges and a way to measure how good these judges are in realistic conversational scenarios.
AI.method: The authors construct RankJudge, which programmatically generates multi-turn conversations grounded in reference documents across several domains (ML, biomedicine, finance). For each example, they create a pair of conversations: one reference conversation and another where a single, controlled flaw is injected in exactly one turn. This produces a clear, unambiguous ranking between the two conversations and localizes the error type. They then evaluate 21 frontier LLMs acting as judges on these pairs, model the judges’ comparative performance using the Bradley–Terry model, and also assign difficulty ratings to conversation pairs. These difficulty scores are used to dynamically select cleaner subsets of data to reduce label noise, and they probe robustness under different observability and correctness criteria as well as with an alternative random-walk–based rating algorithm.
AI.result: RankJudge produces a large set of labeled conversation pairs with precise, turn-level failure categorizations. Using this benchmark, the authors obtain a ranking over 21 LLM judges and show that these rankings are consistent under different evaluation choices: partial access to conversation context, more coarse-grained correctness criteria, and an alternative rating method (random-walk–based) instead of Bradley–Terry. They also show that difficulty-aware curation reduces label noise, supported by human annotation checks.
AI.conclusion: RankJudge provides a scalable, controlled, and realistic benchmark for evaluating LLMs that serve as automatic judges in complex, multi-turn conversational settings. By injecting single, localized flaws into one conversation in each pair, it enables precise, unambiguous preference labels and robust ranking of judge models. The stability of rankings across modeling assumptions and the ability to use difficulty ratings to manage label noise suggest that RankJudge is a reliable tool for selecting and analyzing LLM judges for real-world applications.

title: TO-Agents: A Multi-Agent AI Pipeline for Preference-Guided Topology Optimization
comment: Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the ASME 2026 International Design Engineering Technical Conferences (IDETC2026)
summary: Topology optimization can generate efficient structures, but designers often must manually translate qualitative intent, such as desired visual style, product experience, or manufacturability into solver settings that are not directly tied to those preferences. We present TO-Agents, a multi-agent AI framework that connects natural-language design intent with iterative topology optimization. The framework converts a human-provided problem description into validated solver inputs, runs a topology optimization solver, renders the resulting 3D topology, and uses multi-view vision-language reasoning with an independent judge agent to critique each result and revise solver parameters. We evaluate the framework on two long-horizon design tasks: a cantilever beam benchmark and a phone-stand product design. In both tasks, the designer specifies an aesthetic preference for hierarchically branched structures inspired by natural tree morphologies, and the system performs four revision cycles across ten independent replicates. TO-Agents produces at least one preference-aligned design in 60% of trials for each case study, corresponding to up to 6x more successful trials than an ablated pipeline without visual or historical feedback. Judge scores and human evaluations show that the pipeline can identify effective parameter levers, recover from poor revisions, and expand design exploration. A manufacturing agent further post-processes top-ranked designs for additive manufacturing, enabling end-to-end intent-to-prototype design. We also identify failure modes, including overshooting, selective memory, misplaced tools, and incorrect parameter reasoning. These results suggest that agentic topology optimization can shift designers from low-level parameter tuning toward higher-level specification of form and function, while highlighting safeguards needed for reliable autonomous engineering design.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TO-Agents, a multi-agent AI framework that links natural-language design intent to iterative topology optimization using language and vision-language agents that refine solver parameters over multiple cycles, yielding more preference-aligned and manufacturable designs than baselines.
AI.motivation: Topology optimization can generate highly efficient structures, but its solver parameters are low-level and not directly connected to designers’ qualitative goals such as aesthetics, user experience, or manufacturability; designers must manually translate these fuzzy intentions into technical settings, which is difficult and limits broader use. The authors aim to bridge this gap so that natural-language design intent can drive automated, iterative topology optimization and downstream manufacturing preparation.
AI.method: The authors build TO-Agents, a multi-agent framework in which language and vision-language agents: (1) parse a natural-language design brief into validated topology optimization inputs; (2) run a topology optimization solver; (3) render the resulting 3D topology from multiple views; (4) use a vision-language “judge” agent to critique results with respect to the stated intent and prior history; and (5) revise solver parameters for the next optimization cycle. They evaluate on two long-horizon tasks (a cantilever beam and a phone stand with tree-like aesthetics), perform four revision cycles per run, compare against an ablated pipeline without visual or historical feedback, and incorporate a manufacturing agent to post-process top designs for additive manufacturing.
AI.result: In both the cantilever beam and phone-stand case studies, TO-Agents generates at least one design aligned with the user’s tree-like aesthetic preference in 60% of trials, representing up to a 6x improvement in successful trials over an ablated baseline. Judge scores and human evaluations indicate that the system can discover meaningful parameter levers, recover from poor intermediate revisions, and broaden the explored design space. A manufacturing agent successfully adapts top-ranked designs for additive manufacturing, achieving an end-to-end workflow from intent to prototype-ready geometry. The authors also systematically observe characteristic failure modes such as overshooting parameter changes, losing or selectively remembering prior context, misusing tools, and incorrect reasoning about parameters.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that an agentic, multi-modal topology optimization pipeline can effectively connect natural-language specifications of form and function to low-level solver parameters, helping designers focus on higher-level intent rather than manual parameter tuning. TO-Agents improves alignment with aesthetic preferences and supports manufacturable outputs, but its observed failure modes underscore the need for safeguards and reliability enhancements before such autonomous engineering systems can be robustly deployed in practice.

title: PromptNCE: Pointwise Mutual Information Predictions Using Only LLMs and Contrastive Estimation Prompts
summary: Estimating mutual information from text usually requires training a task-specific critic, which limits its use in low-data settings. We ask whether large language models can instead estimate pointwise mutual information zero-shot, using only prompts and elicited probabilities. We introduce a benchmark with human-derived ground-truth PMI across three publicly available datasets, and evaluate five information-theoretic prompting-based estimators. Our main method, PromptNCE, frames conditional probability estimation as a contrastive task and augments the candidate set with an explicit OTHER category. We show theoretically that adding OTHER recovers the true conditional P(y | x) rather than just a ranking over listed candidates, turning a contrastive prompt into a general-purpose zero-shot probability estimator. PromptNCE is the best zero-shot method on all three datasets, reaching Spearman correlation up to 0.82 with human-derived PMI. We also present a case study in computer science education showing how these estimators can be used to score student knowledge summaries in a low-data setting.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes PromptNCE, a prompting-based method that lets large language models estimate pointwise mutual information (PMI) zero-shot, without task-specific training, and shows it correlates highly with human-derived PMI across datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to estimating mutual information from text depend on training task-specific critics or discriminators, which is impractical in low-data scenarios and for rapid, general-purpose use. The authors want to know whether large language models, via prompting and reading off probabilities, can directly provide accurate pointwise mutual information estimates without additional training. This would make mutual information estimation more accessible, data-efficient, and broadly applicable, including in educational settings where labeled data is scarce.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark with human-derived ground-truth PMI across three public text datasets. They design and compare five prompting-based estimators that use large language models to produce probabilities from which PMI can be computed. Their main method, PromptNCE, casts conditional probability estimation P(y | x) as a contrastive task: given a context x and candidate labels y, the model selects among them and an additional explicit OTHER option. They provide a theoretical analysis showing that including OTHER allows recovery of the actual conditional distribution over y instead of just a relative ranking of listed candidates. They then compute PMI from these estimated probabilities and evaluate performance via correlation with human-derived PMI. They also apply the approach to an educational case study, using the estimators to score student knowledge summaries when only limited labeled data is available.
AI.result: PromptNCE outperforms other zero-shot prompting-based PMI estimators on all three benchmark datasets. It achieves Spearman correlations up to 0.82 between model-estimated PMI and human-derived PMI, indicating strong alignment with human judgments of association. The theoretical result establishes that adding an explicit OTHER option to the contrastive prompt allows recovery of true conditional probabilities instead of only a ranking over candidates. In the computer science education case study, the method successfully scores student knowledge summaries in a low-data setting, demonstrating a practical benefit of the approach.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that large language models can serve as effective zero-shot estimators of pointwise mutual information when appropriately prompted. PromptNCE, which augments contrastive prompts with an OTHER category, provides a principled way to recover full conditional probabilities and achieves strong agreement with human-derived PMI. This makes PMI estimation feasible in low-data contexts and opens up applications such as automated scoring of student work, suggesting a broader role for prompting-based information-theoretic tools built on top of large language models.

title: The Shape of Testimony: A Scalable Framework for Oral History Archive Comparison
summary: Researchers in Holocaust studies have often distinguished between two styles of oral survivor testimony: the USC Shoah Foundation's interviews tend to follow a structured, interviewer-guided format, whereas the Yale Fortunoff Video Archive generally favors a more free-form, open-ended style. This distinction has influenced both scholarly research and the development of later archives. In this study, we critically examine that claim by conducting a large-scale computational analysis of more than 1,600 testimonies from both collections. Leveraging discourse segmentation, topic modeling, and large language model (LLM) based analysis, we quantify the "structuredness" level of testimonies through topic coherence, interviewer-survivor dynamics, and the distribution of question types. Our results generally corroborate the structural differences identified in earlier research, while also revealing significant overlaps between the collections, both within individual interviews and across common narrative patterns. This complicates the simple "structured vs. free-form" dichotomy often applied to these oral histories. Beyond revisiting a foundational claim in Holocaust studies, our work provides a scalable, replicable framework for comparative corpus analysis. As a proof of concept, it suggests broader applications for digital oral history, narrative analysis, and the design of citizen-science annotation platforms.

AI.tldr: The paper computationally compares structure in two major Holocaust survivor testimony archives and finds both expected differences and substantial overlaps, challenging a simple structured vs. free-form dichotomy.
AI.motivation: Scholars have long claimed that USC Shoah interviews are structured and interviewer-led, while Yale Fortunoff interviews are open-ended and free-form. This binary has shaped research and archive design but has not been rigorously tested at scale. The authors want to empirically assess this influential claim using modern computational methods.
AI.method: They conduct a large-scale analysis of over 1,600 testimonies from both archives. Using discourse segmentation, topic modeling, and large language model–based analyses, they operationalize and quantify "structuredness" via metrics such as topic coherence, interviewer–survivor interaction patterns, and distributions of question types.
AI.result: The computational measures largely confirm that USC Shoah testimonies are more structured and interviewer-guided, while Fortunoff testimonies display more open-form characteristics. However, the data also reveal significant overlaps within and across collections, including shared narrative patterns and segments in individual interviews that do not fit the stereotypical style of their archive.
AI.conclusion: The study validates but complicates the established distinction between structured USC Shoah and free-form Fortunoff interviews, showing that the reality is more continuous than binary. It also introduces a scalable, replicable framework for comparative analysis of large oral history corpora, with potential applications to digital oral history, narrative studies, and citizen-science annotation platform design.

title: Reflective Prompt Tuning through Language Model Function-Calling
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become increasingly capable of following instructions and complex reasoning, making prompting a flexible interface for adapting models without parameter updates. Yet prompt design remains labor-intensive and highly sensitive to formatting, phrasing, and instruction order, motivating automated prompt optimization methods that reduce manual effort while preserving inference-time flexibility. However, existing methods often search over prompt candidates or use fixed critique-refine pipelines driven by individual examples or small batches, limiting their ability to capture systematic error patterns and make targeted edits grounded in failure history. We propose Reflective Prompt Tuning (RPT), a framework that uses LLM function calling to simulate the iterative workflow of human prompt engineers. An LLM optimizer calls a diagnostic function that evaluates the target model over an entire optimization set, summarizes recurring failure modes, and returns a structured diagnostic report. The optimizer uses this report, together with an accumulated memory of prior reports, to revise the prompt for the next iteration. RPT further supports confidence-aware optimization by using calibration signals in diagnostic feedback and final prompt selection. Across three reasoning tasks, RPT improves over initial prompts by up to 12.9 points, remains competitive with state of the art, and improves confidence calibration. Our analyses show that RPT is especially effective on multi-hop and mathematical reasoning, producing targeted prompt revisions that align with diagnosed failure patterns and lead to gains in task performance and calibration.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Reflective Prompt Tuning (RPT), an automated framework that iteratively analyzes a model’s systematic errors over a full optimization set and uses structured diagnostic reports to revise prompts, improving reasoning performance and confidence calibration without changing model parameters.
AI.motivation: Prompting is a powerful way to adapt LLMs without parameter updates, but prompt design is manual, brittle to small changes, and difficult to scale. Existing automated prompt optimization methods mainly search over candidates or rely on example-level critique-refine loops, which fail to exploit global patterns of errors across datasets and cannot make focused edits grounded in an accumulated history of failures. The authors aim to automate the workflow of skilled human prompt engineers in a way that captures systematic failure modes, reduces manual effort, and maintains inference-time flexibility.
AI.method: They propose Reflective Prompt Tuning (RPT), where an LLM “optimizer” agent uses function calling to interact with a diagnostic module. In each iteration, the diagnostic function evaluates the target model on an entire optimization set, identifies recurring failure modes, summarizes them into a structured diagnostic report (including calibration signals), and returns this to the optimizer. The optimizer maintains a memory of all previous reports and uses this accumulated history to revise the prompt, generating a new candidate prompt for the next iteration. The process repeats, enabling confidence-aware optimization and final prompt selection based on both accuracy and calibration.
AI.result: On three reasoning benchmarks, RPT improves performance over initial hand-written prompts by up to 12.9 points and remains competitive with state-of-the-art prompt optimization techniques. It is particularly effective on multi-hop and mathematical reasoning tasks, and it yields prompts that produce better-calibrated confidence estimates. Qualitative analyses confirm that the prompt edits produced by RPT correspond closely to the failure modes identified in the diagnostic reports.
AI.conclusion: RPT successfully automates a reflective, dataset-level prompt engineering workflow for LLMs, leveraging structured diagnostics and iterative memory to make targeted prompt revisions. This approach reduces manual prompt design effort, improves both task accuracy and calibration, and is especially valuable for complex reasoning tasks like multi-hop and mathematical reasoning. The framework demonstrates that modeling prompt engineering as an iterative reflection loop over systematic errors is an effective strategy for prompt optimization.

title: MindLoom: Composing Thought Modes for Frontier-Level Reasoning Data Synthesis
comment: Work in Progress. Comments: 27 pages, 4 figures, preprint
summary: Although LLMs have made substantial progress in reasoning, systematically producing frontier-level reasoning data remains difficult. Existing synthesis methods often have limited visibility into the structural factors that govern problem difficulty, which can result in narrow diversity and unstable difficulty control. In this work, we view the difficulty of a reasoning problem as arising from the accumulation of atomic knowledge-reasoning transformations, which we term thought modes. Building on this perspective, we propose MindLoom, a framework for synthesizing frontier-level reasoning data through compositional thought mode engineering. Given a collection of hard problems with verified solutions, MindLoom first decomposes those solutions into thought mode chains that reveal each problem's construction logic. It then trains a retrieval model that matches problem states to compatible thought modes, providing guidance on which reasoning challenges to introduce during synthesis. New problems are composed by iteratively applying retrieved thought modes to seed questions, with distribution-aligned sampling to encourage diverse reasoning coverage. Finally, a rollout-based judging stage labels generated questions by difficulty and supplies judged-correct responses for supervised fine-tuning. We evaluate MindLoom on nine benchmarks covering five STEM disciplines and four mathematical reasoning tasks across multiple model families and sizes. Models fine-tuned on MindLoom-generated data achieves favorable performances over base models, distillation, and external-data baselines across the reported benchmarks. Ablation studies indicate the contribution of each component, and further analysis suggests that MindLoom covers a broad range of reasoning patterns while maintaining useful difficulty control. We have open-sourced our implementation at https://github.com/EachSheep/MindLoom.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MindLoom, a framework that generates high-quality, diverse, and controllable-difficulty reasoning problems for training LLMs by decomposing hard problems into reusable reasoning building blocks (“thought modes”) and recombining them.
AI.motivation: LLMs have improved at reasoning, but it is still hard to systematically create frontier-level reasoning datasets. Existing automatic synthesis methods struggle with: (1) limited understanding of what structurally makes a problem hard, (2) weak control over difficulty, and (3) narrow diversity of reasoning patterns. The authors want a principled way to model and control problem difficulty while generating rich, varied reasoning data for training.
AI.method: They introduce the concept of “thought modes” — atomic knowledge-reasoning transformations that compose into full solutions. The MindLoom pipeline: (1) Start with a set of verified hard problems and their solutions. (2) Decompose these solutions into chains of thought modes that expose how each problem is constructed. (3) Train a retrieval model that, given an intermediate problem state, retrieves compatible thought modes suggesting what reasoning step to introduce next. (4) Generate new problems by iteratively applying retrieved thought modes to seed questions, with sampling calibrated to match the distribution of reasoning patterns in the source data and encourage diversity. (5) Run a rollout-based judging stage where models solve the generated questions; this stage assigns difficulty labels and selects judged-correct solutions, which are then used for supervised fine-tuning. They evaluate across nine benchmarks in five STEM areas and four math reasoning tasks, on multiple LLM families and sizes, and perform ablations on pipeline components.
AI.result: Models fine-tuned on MindLoom-generated data outperform: (1) their base models, (2) distillation-based training, and (3) baselines using external datasets, across the nine reported benchmarks. Ablation results show that each component (thought-mode decomposition, retrieval-guided composition, distribution-aligned sampling, rollout-based judging) contributes meaningfully to performance. Analyses indicate that the generated dataset spans a wide variety of reasoning patterns while allowing reasonably precise control of difficulty levels.
AI.conclusion: Viewing reasoning difficulty as the composition of atomic “thought modes” enables a structured, controllable way to synthesize challenging reasoning problems. MindLoom operationalizes this view through decomposition of hard solutions, retrieval-guided recomposition, and a judging stage, producing high-quality training data that boosts LLM reasoning performance across STEM and math tasks. The framework offers both broad coverage of reasoning behaviors and practical difficulty control, and the open-source release makes it a reusable tool for building frontier-level reasoning datasets.

title: Residual Skill Optimization for Text-to-SQL Ensembles
summary: Text-to-SQL ensembles improve over single-candidate generation by drawing multiple SQL candidates and selecting one, but their effectiveness is bounded by Pass@K, the probability that at least one of K candidates is correct. Existing methods source diversity heuristically through stochastic decoding or prompt variants, leaving candidate sets dominated by correlated failures. We present DivSkill-SQL, a residual skill optimization framework that builds complementary agentic Text-to-SQL ensembles without model fine-tuning: each new skill is optimized on examples the current skill ensemble fails on, provably targeting its marginal contribution to Pass@K. On Spider2-Lite, DivSkill-SQL improves selected accuracy by up to +11.1 points on Snowflake and +8.3 on BigQuery over the strongest ensemble baseline, with consistent gains across two base models (Opus-4.6 and GPT-5.4). Skills optimized on a single dialect transfer without retraining across dialects (Snowflake, BigQuery, SQLite) and to a different task formulation, such as BIRD-Critic (+2.6 pts). Error diagnostics show up to 3x fewer hallucinated schema references and function calls, indicating that gains come from genuinely reliable complementary skills rather than surface-form variation.

AI.tldr: DivSkill-SQL builds complementary, specialized Text-to-SQL ‘skills’ that focus on previously failed queries, forming a more diverse and effective ensemble that significantly boosts accuracy without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Text-to-SQL performance is often boosted by generating multiple SQL candidates and choosing among them, but these ensembles are limited by Pass@K and suffer from correlated errors because diversity is usually induced via ad-hoc methods like stochastic decoding or prompt variations. The authors aim to systematically construct ensembles whose members fail on different examples, thereby increasing the chance that at least one candidate is correct and overcoming the limitations of heuristic diversity.
AI.method: They propose DivSkill-SQL, a residual skill optimization framework. Starting from an initial Text-to-SQL ‘skill’ (agent/prompt configuration), they iteratively train new skills on the residual set: examples that the current ensemble still fails on. Each new skill is optimized to maximize its marginal contribution to Pass@K rather than standalone accuracy. This is done without model fine-tuning, by manipulating prompts/agents and selection policies, and includes a theoretical justification that optimizing on residual failures targets marginal Pass@K improvements. The resulting ensemble is an agentic set of complementary skills whose outputs are aggregated via selection over multiple SQL candidates.
AI.result: On the Spider2-Lite benchmark, DivSkill-SQL significantly outperforms strong ensemble baselines using the same base models (Opus-4.6 and GPT-5.4). It increases selected accuracy by up to +11.1 points on Snowflake and +8.3 points on BigQuery. Skills learned on one SQL dialect transfer well to others (Snowflake, BigQuery, SQLite) and to different task formulations, including BIRD-Critic, where it yields a +2.6-point gain. Error analyses show up to three times fewer hallucinated schema references and function calls relative to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Residual skill optimization can systematically build complementary Text-to-SQL ensemble members that improve Pass@K more effectively than heuristic diversity methods, without requiring model fine-tuning. The resulting DivSkill-SQL ensembles generalize across SQL dialects and related tasks, and their gains stem from more reliable, less hallucination-prone behavior rather than superficial output variation. This suggests that targeted, residual-based skill construction is a principled and transferable way to enhance LLM-based program synthesis systems.

title: AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0: Data Model Expansions and Content Evaluation Framework for Using Agentic AI to Improve Integration between AOPs and New Approach Methodologies (NAMs)
summary: Adverse Outcome Pathways (AOP) are logic models that causally link biological mechanisms that can be measured in a lab to adverse outcomes, relevant to chemical regulatory endpoints. AOPs contextualize new approach methodologies (NAMs), in vitro and in silico methods used as alternatives to animal testing and the sequential events in an AOP serve as multi-scale models spanning biological scales. The AOP-Wiki serves as the global repository for AOPs. While the AOP-Wiki has played a central role in AOP expansion over the past decade, constraints within the current data model and application infrastructure limit the AOP-Wiki from supporting continued AOP growth and evolution. Yet, the transformative power of agentic AI has re-invigorated AOP-Wiki data modernization efforts at a time when core AOP principles can be harnessed to inform use of AI for aggregating and structuring AOP-relevant information. Seizing upon this momentum, we present AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, the third in a series of evidence model prototypes, which concretely demonstrates data model expansions and our vision for how the AOP-Wiki might be transformed to better serve regulatory science and emergent use of AOPs in biomedical and One Health contexts. We aim to lay a foundation to support computationally-generated AOPs and quantitative AOPs (qAOPs) by focussing on solutions for AOP-Wiki internal quality improvement, evidence structuring to enhance AOP FAIRness and AI-readiness, and improved integration between the AOP framework and NAMs to better serve next generation risk assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, an expanded data and evidence model to modernize the AOP-Wiki so it can better support AI, computationally generated AOPs, and quantitative AOPs for regulatory and biomedical applications.
AI.motivation: Current AOP-Wiki data model and infrastructure limit its ability to keep pace with rapid AOP growth, to support emerging AI/NAM applications, and to enable quantitative and computationally generated AOPs. There is a need to improve internal data quality, structure evidence in a FAIR and AI-ready manner, and integrate AOPs more tightly with NAMs for next-generation risk assessment.
AI.method: The authors design and present AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, the third evidence model prototype, which expands the AOP-Wiki data model. They specify new structures for representing evidence, linking multi-scale biological events, improving internal quality controls, and enabling better interoperability with NAMs and AI tools. The prototype serves as a concrete demonstration of how the AOP-Wiki could be technically and semantically transformed.
AI.result: EMOD 3.0 demonstrates that the AOP-Wiki data model can be extended to support richer evidence representation, improved quality management, enhanced FAIRness and AI-readiness, and tighter integration of AOPs with NAM data. It shows a feasible path toward supporting computationally generated AOPs and qAOPs and toward using AI to aggregate and structure AOP-relevant information.
AI.conclusion: Modernizing the AOP-Wiki via EMOD 3.0 can transform it from a static repository into a more dynamic, AI-compatible knowledge infrastructure. This lays the groundwork for computationally generated and quantitative AOPs, improves regulatory and One Health applications, and strengthens the link between AOPs and NAMs for next-generation risk assessment.

title: When Cases Get Rare: A Retrieval Benchmark for Off-Guideline Clinical Question Answering
summary: Across medical specialties, clinical practice is anchored in evidence-based guidelines that codify best studied diagnostic and treatment pathways. These pathways routinely fall short for the long tail of real-world care not covered by guidelines. Most medical large language models (LLMs), however, are trained to encode common, guideline-focused medical knowledge in their parameters. Current evaluations test models primarily on recalling and reasoning with this memorized content, often in multiple-choice settings. Given the fundamental importance of evidence-based reasoning in medicine, it is neither feasible nor reliable to depend on memorization in practice. To address this gap, we introduce OGCaReBench, a free-form retrieval-focused benchmark aimed at evaluating LLMs at answering clinical questions that require going beyond typical guidelines. Extracted from published medical case reports and validated by medical experts, OGCaReBench contains long-form clinical questions requiring free-text answers, providing a systematic framework for assessing open-ended medical reasoning in rare, case-based scenarios. Our experiments reveal that even the best-performing baseline (GPT-5.2) correctly answers only 56% of our benchmark with specialized models only reaching 42%. Augmenting models with retrieved medical articles improves this performance to up to 82% (using GPT-5.2) highlighting the importance of evidence-grounding for real-world medical reasoning tasks. This work thus establishes a foundation for benchmarking and advancing both general-purpose and medical LLMs to produce reliable answers in challenging clinical contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OGCaReBench, a benchmark to test medical LLMs on open-ended, case-based clinical questions that lie beyond standard guidelines, emphasizing retrieval-augmented, evidence-grounded reasoning.
AI.motivation: Clinical care often involves rare or atypical cases not covered by evidence-based guidelines, yet most medical LLMs are trained and evaluated mainly on common, guideline-aligned knowledge and multiple-choice tasks, which stresses memorization rather than real-world, evidence-based reasoning.
AI.method: The authors construct OGCaReBench from published medical case reports, curate long-form clinical questions requiring free-text answers, have these validated by medical experts, and use the benchmark to evaluate baseline general-purpose and specialized medical LLMs, both with and without retrieval of supporting medical articles.
AI.result: Baseline models perform modestly on OGCaReBench, with the strongest baseline (GPT-5.2) correctly answering 56% of questions and specialized medical models only 42%, but performance improves substantially—up to 82% accuracy for GPT-5.2—when models are augmented with retrieved medical literature.
AI.conclusion: OGCaReBench exposes the limitations of current medical LLMs on rare, open-ended clinical scenarios and demonstrates that retrieval-based, evidence-grounded approaches are crucial for reliable medical reasoning, providing a new foundation for evaluating and improving both general and medical LLMs in challenging clinical contexts.

title: Investigating Concept Alignment Using Implausible Category Members
summary: Developing AI systems with a human-like understanding of everyday concepts is a key step towards developing safe, reliable systems whose behavior makes sense to humans. When probing concept understanding, asking questions about plausible category members (e.g., "Is a car a vehicle?") is likely to recall patterns in the model's vast training data. We pursue an alternative strategy, characterizing the boundaries of conceptual categories by asking about implausible category members (e.g., "Is an olive a vehicle?") to probe the kind of concept-level knowledge we take for granted in fellow humans. We characterize concept boundaries for a set of fundamental concepts by studying AI systems' assignments of objects to superordinate categories from a classic psychological study by Rosch and Mervis, as well as their assignments of the same objects to mismatched superordinate categories. We compare these assignments to those made by human participants on the full range of within-category and cross-category assignment tasks. Our results reveal a range of concepts for which which models differ in meaningful and surprising ways from humans, including treating "words" as belonging to categories like "vehicles" and "clothing," identifying several "vegetable" category members as "fruit," and assigning exemplars from non-weapon categories to the "weapons" category. We also demonstrate how these instances of concept misalignment translate into problematic downstream behavior with implications for AI safety.

AI.tldr: The paper probes AI models’ conceptual understanding by testing their judgments on implausible category memberships and finds systematic misalignments with human concept boundaries that can impact safety-critical behavior.
AI.motivation: To build safe, reliable AI whose behavior is understandable to humans, systems need human-like grasp of everyday concepts, including clear boundaries. Typical probing with plausible category questions mainly taps into memorized patterns from training data, not deeper conceptual structure. The authors want a method that better reveals how models represent category boundaries and where they diverge from humans, because such misalignments may create safety risks.
AI.method: They invert the usual evaluation strategy: instead of only asking about plausible category members (e.g., “Is a car a vehicle?”), they also ask about implausible ones (e.g., “Is an olive a vehicle?”). Using a classic Rosch & Mervis dataset of objects and their superordinate categories, they test AI systems on (1) within-category assignments (object to its correct superordinate category) and (2) cross-category, mismatched assignments (object to incorrect superordinate categories). They collect human judgments on the same tasks and then systematically compare model vs. human category assignment patterns, especially at the boundaries and in the implausible cases.
AI.result: They find multiple concept domains where AI models and humans diverge in structured ways. Models sometimes classify items from non-weapon categories as “weapons,” treat abstract entities such as “words” as members of concrete categories like “vehicles” and “clothing,” and mislabel some canonical “vegetable” exemplars as “fruit.” These are not random errors but indicate meaningful differences in concept boundaries and the underlying conceptual structure learned by the models. The authors also show concrete downstream tasks where these misalignments manifest as problematic or unsafe behavior.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current AI systems lack fully human-like concept boundaries and can make categorization judgments that humans would find implausible or alarming, especially in safety-relevant categories. Probing with implausible category membership questions is an informative diagnostic tool for uncovering these misalignments. The conceptual gaps they reveal underscore the need for improved methods to align model concept representations with human ones to ensure safer, more predictable AI behavior.

title: Does Slightly Mean Somewhat? Measuring Vague Intensity Words in LLM Numeric Actions
summary:
Do language models preserve the ordinal meaning of intensity words when those words must produce numeric actions? I study a researcher-constructed scale of 10 English degree modifiers, from slightly to drastically, informed by the Quirk et al. degree-modifier taxonomy, in a controlled resource-allocation environment where Claude Haiku receives a natural-language instruction, produces a numeric allocation, and a deterministic backend converts that allocation into a measurable outcome. The only variable that changes between runs is the intensity word or the starting system state, isolating their effects on the model's numeric output.
  Across 6,620 runs at T=0.0 and T=0.7, three patterns emerge. First, the model compresses 10 intensity words into 5 distinct median outputs: four lower-tier words all map to the same value, while stronger words break into higher regimes (Spearman rho = 0.845, p < 0.001). Second, when the current system state is supplied as context, separate Kruskal-Wallis tests show that grouping by starting allocation captures far more rank-based variance than grouping by word (epsilon-squared baseline = 0.782 vs. epsilon-squared word = 0.079), and lexical differentiation collapses to zero as the system approaches capacity. Third, near feasibility limits the model exhibits three behavioral modes: weak words hedge with small adjustments, strong words abstain entirely, and the word drastically pushes to the local ceiling. These patterns persist across temperature, with stochastic sampling broadening distributions but not restoring ordinal distinctions between words. In this model and domain, the model's numeric interpretation of vague intensity words is compressed, state-dependent, and discontinuous near operational boundaries.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether a language model preserves the ordinal scale of intensity adverbs (e.g., slightly, drastically) when it must turn them into numerical actions. It finds that the model heavily compresses the 10-word scale, makes its numerical choices mostly based on current system state rather than wording, and behaves discontinuously near system limits.
AI.motivation: Language models are often asked to turn vague natural-language intensity words into specific numeric actions (budgets, probabilities, allocations). It is unclear whether models preserve the intended ordinal relationships among such words or how context and system constraints affect their numeric interpretation. Understanding this is important for safety, calibration, and the design of natural-language interfaces for decision making.
AI.method: The author builds a 10-point English degree-modifier scale (from slightly to drastically) based on the Quirk et al. taxonomy. Using Claude Haiku in a controlled resource-allocation task, the model receives instructions containing one of these modifiers and outputs a numeric allocation, which a deterministic backend converts into measurable outcomes. The only factors varied are: (1) which intensity word appears, and (2) the starting system state. The author runs 6,620 trials at temperatures 0.0 and 0.7, then analyzes ordinal relationships and variance using rank-based statistics, including Spearman correlations and Kruskal-Wallis tests with epsilon-squared effect sizes.
AI.result: (1) The 10 intensity words collapse into 5 distinct median numeric outputs, with four weaker modifiers mapping to the same value and stronger ones occupying a few higher regimes (overall ordinal correlation Spearman rho = 0.845, p < 0.001). (2) When the current system state is given as context, starting allocation explains much more rank variance than the choice of word (epsilon-squared baseline = 0.782 vs. 0.079), and differences between words vanish as the system nears capacity. (3) Near feasibility limits, the model shows three modes: weak words trigger small incremental changes, strong words often abstain, and drastically pushes allocations up to the local maximum. These patterns are robust across temperatures: higher temperature widens distributions but does not recover fine-grained ordinal distinctions among words.
AI.conclusion: In this Claude Haiku allocation setting, intensity words are not mapped to a fine-grained, monotonic numeric scale. Instead, their numeric interpretations are compressed into a few buckets, strongly modulated by system state, and become discontinuous near operational limits. This suggests that language models may not reliably preserve ordinal meaning of vague degree modifiers in decision-making tasks, which has implications for designing prompts, interfaces, and safety mechanisms that rely on precise natural-language gradations.

title: The Impact of AI Usage and Informativeness on Skill Development in Logical Reasoning
comment: Accepted at Hybrid Human Artificial Intelligence (HHAI) 2026
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is being increasingly integrated into human problem-solving, yet its effects on individual skill development remain unclear. We examine how both AI usage and informativeness can shape learning in the context of a controlled logical reasoning task with on-demand access to AI assistance. We find that greater AI usage is associated with weaker skill development: heavy AI users underperform relative to comparable peers, whereas light AI users perform similarly to matched users who do not use AI. We also find in our study that these patterns are mediated by AI informativeness. Low-information AI neither improves immediate performance nor preserves performance after AI assistance is removed, and is linked to weaker learning overall. On the other hand, high-information AI was found to improve short-run performance without reducing post-AI outcomes on average in our experiments, but with heterogeneous effects. Our findings in general suggest that AI can, depending on context, either complement human skill development by amplifying independent reasoning or can act as a substitute that undermines such reasoning, with the implication that regulating AI access and usage will be important for promoting skill development in the presence of AI assistance.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how using AI assistance affects people’s ability to learn and improve their own logical reasoning skills over time.
AI.motivation: As AI tools become common in everyday problem-solving, there is uncertainty about whether relying on them helps people learn better or instead causes them to become dependent and develop weaker skills. The authors want to provide causal evidence, in a controlled setting, on how AI use and the quality of AI information influence human learning.
AI.method: The authors run controlled experiments involving a logical reasoning task in which participants can request help from an AI system. They vary how much and how often participants use AI assistance and manipulate the informativeness (quality/clarity/usefulness) of the AI’s outputs. They then compare immediate task performance and post-AI performance when assistance is removed, between heavy AI users, light users, and non-users, and under low- vs high-information AI conditions.
AI.result: Heavy users of AI develop weaker reasoning skills than comparable participants who used AI lightly or not at all. Light users perform about the same as carefully matched non-users in post-AI tests. Low-information AI fails to improve short-run performance and is associated with worse learning overall. High-information AI improves immediate task performance and, on average, does not harm post-AI outcomes, though its effects are heterogeneous across individuals.
AI.conclusion: AI assistance can either complement or substitute for human reasoning, depending on how much people rely on it and how informative its output is. Overreliance on AI, especially when it is not very informative, can undermine learning and skill development. Well-designed, high-information AI can boost performance without necessarily harming long-term skills, but its impact varies by user. Therefore, policies and designs that regulate or structure AI access and usage are important to ensure AI supports, rather than erodes, human skill development.

title: Comparing LLM and Fine-Tuned Model Performance on NVDRS Circumstance Extraction with Varying Prompt Complexity
summary: Suicide is a leading cause of death in the United States, and understanding the circumstances that precede it requires extracting structured information from death investigation narratives. Many of these circumstances require semantic inference beyond simple keyword matching. We develop a ``Complexity Score'' algorithm that analyzes coding manual structure to predict when detailed prompts with full coding guidelines improve over name-only prompts. We then construct a hybrid approach that selects prompt strategy per circumstance. We evaluate large language models (LLMs) against fine-tuned RoBERTa on 25 inferentially complex circumstances from the National Violent Death Reporting System (NVDRS). We found that LLMs substantially outperform on low-prevalence circumstances where training data is insufficient. We further demonstrate that our framework generalizes across frontier LLMs, with GPT-5.2, Gemini 2.5 Pro and Llama-3 70B showing consistent performance patterns. These findings support a hybrid architecture where LLMs handle rare, inferentially complex circumstances while fine-tuned models handle common ones.

AI.tldr: The paper develops and evaluates a hybrid system using LLMs and fine-tuned RoBERTa to extract complex, low-prevalence suicide-related circumstances from death investigation narratives, guided by a new Complexity Score for prompt design.
AI.motivation: Suicide is a major cause of death in the U.S., and accurately understanding the nuanced circumstances preceding suicide from unstructured death investigation narratives is difficult. Many key risk factors are rare and require semantic inference beyond keyword search, and training data for these low-prevalence, complex labels is limited. The authors are motivated to see whether LLMs, carefully prompted, can complement or outperform traditional fine-tuned models for such difficult circumstances, and how to choose the right prompting strategy per label.
AI.method: The authors introduce a Complexity Score algorithm that analyzes the structure of coding manuals (including the depth and richness of guidelines and decision rules) to predict when detailed prompts with full coding instructions will outperform simpler name-only prompts. Using this score, they design a hybrid prompting strategy that selects, for each circumstance variable, whether to use a detailed or minimal prompt. They then compare large language models (including several frontier models) to a fine-tuned RoBERTa classifier on 25 inferentially complex, mostly low-prevalence circumstance labels from the NVDRS dataset, assessing performance across models and prompt types.
AI.result: The study finds that large language models substantially outperform the fine-tuned RoBERTa model on low-prevalence, inferentially complex circumstances where there is insufficient training data for supervised learning to work well. The Complexity Score successfully predicts where detailed prompts yield gains. Performance patterns are consistent across multiple frontier LLMs (GPT-5.2, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Llama-3 70B), showing that the approach is not tied to a single model family.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that LLMs are particularly effective for extracting rare, semantically complex circumstance labels from death narratives, while fine-tuned models remain well-suited for common labels with adequate training data. Their results advocate for a hybrid architecture: use LLMs, guided by the Complexity Score and detailed prompts, for rare and complex variables, and rely on task-specific fine-tuned models for frequent ones. This division of labor yields better overall performance and more robust coding of suicide-related circumstances in public health surveillance data.

title: Latent-space Attacks for Refusal Evasion in Language Models
summary: Safety-aligned language models are trained to refuse harmful requests, yet refusal behavior can be suppressed by steering their internal representations. Existing methods do so by ablating a refusal direction from model activations, aiming to remove refusal from the model's residual stream. Despite their empirical success, these methods lack a principled account of the latent-space transformation they induce and why it suppresses refusal. In this work, we recast refusal suppression as a latent-space evasion attack against linear probes trained to separate refused from answered prompts. Under this view, prior work's difference-in-means direction naturally defines such a probe, and its ablation is exactly a projection onto its decision boundary, i.e., a minimum-confidence evasion attack. This perspective not only explains the empirical success of prior work but also admits a key limitation: evasion stops at the decision boundary, motivating the need to push representations further into the compliant region, i.e., where the model answers. We leverage this by proposing a Controlled Latent-space Evasion attack that projects representations past the boundary with an optimized confidence. We achieve state-of-the-art attack success rate across 15 instruction-tuned, multimodal, and reasoning models, outperforming existing refusal-ablation baselines and specialized jailbreak attacks.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes suppression of safety refusals in language models as an evasion attack in latent space and proposes a stronger controlled evasion method that more reliably pushes model representations into an answer-giving region, outperforming previous jailbreak approaches.
AI.motivation: Safety-aligned language models refuse harmful queries, but recent work shows that internal representation steering can circumvent these safeguards. Existing approaches work empirically but lack a principled view of what operation they implement in latent space and why it defeats refusal, and they may be limited by only minimally crossing the refusal decision boundary. The authors aim to provide a rigorous geometric/probing perspective and design a more powerful, controllable attack.
AI.method: 1) Model the distinction between refusal and answering as a linear separability problem, training linear probes on internal activations to classify prompts as refused vs. answered. 2) Show that prior "difference-in-means" refusal directions correspond to such a probe and that ablating this direction is equivalent to projecting activations onto the probe’s decision boundary (a minimum-confidence evasion attack). 3) Identify the limitation that this only reaches the boundary. 4) Propose Controlled Latent-space Evasion, which explicitly projects internal representations beyond the boundary into the compliant region with a tunable confidence margin, optimizing how far and in what way activations are moved while maintaining model coherence. 5) Evaluate across 15 instruction-tuned, multimodal, and reasoning models and compare to refusal-ablation and jailbreak baselines.
AI.result: They formalize previous refusal-ablation methods as minimum-confidence latent evasion attacks against linear probes, clarifying why they work and where they fail. Their new Controlled Latent-space Evasion method yields a higher attack success rate across a diverse set of 15 models, surpassing existing refusal-ablation strategies and specialized jailbreak methods, while effectively steering representations deeper into the answer-giving region.
AI.conclusion: Viewing refusal suppression as a latent-space evasion problem provides a clear geometric and probing-based explanation for prior steering methods and reveals their intrinsic limitation of halting at the decision boundary. By intentionally moving representations past this boundary with controlled confidence, the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art success at defeating refusal over a broad model suite, indicating that such safety behaviors can be systematically and more robustly circumvented via latent-space manipulation.

title: ACC: Compiling Agent Trajectories for Long-Context Training
summary: Recent development of agents has renewed demand for long-context reasoning capacity of LLMs. However, training LLMs for this capacity requires costly long-document curation or heuristic context synthesis. We observe that agents produce massive trajectories when solving problems, invoking tools and receiving environment observations across many turns. The evidence needed to answer the original question is thus scattered throughout these turns, requiring integration of distant context segments. Nevertheless, standard agent SFT masks tool responses and only trains turn-level tool selection, creating a supervision blind spot where these scattered signals go unused. We propose Agent Context Compilation (ACC), which converts trajectories from search, software engineering, and database querying agents into long-context QA pairs that combine the original question with tool responses and environment observations gathered across multiple turns, training the model to answer directly without tool use. This makes the dependencies between the question and the evidence explicit, enabling direct supervision of long-context reasoning over distant segments without additional annotation. ACC is a simple but effective approach that can be combined with any existing long-context extension or training method, providing scalable supervised fine-tuning data. We validate ACC on long-range dependency modeling tasks through MRCR and GraphWalks, challenging benchmarks requiring cross-turn coreference resolution and graph traversal over extended contexts. Training Qwen3-30B-A3B with ACC achieves 68.3 on MRCR (+18.1) and 77.5 on GraphWalks (+7.6), results comparable to Qwen3-235B-A22B, while preserving general capabilities on GPQA, MMLU-Pro, AIME, and IFEval. Further mechanism analysis reveals that the ACC-trained model exhibits task-adaptive attention restructuring and expert specialization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Agent Context Compilation (ACC), a method that converts multi-turn agent trajectories into long-context QA training data, enabling LLMs to learn long-range reasoning without extra manual annotation, and achieves large gains on long-context benchmarks with a 30B model reaching performance similar to a 235B model.
AI.motivation: LLMs used as agents must reason over very long contexts, since evidence is scattered across many tool calls and environment observations.
