Current supervision typically relies on textual descriptions of images rather than direct region-level supervision, and the causal role of visual attention in producing correct answers is under-exploited. This results in a perception–reasoning disconnect and limited gains on multimodal benchmarks. The paper aims to address these limitations.
AI.method: The authors propose Faithful-MR1, a two-stage training framework that explicitly structures and reinforces faithful multimodal reasoning. In the Anchoring stage, perception is turned into a dedicated pre-reasoning subtask by introducing a special <Focus> token whose attention is directly supervised on image regions instead of textual descriptions, thereby aligning visual attention with ground-truth evidence. In the Reinforcing stage, they apply counterfactual image interventions to reveal where vision causally impacts answers, and then use RL-like rewards to reinforce trajectories that (1) yield correct answers and (2) concentrate visual attention on causally important regions. This combines explicit attention anchoring with reward-based reinforcement of faithful visual use.
AI.result: On Qwen2.5-VL-Instruct 3B and 7B backbones, Faithful-MR1 achieves superior performance on multimodal reasoning benchmarks compared to recent RLVR-style and other multimodal reasoning baselines, while also requiring substantially less training data. The experiments show improved alignment between attended image regions and task-relevant evidence, and better consistency between perception and downstream reasoning behavior.
AI.conclusion: Faithful-MR1 effectively addresses both faithful perception and faithful use of visual evidence in multimodal reasoning. By supervising attention on image regions via a <Focus> token and reinforcing causally grounded visual attention through counterfactual interventions, the framework reduces the perception–reasoning disconnect and yields stronger, more data-efficient multimodal reasoning performance. This suggests that explicitly modeling and rewarding faithful visual attention is key to advancing RLVR for multimodal LLMs.

title: Format-Constraint Coupling in Knowledge Graph Construction from Statistical Tables
comment: 8 pages main body, 18 pages appendices. Submitted to EMNLP 2026 via ACL Rolling Review (ARR). Corresponding author: Yuxiang Feng (yxfeng@scut.edu.cn). Code and data available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/sge_lightrag-BE19
summary: An extraction schema should not reduce knowledge graph fidelity. On statistical CSV, however, it can. We study country-by-year time-series matrices, a common layout on open-data portals. In this setting, serialization format and schema constraints interact super-additively. Their joint effect exceeds the sum of independent effects by up to +1.180 (2x2 factorial, 6 datasets). Bootstrap 95% CIs are strictly positive on 4/6 datasets, with strongest evidence on wide Type-II matrices. More critically, a schema applied to a mismatched format can trigger catastrophic mismatch. Fact coverage falls below the unconstrained baseline on 4/6 datasets through entity inflation or extraction refusal. We call this observed pattern format-constraint coupling. Probing and token ablation support a surface-form anchoring explanation centred on column-name references. Controlled variants across format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and LLM families show the same direction within the measured scope; one LLM family shows only partial activation. The observation also has a diagnostic consequence. Three standard retrieval modes largely mask construction quality (delta <= 1pp), whereas direct graph access exposes gaps up to +47.6pp (p < 0.0001). To support fidelity-aware evaluation, we release CSVFidelity-Bench. It contains 15 datasets, 11 Type-II matrices, 4 Type-III tables, and 1,892 Gold Standard facts across 6 domains.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that how data is serialized in CSV and how extraction schemas are defined can interact to significantly distort knowledge graph extraction fidelity from country-by-year tables, and introduces a benchmark to evaluate this.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to extract structured knowledge graphs from semi-structured sources like CSVs, often assuming that adding an extraction schema will only improve or at least preserve factual coverage. In practice, many open-data portals publish country-by-year statistical data in matrix-like CSV layouts whose structure may not align with the schema. The authors are motivated to examine whether the combination of CSV layout and schema constraints can systematically reduce extraction fidelity, and to provide tools to measure and diagnose such failures.
AI.method: They analyze LLM-based extraction on country-by-year time-series matrices arranged as statistical CSVs, using a 2x2 factorial design across six datasets to separately and jointly test effects of serialization format and schema constraints. They measure fact coverage against an unconstrained baseline, apply bootstrap confidence intervals, and characterize cases where schema and format mismatch cause catastrophic failures (entity inflation or extraction refusal). They conduct probing and token-ablation experiments to test a surface-form anchoring hypothesis focused on column-name references, and run controlled comparisons over multiple format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosting setups, and different LLM families. They also evaluate standard retrieval modes versus direct graph access to see how much construction quality issues are masked. Finally, they build and release CSVFidelity-Bench, a benchmark of 15 datasets with annotated Gold Standard facts across several matrix/table types and domains.
AI.result: They find super-additive interactions between serialization format and schema constraints: their joint negative effect on knowledge graph extraction fidelity can exceed the sum of their individual effects by up to +1.180 in their metric across six datasets, with bootstrap 95% confidence intervals strictly positive for four of six datasets, especially on wide Type-II matrices. When schemas are applied to mismatched formats, extraction performance can become catastrophically worse than an unconstrained baseline, with fact coverage dropping below baseline on four of six datasets due to entity inflation or extraction refusal. The pattern, termed format-constraint coupling, appears consistently across tested format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and most LLM families (one family only partially shows the effect). Retrieval-style access largely hides these problems, with differences within 1 percentage point, whereas direct graph access reveals large fidelity gaps up to +47.6 percentage points, all highly statistically significant. The authors also provide empirical support, via probing and token ablations, for a surface-form anchoring explanation tied to column-name references.
AI.conclusion: Extraction schemas are not guaranteed to preserve or improve knowledge graph fidelity when applied to statistical CSV matrices; instead, schemas can interact with specific serialization formats to produce super-additive and sometimes catastrophic decreases in fact coverage, a phenomenon the authors call format-constraint coupling. This coupling is plausibly driven by surface-form anchoring on column labels, and it generalizes across datasets, formats, retrieval settings, and most LLM families tested. Because standard retrieval interfaces can mask substantial construction errors, fidelity should be evaluated via direct graph access using targeted benchmarks. CSVFidelity-Bench is introduced as such a resource, enabling more fidelity-aware evaluation of LLM-based knowledge graph construction from semi-structured CSVs.

title: Echo: Learning from Experience Data via User-Driven Refinement
summary:
Static "human data" faces inherent limitations: it is expensive to scale and bounded by the knowledge of its creators. Continuous learning from "experience data" - interactions between agents and their environments - promises to transcend these barriers. Today, the widespread deployment of AI agents grants us low-cost access to massive streams of such real-world experience. However, raw interaction logs are inherently noisy, filled with trial-and-error and low information density, rendering them inefficient for direct model training.
  We introduce Echo, a generalized framework designed to operationalize the transition from raw experience to learnable knowledge, effectively "echoing" environmental feedback back into the training loop for model optimization. In today's agent ecosystem, user refinement serves as a primary source of such feedback: driven by responsibility for the outcome, users rigorously transform flawed agent proposals into verified solutions. These user-driven refinement sequences inherently distill agents' crude attempts into high-quality training signals. Echo systematically harvests these signals to continuously align the agent with real-world needs. Large-scale validation in a production code completion environment confirms that Echo effectively harnesses this pipeline, breaking the static performance ceiling by increasing the acceptance rate from 25.7% to 35.7%.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Echo, a framework that converts noisy real-world agent interaction logs into high-quality training signals, enabling continuous learning that surpasses static human-labeled data.
AI.motivation: Static human-labeled datasets are costly to scale and limited by the knowledge and coverage of their curators. Meanwhile, AI agents deployed in the real world generate huge amounts of interaction data, but these logs are noisy and inefficient to use directly for training. There is a need for a systematic way to transform these raw interactions into reliable, learnable feedback that can continually improve agent performance and alignment with real user needs.
AI.method: The authors propose Echo, a generalized framework that processes real-world agent-environment interaction logs, with a focus on user-driven refinement of agent outputs. In current agent ecosystems, users often correct or refine imperfect agent responses into verified solutions. Echo identifies, extracts, and structures these refinement sequences as high-quality training signals. It then feeds them back into the training loop, effectively closing the loop between deployment feedback and model optimization in a scalable and automated way.
AI.result: Deployed in a production code-completion environment, Echo improved the model’s practical performance, as measured by user acceptance rate of suggestions. The acceptance rate increased from 25.7% to 35.7%, demonstrating that leveraging user refinement data via Echo breaks through the performance plateau achievable with static human-labeled datasets alone.
AI.conclusion: Echo provides an effective bridge from noisy real-world interaction logs to learnable supervision, enabling continuous post-deployment optimization of AI agents. By systematically harvesting and utilizing user refinement as high-quality feedback, Echo surpasses the limitations of static datasets and yields substantial gains in real-world acceptance metrics, indicating that experience-driven learning can sustainably improve deployed AI systems.

title: ArabDiscrim: A Decade-Long Arabic Facebook Corpus on Racism and Discrimination
summary: We present ArabDiscrim, a decade-long lexical resource and corpus of 293K public Arabic Facebook posts (2014--2024) discussing racism and discrimination. Unlike existing Twitter-centric datasets, ArabDiscrim integrates platform-native engagement signals, including reactions, shares, comments, and page metadata, enabling joint analysis of language and audience response. The resource includes 200 curated terms (100 racism-related and 100 discrimination-related) with morphological regex families (13+ inflections per lemma), and 20 discrimination axes capturing identity-based grounds for unequal treatment. It also provides explicit attribution patterns. Released under a restricted research-use license for ethical compliance with platform terms, ArabDiscrim supports weak supervision, axis-aware sampling, and platform ecology research. By bridging lexical depth and ecological validity, it establishes a foundation for fairness-oriented, platform-aware Arabic NLP.

AI.tldr: ArabDiscrim is a decade-long Arabic Facebook corpus and lexical resource about racism and discrimination, enriched with engagement signals and designed for fairness-oriented, platform-aware NLP research.
AI.motivation: Existing resources on racism and discrimination in Arabic are typically Twitter-focused, lack long-term temporal coverage, and do not integrate platform-native engagement signals. There is a need for an ecologically valid dataset that connects rich lexical modeling of racism/discrimination with how users interact with such content on a major platform (Facebook), to better support fairness, bias, and platform ecology research in Arabic NLP.
AI.method: The authors collected 293K public Arabic Facebook posts from 2014–2024 that discuss racism and discrimination. They constructed a lexical resource of 200 curated seed terms (100 racism-related, 100 discrimination-related), expanded into morphological regex families (13+ inflections per lemma) to cover Arabic variation. They also defined 20 discrimination axes to categorize content by identity-based grounds and encoded explicit attribution patterns. The corpus is released under a restricted research-use license to comply with platform terms and ethical constraints. The resource is designed to support weak supervision, axis-aware sampling, and studies of engagement using integrated platform-native signals (reactions, shares, comments, and page metadata).
AI.result: The outcome is ArabDiscrim, a combined lexical and corpus resource: (1) a decade-long, 293K-post Arabic Facebook dataset around racism and discrimination; (2) a lexicon of 200 terms with extensive morphological expansions; (3) a taxonomy of 20 discrimination axes; (4) patterns for attributing discriminatory content; and (5) linked engagement and metadata signals. Together, these enable joint study of language, discrimination types, and audience response in an Arabic social media context.
AI.conclusion: ArabDiscrim provides a foundational, ecologically valid resource for studying racism and discrimination in Arabic Facebook discourse, supporting fairness-oriented and platform-aware NLP approaches. By combining deep lexical coverage, axis annotations, and engagement signals over a decade, it facilitates weak supervision, targeted sampling, and research on how discriminatory content functions and spreads on social platforms, while remaining aligned with ethical and platform compliance constraints.

title: A Comparative Study of Language Models for Khmer Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising paradigm for grounding large language model (LLM) outputs in retrieved evidence, thereby reducing hallucination and improving factual accuracy. Its efficacy, however, remains largely unexamined for low-resource, non-Latin-script languages such as Khmer. In this paper, we present a RAG-based question answering system for Khmer-language telecom-domain documents. We conduct a two-phase comparative evaluation. First, we benchmark three embedding models: BGE-M3 (567M), Jina-Embeddings-v3 (570M), and Qwen3-Embedding (597M), for dense retrieval over Khmer documents. BGE-M3 consistently performs best, achieving a Hit Rate@3 of 0.285, File Hit Rate@3 of 0.700, MRR@3 of 0.221, and Precision@3 of 0.112, substantially outperforming the other retrievers. Second, using BGE-M3 as the selected retriever, we evaluate five generator backends: Qwen3 (8B), Qwen3.5 (9B), Sailor2-8B-Chat, SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat, and Llama-SEA-LION-v2-8B-IT, on a curated golden dataset of 200 Khmer question-answer pairs. To quantify system performance, we apply six RAGAS-inspired metrics: faithfulness, answer relevance, context relevance, factual correctness, answer similarity, and answer correctness. The results show no single model dominates across all metrics: Qwen3.5-9B achieves the highest faithfulness (0.859) and context relevance (0.726), Qwen3-8B attains the highest factual correctness (0.380), and SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat performs best on answer relevance (0.867), answer similarity (0.836), and answer correctness (0.599). These findings highlight that retriever choice remains a major bottleneck for Khmer RAG, while generator strengths vary depending on whether the priority is grounding, factual precision, or semantic similarity.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well Retrieval-Augmented Generation works for Khmer-language telecom documents, comparing different retrievers and generators and finding that retriever quality is the main bottleneck and that generators excel on different metrics.
AI.motivation: RAG is effective for reducing hallucinations in large language models, but its behavior in low-resource, non-Latin-script languages like Khmer is not well understood. There is a need for domain-specific, Khmer-language QA systems (here, telecom) and for empirical evidence on which retrievers and generators work best in this setting.
AI.method: The authors build a RAG-based QA system for Khmer telecom documents and run a two-phase evaluation. (1) They benchmark three dense retrieval embedding models—BGE-M3, Jina-Embeddings-v3, and Qwen3-Embedding—on Khmer documents using ranking metrics such as Hit Rate@3, File Hit Rate@3, MRR@3, and Precision@3. (2) They select the best retriever (BGE-M3) and then compare five LLM generators—Qwen3, Qwen3.5, Sailor2-8B-Chat, SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat, Llama-SEA-LION-v2-8B-IT—on a 200-item Khmer QA gold set, evaluating them with six RAGAS-inspired metrics (faithfulness, answer relevance, context relevance, factual correctness, answer similarity, answer correctness).
AI.result: For retrieval, BGE-M3 clearly outperforms Jina-Embeddings-v3 and Qwen3-Embedding, achieving Hit Rate@3 of 0.285, File Hit Rate@3 of 0.700, MRR@3 of 0.221, and Precision@3 of 0.112. For generation, no single LLM is best on all dimensions: Qwen3.5-9B has the highest faithfulness (0.859) and context relevance (0.726), Qwen3-8B leads in factual correctness (0.380), and SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat scores best on answer relevance (0.867), answer similarity (0.836), and answer correctness (0.599).
AI.conclusion: Retriever performance is a primary bottleneck for Khmer RAG systems, even more than the choice of generator. Different generator models exhibit complementary strengths: some are better grounded and context-aligned, others more factually precise or semantically similar to the gold answers. Effective Khmer RAG systems will likely require further improvements in retrieval quality and careful selection or combination of generators based on the target quality criteria.

title: The Log is the Agent: Event-Sourced Reactive Graphs for Auditable, Forkable Agentic Systems
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure. Open-source Apache-2.0 implementation with reproducible quickstart demo, deterministic replay, fork-and-diff, and lineage tracing
summary: Most agent frameworks are built around the language model: a conversation loop comes first, then tools, then rules, and finally a logging layer bolted on for observability, with state persisted as retrievable "memory." We describe ActiveGraph, a runtime that inverts this arrangement. The append-only event log is the source of truth; the working graph is a deterministic projection of that log; and behaviors--ordinary functions, classes, LLM-backed routines, or logic attached to typed edges--react to changes in the graph and emit new events. No component instructs another; coordination happens entirely through the shared graph. This single design decision yields three properties that retrieval-and-summarization memory systems do not provide: deterministic replay of any run from its log, cheap forking that branches a run at any event without re-executing the shared prefix, and end-to-end lineage from a high-level goal down to the individual model call that produced each artifact. We present the architecture, a determinism contract that makes replay sound, and a worked diligence example whose full causal structure is reconstructable from the log alone. We discuss--without claiming to demonstrate--why this substrate is unusually well suited to self-improving agents, and how it extends the BabyAGI lineage and prior graph-memory research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ActiveGraph, an agent runtime centered on an append-only event log and deterministic graph projection, enabling deterministic replay, cheap forking, and full lineage tracking for agent runs.
AI.motivation: Existing agent frameworks are LLM-centric: they start from a conversation loop and layer on tools, rules, memory, and logging as secondary concerns. This makes runs hard to deterministically replay, to fork at arbitrary points, and to trace end-to-end lineage from goals to specific model calls. Retrieval/summarization-based memories offer persistence but not rigorous causal structure or reproducibility. The authors want a substrate that treats state, coordination, and observability as first-class, in a way that better supports debugging, provenance, experimentation, and potentially self-improving agents.
AI.method: They propose ActiveGraph, a runtime whose core abstraction is an append-only event log. The current "working" state of the agent is represented as a graph that is a deterministic projection of that log. Behaviors (which can be normal code, LLM-backed routines, or logic on typed edges) subscribe to graph changes and emit new events back into the log. No component calls another directly; instead, all coordination is mediated through the shared graph and events. They define a determinism contract that constrains behaviors so that replaying the same log always reconstructs the same graph and causal structure. They also present a worked diligence-style example that illustrates how complex agent behavior arises and can be reconstructed solely from the log.
AI.result: Conceptually, they obtain three key properties: (1) deterministic replay of any agent run by reprocessing the log; (2) cheap forking, by branching execution at any event without recomputing the shared prefix; and (3) complete lineage, allowing users to trace from high-level goals down to each individual model call and artifact it produced. The diligence example demonstrates that the full causal structure of an agent’s behavior can be recovered from the log alone. They also relate ActiveGraph’s design to BabyAGI-style graph agents and prior graph-memory systems, arguing it generalizes and structures those ideas.
AI.conclusion: Re-centering the agent runtime on an append-only event log and deterministic graph projection, rather than on an LLM-centric conversation loop, yields strong guarantees about replayability, forking, and provenance that retrieval-based memories lack. ActiveGraph provides a coordination substrate where components are decoupled and interact only through graph events, making complex behaviors more transparent and auditable. The authors suggest that this structure is particularly promising for building self-improving agents and advancing graph-based agent research, though they stop short of claiming a full demonstration of such capabilities.

title: Cross-Lingual Consensus: Aligning Multilingual Cultural Knowledge via Multilingual Self-Consistency
comment: Accepted to The 1st Workshop on Multilinguality in the Era of Large Language Models
summary: Although Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong capabilities across various tasks, they exhibit significant performance discrepancies across languages. While prompting LLMs in English typically yields the highest general performance, it often induces a Western-centric bias, hindering the model's ability to accurately reflect diverse cultural knowledge. We hypothesize that LLMs already possess rich cultural knowledge embedded within local-language representations, but fail to retrieve it when prompted in English. To bridge this cross-lingual knowledge gap, we propose a novel self-supervised framework. Our method leverages multilingual self-consistency to identify the most reliable cultural responses across languages, combined with a self-critique mechanism to transfer this knowledge to the weaker language. Evaluations on the BLEnD benchmark demonstrate that our approach significantly improves cultural alignment-boosting performance on English queries by an average of 5.03%-relying entirely on self-generated data. Ultimately, our work demonstrates that latent cultural knowledge can be successfully surfaced and propagated across languages, enabling more culturally equitable and consistent LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a self-supervised framework that leverages multilingual self-consistency and self-critique to surface and transfer latent cultural knowledge between languages in LLMs, improving cultural alignment for English prompts using only self-generated data.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform unevenly across languages: English prompts give the best overall performance but introduce Western-centric bias, failing to reflect diverse cultural knowledge. The authors are motivated by the need to make LLMs more culturally equitable and to better utilize cultural knowledge that may already be encoded in non-English (local-language) representations but is not effectively accessed when using English prompts.
AI.method: They hypothesize that rich culture-specific knowledge is embedded in local-language representations and design a self-supervised framework to access it. The method: (1) query the LLM in multiple languages for culturally relevant questions; (2) use multilingual self-consistency to compare answers across languages and identify the most reliable cultural responses; (3) employ a self-critique mechanism where the model reflects on these responses and distills or transfers the high-quality cultural knowledge into the weaker language space (e.g., English) without human labels, relying purely on self-generated data.
AI.result: On the BLEnD benchmark for cultural alignment, their method significantly improves performance on English queries, achieving an average gain of 5.03%. All improvements are obtained in a self-supervised way, using only model-generated signals rather than external annotations.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that latent cultural knowledge is already present in LLMs but is underutilized when interacting via English. By exploiting multilingual self-consistency and self-critique, this knowledge can be surfaced and propagated across languages, leading to more culturally aligned, equitable, and consistent LLM behavior across linguistic contexts.

title: Active Evidence-Seeking and Diagnostic Reasoning in Large Language Models for Clinical Decision Support
summary: Large language models perform well on static medical examinations, yet clinical diagnosis often requires iterative evidence gathering under uncertainty. Building on prior interactive evaluation efforts, we introduce an OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator and a controlled, reproducible benchmark for active diagnostic inquiry. Across 468 cases and 15 models in our protocol, we observe that multi-turn evidence seeking reduces diagnostic accuracy by 12.75% and lowers supporting-evidence quality by 24.36% relative to full-context evaluation; error analyses associate these drops with premature diagnostic closure and inefficient questioning. Together, these results suggest that static full-context benchmarks may overestimate performance in interactive evidence-seeking settings, motivating complementary interactive assessment for safer clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: They build an OSCE-style interactive benchmark with standardized patient simulators to test how large language models perform in multi-turn clinical diagnosis, finding that interactive evidence-gathering actually makes models markedly worse than static, full-information tests.
AI.motivation: Existing medical benchmarks for LLMs are mostly static multiple-choice or single-shot questions, which don’t capture the iterative, uncertain, and interactive nature of real clinical diagnosis. There is a need for controlled, reproducible ways to evaluate how models ask questions, gather evidence, and revise diagnoses over multiple turns, because this better reflects real-world clinical decision-making and safety concerns.
AI.method: The authors design an OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator that allows large language models to conduct multi-turn diagnostic conversations. They construct a controlled benchmark of 468 clinical cases and evaluate 15 different models under two conditions: (1) full-context, where the entire case information is provided up front, and (2) active diagnostic inquiry, where the model must iteratively ask questions to collect information. They then compare diagnostic accuracy and the quality of supporting evidence between these conditions and perform error analyses to identify patterns such as premature closure and inefficient questioning.
AI.result: Across 468 cases and 15 models, diagnostic accuracy in the interactive, multi-turn condition is 12.75% lower than in the full-context setting, and the quality of supporting evidence is reduced by 24.36%. The authors’ qualitative error analysis links these performance drops to behaviors like making a diagnosis too early (premature diagnostic closure) and asking suboptimal or redundant questions (inefficient questioning).
AI.conclusion: Static, full-context medical benchmarks likely overestimate large language model performance in realistic, interactive diagnostic scenarios. Multi-turn evidence gathering exposes important weaknesses—particularly premature closure and poor question strategies—indicating that complementary interactive evaluation frameworks, like their OSCE-style simulator, are necessary to more accurately gauge clinical usefulness and to guide safer deployment in decision support settings.

title: Psy-Chronicle:A Structured Pipeline for Synthesizing Long-Horizon Campus Psychological Counseling Dialogues
summary: In recent years, large language models have shown substantial potential in psychological support tasks. However, existing psychological counseling data mostly rely on single-turn question answering or short multi-turn dialogues, making it difficult to characterize how college students' psychological distress accumulates, interacts, and gradually evolves over long periods within campus life events. To address this issue, this paper proposes Psy-Chronicle, a structured data-generation framework for synthesizing long-horizon campus psychological counseling dialogues. We generate a semester-spanning temporal stress event graph to model the chronological order and evolutionary dependencies among campus stress events. Through interactive simulation between a student agent and a counselor agent, together with a structured memory integration mechanism, Psy-Chronicle generates long-horizon dialogues with continuity across counseling sessions. Based on Psy-Chronicle, we construct and open-source CPCD, a Chinese long-horizon dialogue dataset for college psychological counseling, containing 100 student profiles, 90,000 counseling dialogues. We further build CPCD-Bench to evaluate models' long-horizon campus counseling capabilities from three dimensions: session-level response, long-horizon memory recall, and temporal-causal reasoning. Experimental results show that CPCD effectively improves session-level response generation and long-horizon memory recall for models with the same base architecture. Meanwhile, improvements in temporal-causal reasoning remain limited, indicating that event-chain organization and causal explanation are key challenges in long-horizon psychological counseling modeling. The related code and data are available at: https://github.com/EdwinUSTB/Psy-Chronicle

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Psy-Chronicle, a framework to generate realistic long-term psychological counseling dialogues for college students, and releases a large Chinese dataset (CPCD) and benchmark (CPCD-Bench) to evaluate long-horizon counseling capabilities of LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing psychological counseling datasets mainly consist of single-turn or short multi-turn dialogues, which cannot capture how college students’ psychological stress accumulates, interacts, and evolves over a semester of campus life. This lack of long-horizon, temporally structured data limits the development and evaluation of language models intended for realistic psychological support scenarios that require continuity, memory, and reasoning over extended periods.
AI.method: The authors propose Psy-Chronicle, a data-generation framework that synthesizes long-horizon campus counseling dialogues. First, they construct a semester-long temporal stress event graph that encodes the chronological order and evolutionary dependencies among stress-related campus events. Then they run interactive simulations between a student agent and a counselor agent, where a structured memory integration mechanism maintains continuity across multiple counseling sessions. Using this framework, they generate CPCD, a Chinese long-horizon psychological counseling dataset with 100 student profiles and 90,000 dialogues, and design CPCD-Bench, a benchmark evaluating models on session-level response quality, long-horizon memory recall, and temporal-causal reasoning.
AI.result: Using CPCD to train or adapt models with the same base architecture leads to measurable gains in session-level response generation quality and in the ability to recall long-horizon information from counseling histories. However, performance gains in temporal-causal reasoning—understanding and explaining how earlier events lead to later psychological states—are small, suggesting current models still struggle with complex event chains and causal explanations over long timescales.
AI.conclusion: Psy-Chronicle provides a structured way to synthesize realistic, semester-long psychological counseling dialogues and yields CPCD and CPCD-Bench as valuable resources for the community. While the dataset clearly improves models’ response quality and long-term memory handling, temporal-causal reasoning over extended event chains remains a key open challenge for long-horizon psychological counseling modeling, pointing to the need for new architectures or training strategies that better capture event organization and causal structure.

title: Enhancing Visual Token Representations for Video Large Language Models via Training-Free Spatial-Temporal Pooling and Gridding
summary: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly advanced video understanding tasks, yet challenges remain in efficiently compressing visual tokens while preserving spatiotemporal interactions. Existing methods, such as LLaVA family, utilize simplistic pooling or interpolation techniques that overlook the intricate dynamics of visual tokens. To bridge this gap, we propose ST-GridPool, a novel training-free visual token enhancement method designed specifically for Video LLMs. Our approach integrates Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG), which captures multi-grained spatiotemporal interactions through hierarchical temporal gridding, and Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP), which preserves high-information visual regions by leveraging the correlation between token norms and semantic richness. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate that ST-GridPool consistently enhances performance of Video LLMs without requiring costly retraining. Our method offers an efficient and plug-and-play solution for improving visual token representations. Our code is available in https://github.com/bingjunluo/ST-GridPool.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ST-GridPool, a training-free, plug-and-play method to improve video understanding in Multimodal Large Language Models by better compressing visual tokens while preserving spatiotemporal information.
AI.motivation: Although recent MLLMs have advanced video understanding, their visual token compression is often naive, using simple pooling or interpolation that fails to capture complex spatiotemporal interactions. This leads to inefficiencies and loss of important visual information, especially in video contexts where temporal dynamics are crucial. The authors aim to design a more informed, yet efficient, token processing scheme that can be used without retraining existing Video LLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose ST-GridPool, a visual token enhancement module for Video LLMs. It combines two key components: (1) Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG), which structures video tokens into hierarchical temporal grids to capture multi-scale spatiotemporal interactions; and (2) Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP), which selectively preserves visually and semantically rich regions using token norms as a proxy for information content. The method operates as a training-free, plug-and-play preprocessing step on visual tokens before they are fed into the LLM.
AI.result: Across multiple video understanding benchmarks, incorporating ST-GridPool into existing Video LLMs consistently improves their performance. These gains are achieved without additional training or fine-tuning, showing that the module effectively enhances visual token representations while remaining computationally efficient.
AI.conclusion: ST-GridPool provides an effective, training-free solution to enhance visual token representations in Video LLMs by better modeling spatiotemporal interactions and preserving informative regions. Its plug-and-play nature and consistent performance improvements suggest it is a practical way to upgrade current video understanding systems without incurring retraining costs.

title: Do Factual Recall Mechanisms Carry over from Text to Speech in Multimodal Language Models?
comment: In *SEM 2026, the 15th Joint Conference on Lexical and Computational Semantics
summary:
In recent years, several Speech Language Models (SLMs) that represent speech and written text jointly have been presented. The question then emerges about how model-internal mechanisms are similar and different when operating in the two modalities. We focus on how these systems encode, store, and retrieve factual knowledge, which has previously been investigated for text-only models. To investigate mechanisms behind the storage and recall of factual association in SLMs, we leverage Causal Mediation Analysis, a technique previously applied to text-based models.
  Initial results using SpiritLM, a multimodal model integrating discrete speech tokens reveal discrepancies between text-to-text and speech-to-text results, suggesting that the emergent mechanisms for factual recall are only partially carried over from the text to the speech modality. These results advance our understanding of how internal mechanisms encode factual associations in SLMs while contributing insights for improving speech-enabled AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how speech-language models internally store and retrieve factual knowledge in speech versus text, finding that mechanisms for factual recall differ between modalities.
AI.motivation: Speech Language Models now jointly handle speech and text, but we don’t yet understand whether they use the same internal mechanisms to encode and recall factual knowledge in both modalities. Prior work has focused on text-only language models, leaving a gap in understanding how factual associations are represented when input or output is speech.
AI.method: The authors apply Causal Mediation Analysis—previously used to probe factual recall circuitry in text-only models—to a multimodal speech-text model called SpiritLM, which uses discrete speech tokens. They compare internal causal pathways and mediation effects for factual associations in text-to-text versus speech-to-text settings to see which mechanisms are shared or modality-specific.
AI.result: Experiments on SpiritLM show notable discrepancies between how the model recalls factual information when processing text-to-text versus speech-to-text. The causal mediators and pathways supporting factual recall in the text modality are only partially active or effective in the speech modality, indicating modality-specific internal mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The mechanisms that encode and retrieve factual associations in Speech Language Models do not fully transfer from text to speech. This partial overlap suggests that speech-enabled AI may require modality-aware architectures or training strategies to achieve robust factual recall from spoken input, and the findings deepen understanding of internal representations in multimodal SLMs.

title: A Camera-Cooperative ISAC Framework for Multimodal Non-Cooperative UAVs Sensing
summary: The detection of non-cooperative unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) presents significant challenges for Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems due to the inherent limitations of single-modal perception and the competition for shared communication and sensing resources. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Camera-Cooperative ISAC (CC-ISAC) framework that employs multimodal sensing to enable efficient UAV beam steering and tracking. The proposed framework employs cameras for coarse-grained airspace monitoring and utilizes ISAC for fine-grained, high-precision sensing, forming a complementary perception loop that enhances both sensing accuracy and resource efficiency. Within this framework, two key modules are developed: (1) a Vision-to-Echo Data Alignment (V2EDA) model that aligns visual and echo-domain features through cross-attention mechanisms, and (2) a Multimodal Fusion-Based Estimation (MMFE) model that integrates historical multimodal data with current observations for robust state estimation. Extensive evaluations conducted on the DeepSense 6G dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves an average reduction of 71% in beam steering overhead and 1.69-11.15% in tracking overhead while maintaining high angular estimation accuracy. The CC-ISAC framework effectively mitigates resource contention between sensing and communication, enabling reliable UAV surveillance while freeing substantial system resources for additional communication tasks, thereby representing a practical advancement in ISAC system design.

AI.tldr: Proposes a camera-cooperative integrated sensing and communication (CC-ISAC) framework that uses multimodal (vision + ISAC) sensing plus two neural modules (V2EDA and MMFE) to reduce beam steering and tracking overhead for non-cooperative UAV detection while keeping high angular accuracy.
AI.motivation: Non-cooperative UAVs are hard to detect and track with current ISAC systems because (i) single-modal sensing (only radar/communication-side echo) limits perception performance and (ii) sensing and communication share resources, causing contention and high overhead (e.g., frequent beam sweeping). A more efficient, accurate, and resource-aware solution is needed for reliable UAV surveillance in ISAC networks.
AI.method: 1) Propose a Camera-Cooperative ISAC (CC-ISAC) framework where cameras perform coarse airspace monitoring and ISAC handles fine, high-precision sensing, organized as a complementary perception loop. 2) Design a Vision-to-Echo Data Alignment (V2EDA) model that uses cross-attention to align and correlate visual features with echo-domain (RF) features. 3) Design a Multimodal Fusion-Based Estimation (MMFE) model that fuses historical multimodal observations and current measurements to estimate UAV states robustly. 4) Implement and evaluate the framework on the DeepSense 6G dataset, comparing steering and tracking overheads and angular estimation accuracy against baselines.
AI.result: On the DeepSense 6G dataset, the CC-ISAC framework achieves about 71% reduction in beam steering overhead and 1.69–11.15% reduction in tracking overhead compared with baselines, while maintaining high angular estimation accuracy for UAV tracking.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging cooperative cameras with ISAC and learning-based multimodal alignment/fusion significantly reduces sensing-related overhead in ISAC systems while preserving accurate UAV state estimation. This mitigates resource contention between sensing and communication, freeing substantial resources for additional communication tasks. The framework is a practical and effective architectural direction for future ISAC-based UAV surveillance systems.

title: Structure Retention in Embedding Spaces as a Predictor of Benchmark Performance
summary: In this paper, we show that high-performing embedding models organize their embedding spaces in a consistent way. We evaluate 25 contemporary embedding models on five MTEB tasks spanning four diverse task categories (retrieval, bitext mining, pair classification, and summarization) in both English and multilingual settings, and reveal that nearest-neighbor overlap and magnitude differences in independent component analysis (ICA) between paired text instances strongly correlate (even up to 0.97) with performance on the given task. Ultimately, we show that embedding tasks display varying degrees of linearity and reliance on retention of local information. Our results further the understanding of embeddings, their relation to model performance, and shed light on possible future training objectives and optimizing conditional embeddings.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how high-performing text embedding models structure their embedding spaces and links these structural properties to downstream task performance.
AI.motivation: Many embedding models exist, but we lack a clear understanding of how their internal geometry relates to performance across different tasks and languages. A better grasp of this structure could guide model selection, design, and training objectives for improved embeddings.
AI.method: The authors evaluate 25 modern embedding models on five MTEB benchmark tasks from four categories (retrieval, bitext mining, pair classification, summarization) in both English and multilingual settings. They analyze the embedding spaces by measuring nearest-neighbor overlap and comparing magnitude differences of components after applying independent component analysis (ICA) to paired text instances. They then correlate these geometric/structural measures with task performance.
AI.result: They find that: (1) High-performing embedding models organize their spaces in a consistent manner across tasks. (2) Nearest-neighbor overlap and ICA component magnitude differences between paired texts correlate very strongly with task performance, with correlation coefficients up to 0.97. (3) Different embedding tasks show different degrees of linearity and dependence on preserving local neighborhood information.
AI.conclusion: The geometry of embedding spaces, especially neighbor structure and ICA-based component behavior, is highly predictive of downstream performance. Tasks differ in how linear they are and how much they rely on local information retention, suggesting that understanding and controlling embedding-space structure can inform future training objectives and the optimization of conditional embeddings.

title: Knowledge Graph Re-engineering Along the Ontological Continuum (extended version)
summary: Knowledge graphs have become the primary vehicle for data integration and are critical to the success of modern AI, but the diversity of KG modelling practices, from lightweight vocabularies to richly axiomatised ontologies, makes integration and reuse expensive and brittle. This challenge is particularly acute in neuro-symbolic AI, where bridging neural and symbolic components depends on the ability to reengineer KGs to fit new requirements; GenAI now offers unprecedented automation capability, but without a principled understanding of the KG space, such automation remains conceptually ungrounded. We introduce the ontological continuum as that missing conceptualisation, a theoretical construct a theoretical construct whose characterisation framework is defined by two orthogonal distinctions: semantics vs pragmatics, and properties vs affordances; together these define a vocabulary to describe, compare, navigate, and transform KGs across the full range of modelling practices. The methodological stance is empirical: rather than prescribing how KGs should be modelled, the continuum aims to define a theory of the existent, derived from observation of real-world KG engineering practices and whose structure can be made formally explicit, for example, through Formal Concept Analysis (FCA). We ground the vision through a case study on provenance knowledge, showing how a single concern manifests differently across the continuum. We articulate five open research challenges and invite the community to develop the ontological continuum as a shared research agenda.

AI.tldr: Introduces the 'ontological continuum' as a conceptual framework to describe, compare, and transform diverse knowledge graph modelling practices, grounded in empirical observation and illustrated with a provenance case study, and proposes it as a shared research agenda.
AI.motivation: Knowledge graphs are central to data integration and AI, but their highly diverse modelling practices—from simple vocabularies to richly axiomatised ontologies—make integration, reuse, and reengineering difficult, especially for neuro-symbolic AI and GenAI-driven automation. A principled, unified conceptualisation of the space of KG modelling practices is missing, which limits systematic analysis, navigation, and automated transformation of KGs.
AI.method: The paper proposes the 'ontological continuum' as a theoretical construct defined along two orthogonal axes: (1) semantics vs. pragmatics and (2) properties vs. affordances. This yields a characterisation framework and vocabulary for describing and comparing KGs. The stance is empirical and observational: instead of prescribing best practices, the authors derive the continuum from real-world KG engineering patterns and suggest making its structure explicit via Formal Concept Analysis (FCA). A case study on provenance knowledge demonstrates how one concern appears across different points in the continuum.
AI.result: The work yields: (1) a conceptual framework—the ontological continuum—organised by semantics/pragmatics and properties/affordances; (2) an associated vocabulary and way to position different KG artefacts along this continuum; (3) a provenance-focused case study that shows practical instantiations of the continuum; and (4) the identification of five open research challenges related to formalising, operationalising, and exploiting this continuum for KG engineering and neuro-symbolic AI.
AI.conclusion: The ontological continuum provides a principled, empirically grounded way to conceptualise the diverse landscape of KG modelling practices, enabling more systematic comparison, navigation, and transformation of KGs. This can underpin more robust neuro-symbolic integration and GenAI-based KG automation. The authors conclude by positioning the continuum not as a finished theory but as an agenda-setting construct, calling on the community to refine, formalise, and apply it, including through tools such as Formal Concept Analysis and domain case studies like provenance.

title: Evaluation of Chunking Strategies for Effective Text Embedding in Low-Resource Language on Agricultural Documents
summary: In this study, we compare the performance of four text chunking approaches: Recursive, Khmer-Aware, Sentence-Based, and LLM-Based within a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework applied to Khmer agricultural documents. The document chunks are encoded using the BGE-M3 multilingual embedding model and retrieved using the FAISS library. Performance is evaluated using four metrics: Average Retrieval Score (L2 distance), Answer Relevance, Khmer Coverage, and Khmer Intersection over Union, all measured against ground-truth question-answer pairs. For evaluation, we perform 5-fold cross-validation over 18 question-answer pairs. We observe the best performance for the character-based Recursive chunking method with a chunk size of 300 characters, achieving the lowest L2 distance (0.4295 +- 0.0461), highest Answer Relevance (0.8663 +- 0.0199), and highest Khmer IoU (0.6441 +- 0.0347). A paired t-test shows a statistically significant improvement over the Sentence-Based chunking method in L2 distance (p = 0.0121). These results highlight the importance of segmentation granularity and structural preservation for optimizing dense retrieval in morphologically complex, low-resource languages such as Khmer.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates four text chunking strategies within a Khmer RAG system and finds that a 300-character recursive chunking approach yields the best retrieval and answer quality, underscoring the role of segmentation granularity in low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: To improve Retrieval-Augmented Generation performance for Khmer, a morphologically complex and low-resource language, by identifying which text chunking strategy yields the best dense retrieval and QA quality over agricultural documents.
AI.method: Implement four chunking methods (Recursive, Khmer-Aware, Sentence-Based, LLM-Based) on Khmer agricultural texts in a RAG pipeline; encode chunks using the BGE-M3 multilingual embedding model; index and retrieve with FAISS; evaluate using L2 distance, Answer Relevance, Khmer Coverage, and Khmer IoU against ground-truth QA pairs with 5-fold cross-validation; compare methods statistically via paired t-tests.
AI.result: Recursive character-based chunking with a 300-character chunk size achieves the best performance: lowest L2 distance (0.4295 ± 0.0461), highest Answer Relevance (0.8663 ± 0.0199), and highest Khmer IoU (0.6441 ± 0.0347), and significantly outperforms Sentence-Based chunking in L2 distance (p = 0.0121).
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, structurally aware recursive chunking significantly improves dense retrieval and answer quality for Khmer RAG, demonstrating that appropriate segmentation granularity and structural preservation are crucial for morphologically complex, low-resource languages.

title: MPDocBench-Parse: Benchmarking Practical Multi-page Document Parsing
summary: Document parsing converts visually rich documents into machine-readable structured representations, forming a crucial foundation for information systems. Although many benchmarks have been proposed for document parsing, they remain inadequate for realistic scenarios. Existing benchmarks either focus on specific tasks or assess only single-page, text-centric settings, making them insufficient for practical multi-page parsing. Moreover, they lack fine-grained evaluation of semantic continuity, hierarchical structure recovery, and visual content preservation. To address these gaps, we propose MPDocBench-Parse, a benchmark for multi-page document parsing in real-world applications. It contains 433 manually annotated documents with 3,246 pages, covering 15 document types in English and Chinese, with diverse layout styles, and supports document-level end-to-end evaluation. We further design a comprehensive protocol for content fidelity and logical structure, covering text, table, and formula recognition, truncated text and table merging, figure extraction, reading order, and heading hierarchy recovery. Experiments show that, while existing models perform well on basic text extraction, they still suffer clear limitations in semantic continuity integration, visual content parsing, and hierarchical structure recovery. MPDocBench-Parse provides a unified foundation for advancing document parsing toward more realistic scenarios.

AI.tldr: MPDocBench-Parse is a new benchmark for realistic multi-page document parsing, providing fine-grained, document-level evaluation across text, tables, formulas, figures, and hierarchical structure.
AI.motivation: Existing document parsing benchmarks do not reflect realistic, multi-page, visually rich documents. They typically focus on single-page, text-centric tasks, lack document-level evaluation, and do not assess key aspects such as semantic continuity across pages, hierarchical structure recovery, and preservation of visual content. This gap makes it difficult to measure and drive progress on end-to-end document understanding systems used in real-world applications.
AI.method: The authors construct MPDocBench-Parse, a benchmark composed of 433 manually annotated documents with 3,246 pages in total. The documents span 15 types in both English and Chinese and exhibit diverse layouts. They define an end-to-end, document-level evaluation protocol that jointly measures content fidelity and logical structure. The protocol includes metrics for text, table, and formula recognition; merging of truncated text and tables across page boundaries; figure extraction; reading order; and recovery of heading hierarchy.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations of existing document parsing models on MPDocBench-Parse show that, although current systems are relatively strong at basic text extraction, they perform poorly on more complex, realistic aspects. In particular, they show clear weaknesses in integrating semantic continuity across pages, parsing visual content such as figures, and reconstructing hierarchical document structure.
AI.conclusion: MPDocBench-Parse fills a critical gap in document parsing evaluation by offering a realistic, multi-page, and fine-grained benchmark. It exposes limitations of current models beyond simple text extraction and provides a unified, document-level foundation to guide future research toward robust, real-world document parsing systems.

title: Audience Engagement with Arabic Women's Social Empowerment and Wellbeing: A Decadal Corpus
summary: This paper presents the Arabic Women and Society Corpus, a ten year collection of 252,487 public Arabic Facebook posts related to women's empowerment and social wellbeing. The corpus was collected from 51,660 pages across 77 countries between 2013 and 2024, resulting in more than 267 million user interactions. Each post includes engagement metrics such as shares, comments, and emotional reactions, providing a unique view of audience sentiment and social attention. The data were processed using an automated pipeline with language identification, normalization, and metadata cleaning to ensure reliability and reproducibility. The corpus enables large scale analysis of gender discourse, social reform, and emotional engagement across Arabic dialects. It supports research in Arabic natural language processing, computational social science, and digital communication studies. The dataset and accompanying documentation will be released under request for research use.

AI.tldr: Introduction of a large-scale Arabic Facebook corpus focused on women’s empowerment and social wellbeing, supporting research on gender discourse and Arabic NLP.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of large, systematically collected social media corpora in Arabic focused specifically on women’s empowerment and social wellbeing, which are crucial for understanding gender discourse, social reform, and emotional engagement in Arabic-speaking societies.
AI.method: The authors collected 252,487 public Arabic Facebook posts from 51,660 pages across 77 countries over ten years (2013–2024). They used an automated pipeline including language identification, text normalization, and metadata cleaning to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the dataset. Each post is accompanied by engagement metrics such as shares, comments, and emotional reactions.
AI.result: The outcome is the Arabic Women and Society Corpus, a curated dataset containing the posts plus rich engagement metadata, totaling over 267 million user interactions. The corpus is cleaned, language-filtered, and documented for research use.
AI.conclusion: The released corpus enables large-scale, data-driven studies of women-related discourse, gender issues, and social reform across Arabic dialects and regions, and provides a valuable resource for Arabic NLP, computational social science, and digital communication research. Access is available upon request for research purposes.

title: ExComm: Exploration-Stage Communication for Error-Resilient Agentic Test-Time Scaling
summary: A common failure mode in long-horizon agentic test-time scaling is error propagation, where factual errors or invalid deductions introduced at intermediate steps persist in the agent's belief state and contaminate later reasoning. Existing test-time scaling methods provide limited control over this process, as they often rely on agents to detect their own mistakes, select among flawed trajectories, or refine solutions only after errors have already shaped the reasoning path. We propose ExComm, a communication protocol for exploration-stage agentic test-time scaling. ExComm is motivated by the empirical observation that the majority of intermediate errors in parallel agentic reasoning produce detectable cross-agent factual conflicts. Leveraging the iterative structure of agentic workflows, ExComm periodically audits agent belief states to detect such conflicts, resolves them through a dedicated tool-based verification loop, and returns concise, targeted feedback to the involved agents. Corrections are incorporated through soft belief updates, which append verified feedback rather than overwriting existing beliefs. Furthermore, to prevent collapsing trajectory diversity due to communication, ExComm further introduces a trajectory diversification module that redirects redundant trajectories toward orthogonal strategies. Experiments on AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and GAIA with Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite and Qwen3.5-4B show that ExComm consistently outperforms strong test-time scaling baselines, achieving average performance gains of 5.7% and 5.0% over the best-performing baselines, respectively. Further analyses demonstrate improved error recovery, favorable scaling behavior, stronger diversity than adapted communication baselines, and the best performance-cost trade-off among the evaluated methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ExComm, a communication-based framework for multi‑agent test-time scaling that detects and corrects cross-agent factual conflicts during reasoning, thereby reducing error propagation and improving long‑horizon performance.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon, multi-step reasoning agents often suffer from error propagation: once an intermediate mistake enters the belief state, it persists and corrupts subsequent reasoning. Existing test-time scaling methods mainly rely on agents to self-correct or select better trajectories after errors have already influenced the reasoning path, offering limited control over how intermediate beliefs evolve. There is a need for a mechanism that can monitor, audit, and correct beliefs during the exploration stage in a way that mitigates error propagation while preserving trajectory diversity and keeping computational cost reasonable.
AI.method: The authors propose ExComm, a communication protocol designed for the exploration stage of agentic test-time scaling. Multiple agents reason in parallel, and ExComm periodically audits their belief states to identify cross-agent factual conflicts—disagreements that signal possible errors. When conflicts are detected, a tool-based verification loop is invoked to fact-check the conflicting claims. Verified results are then sent back as concise feedback to the specific agents involved. Instead of overwriting their states, agents perform soft belief updates, appending the verified information. To avoid convergence to a single reasoning path and maintain exploration benefits, ExComm also includes a trajectory diversification module that detects redundant trajectories and redirects them toward orthogonal (different) reasoning strategies.
AI.result: On benchmarks AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and GAIA, using Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite and Qwen3.5-4B as base models, ExComm achieves consistent performance improvements over strong test-time scaling baselines. The average gain over the best-performing baseline is reported as 5.7% for Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite and 5.0% for Qwen3.5-4B. Additional analyses show that ExComm improves error recovery, yields better scaling behavior as resources increase, maintains greater trajectory diversity than communication-based baselines adapted from prior work, and offers the best performance-cost trade-off among the evaluated methods.
AI.conclusion: ExComm demonstrates that explicit, conflict-driven communication and verification during the exploration stage can substantially mitigate error propagation in long-horizon agentic reasoning. By auditing belief states, resolving factual conflicts via tools, and applying soft belief updates while preserving trajectory diversity, ExComm outperforms existing test-time scaling baselines across multiple benchmarks and models. The approach suggests that structured multi-agent communication with verification is a promising direction for more reliable and cost-effective test-time scaling of reasoning agents.

title: GHI: Graphormer over Conditioned Hypergraph Incidence for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
summary: Aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) requires models to bind sentiment evidence to the correct aspect, making it a natural testbed for fine-grained structural reasoning. We introduce GHI, a Graphormer-over-Conditioned-Hypergraph-Incidence framework that is designed as an incidence-based structural reasoning layer built on a bipartite topology. GHI represents diverse linguistic and semantic evidence as token--hyperedge incidence relations, allowing different structural signals to be incorporated through a unified interface. Extensive experiments on six standard ABSA benchmarks show that GHI outperforms all baselines on the SemEval domains, and multi-seed evaluations show stable improvements over strong DeBERTa. Further experiments show that with only 247M parameters, GHI approaches the performance of 11B Flan-T5 based methods on the ISE benchmark. Moreover, it demonstrates strong robustness on the challenging ARTS datasets, maintaining highly competitive performance where traditional models degrade. These results demonstrate that compact structural reasoning remains a valuable alternative to scale-driven approaches for fine-grained tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes GHI, a structural reasoning layer based on a hypergraph-incidence Graphormer, for aspect-based sentiment analysis, achieving strong performance with relatively compact model size.
AI.motivation: Aspect-based sentiment analysis needs models that can accurately connect sentiment expressions to the correct aspects, which is a challenging fine-grained reasoning problem. Existing approaches often rely on scaling model size rather than improving structural reasoning, and they may struggle to robustly bind evidence to aspects, especially on challenging benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors design GHI, a Graphormer-over-Conditioned-Hypergraph-Incidence framework. It builds a bipartite graph between tokens and hyperedges, where hyperedges encode diverse linguistic and semantic evidence. These token–hyperedge incidence relations let multiple structural signals be injected through a unified interface. GHI is used as a structural reasoning layer on top of a language model backbone (e.g., DeBERTa).
AI.result: On six standard ABSA benchmarks, GHI outperforms all baselines on SemEval domains and shows stable gains over a strong DeBERTa baseline across multiple random seeds. With 247M parameters, GHI nearly matches the performance of much larger (11B parameter) Flan-T5-based methods on the ISE benchmark. On the difficult ARTS datasets, GHI remains robust and competitive while traditional models show performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: Compact, incidence-based structural reasoning via GHI can rival or surpass scale-driven large models on fine-grained ABSA tasks. Hypergraph-incidence representations provide an effective, unified way to integrate diverse structural signals and improve robustness, highlighting that better structure, not just model size, is crucial for fine-grained sentiment reasoning.

title: ArborKV: Structure-Aware KV Cache Management for Scaling Tree-based LLM Reasoning
summary: Recent progress in LLM reasoning has increasingly shifted from single-pass generation to explicit search over intermediate reasoning states. Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) organizes inference to tree-structured search with branching and backtracking, but it substantially amplifies the Key--Value (KV) cache: retaining KV states for a frontier of partial trajectories quickly becomes a memory bottleneck that limits throughput and constrains search depth and width under fixed hardware budgets. We address this challenge by observing that KV reuse in ToT-style inference is governed by search dynamics: near-term decoding depends primarily on the active branch and its ancestors, whereas inactive subtrees have low short-term reuse probability yet must remain recoverable for backtracking. Motivated by this, we propose ArborKV, a structure-aware eviction framework that couples a lightweight value estimator with a tree-aware allocation policy, and performs purely token-extractive eviction with lazy rehydration to support revisits. Experiments on ToT-style reasoning benchmarks show that ArborKV achieves up to ~4x peak KV-memory reduction while preserving near-full-retention accuracy, enabling larger search configurations under fixed device budgets that would otherwise run out of memory.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ArborKV, a tree-structure-aware KV-cache eviction and recovery framework for Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) style LLM reasoning that significantly reduces memory usage while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) reasoning requires exploring many partial reasoning branches, but standard implementations must retain the full key–value (KV) cache for every active and potentially revisitable branch. This causes KV memory to grow quickly with search width and depth, becoming the throughput and scalability bottleneck under fixed GPU/TPU memory budgets, and preventing the use of larger or more exhaustive search configurations.
AI.method: The authors analyze KV reuse patterns in ToT-style search and observe that near-future decoding mostly relies on the currently active branch and its ancestors, while inactive subtrees are rarely accessed in the short term but must be recoverable for possible backtracking. Based on this, they design ArborKV: (1) a lightweight value estimator that scores which KV segments are least valuable for near-term decoding, (2) a tree-aware allocation and eviction policy that prefers to keep KV for the active path and ancestors while evicting lower-priority inactive subtrees, and (3) a token-extractive eviction mechanism with lazy rehydration, where evicted states can be efficiently reconstructed from stored tokens when a branch is revisited. This yields a structure-aware, dynamic memory manager for KV caches during ToT search.
AI.result: On Tree-of-Thoughts-style reasoning benchmarks, ArborKV reduces peak KV memory usage by up to about 4x compared to full retention, while maintaining accuracy close to the baseline that keeps all KV states. This allows running deeper or wider ToT searches, or using larger models, within the same device memory budget—configurations that would otherwise exhaust memory.
AI.conclusion: ArborKV effectively decouples Tree-of-Thoughts search quality from KV cache growth by using a tree-structure-aware, value-guided eviction and lazy rehydration strategy. This significantly improves memory efficiency—up to 4x peak KV reduction—without notable accuracy loss, thereby enabling more ambitious ToT search configurations under fixed hardware constraints.

title: IdioLink: Retrieving Meaning Beyond Words Across Idiomatic and Literal Expressions
summary: Idioms pose a fundamental challenge for language models, as their meaning cannot be inferred from surface form alone. Understanding such expressions, therefore, requires semantic abstraction beyond lexical overlap. We introduce IdioLink, a retrieval benchmark designed to test whether models can link idiomatic expressions to conceptually equivalent meanings expressed in literal or paraphrased forms. IdioLink comprises 10,700 documents and 2,140 queries, spanning 107 idioms with both literal and figurative uses. Each document and query is annotated with spans that convey the core meaning. Evaluating strong embedding baselines (e.g., BGE, E5, Contriever, and Qwen), we show that current models struggle to retrieve equivalent meanings across divergent surface realizations, relying instead on topical and shallow semantic cues. IdioLink exposes key gaps in idiom-aware semantic retrieval and provides a challenging testbed for future models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces IdioLink, a benchmark to test whether language models can correctly link idiomatic expressions to their literal or paraphrased meanings, revealing that current embedding models largely fail at idiom-aware semantic retrieval.
AI.motivation: Idioms are challenging for language models because their meanings cannot be deduced from individual words or surface similarity. Existing retrieval and embedding models often rely on lexical overlap or shallow topical cues and are not explicitly evaluated on their ability to understand and match idiomatic meaning. There is a need for a benchmark that specifically tests semantic abstraction and idiom understanding in retrieval settings.
AI.method: The authors construct IdioLink, a retrieval benchmark containing 10,700 documents and 2,140 queries covering 107 idioms with both literal and figurative uses. For each query–document pair, they annotate the text spans that convey the core meaning of the idiom, whether expressed idiomatically, literally, or via paraphrase. They then evaluate several strong embedding-based retrieval models (BGE, E5, Contriever, Qwen) on the task of retrieving documents that are conceptually equivalent in meaning despite surface differences.
AI.result: The evaluated embedding models perform poorly at retrieving documents that express the same core meaning as the query when idioms are involved, especially when surface forms diverge. Their retrieval behavior indicates a reliance on topical similarity and shallow semantic cues rather than robust idiom-level semantic abstraction.
AI.conclusion: IdioLink highlights that current embedding and retrieval models are not yet proficient at idiom-aware semantic retrieval. The benchmark exposes significant gaps in their ability to match conceptually equivalent meanings beyond surface form and offers a challenging testbed to drive research on deeper semantic understanding of idiomatic expressions in retrieval models.

title: Perception or Prejudice: Can MLLMs Go Beyond First Impressions of Personality?
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are increasingly deployed in human-facing roles where personality perception is critical, yet existing benchmarks evaluate this capability solely on numerical Big Five score prediction, leaving open whether models truly perceive personality through behavioral understanding or merely prejudge through superficial pattern matching. We address this gap with three contributions. (i) A new task: we formalize Grounded Personality Reasoning (GPR), which requires MLLMs to anchor each Big Five rating in observable evidence through a chain of rating, reasoning, and grounding. (ii) A new dataset: we release MM-OCEAN (1,104 videos, 5,320 MCQs), produced by a multi-agent pipeline with human verification, with timestamped behavioral observations, evidence-grounded trait analyses, and seven categories of cue-grounding MCQs. (iii) Benchmark and analysis: we design a three-tier evaluation (rating, reasoning, grounding) plus four sample-level failure-mode metrics: Prejudice Rate (PR), Confabulation Rate (CR), Integration-failure Rate (IR), and Holistic-grounding Rate (HR), and benchmark 27 MLLMs (13 closed, 14 open). The analysis uncovers a striking Prejudice Gap: across the field, 51% of correct ratings are not grounded in retrieved cues, and the Holistic-Grounding Rate spans only 0-33.5%. These findings expose a disconnect between getting the right score and reasoning for the right reason, charting a roadmap for grounded social cognition in MLLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes and benchmarks a new way to test whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) genuinely understand human personality from observable behavior, rather than guessing traits from superficial cues.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of MLLMs on personality focus almost entirely on how accurately models predict numerical Big Five trait scores. This leaves a critical gap: a model might achieve good scores by stereotyping based on appearance or context instead of properly interpreting behavioral evidence. In human-facing applications—like hiring, education, or mental health support—this difference is crucial. The authors want an evaluation framework that tests whether models (1) ground their trait judgments in concrete, observable cues, and (2) reason about those cues in a psychologically meaningful way, rather than relying on prejudiced shortcuts.
AI.method: They introduce Grounded Personality Reasoning (GPR), a task that requires models to produce, for each Big Five trait rating: (a) a trait score, (b) an explanation (reasoning), and (c) explicit links to specific observable cues in the video (grounding). To support this, they build MM-OCEAN, a dataset of 1,104 videos with 5,320 multiple-choice questions. A multi-agent annotation pipeline, followed by human verification, produces timestamped behavioral observations, evidence-grounded trait analyses, and seven types of cue-grounding MCQs. They then define a three-tier evaluation protocol—rating accuracy, reasoning quality, and grounding correctness—plus four failure-mode metrics: Prejudice Rate (correct rating without correct evidence), Confabulation Rate (using non-existent or wrong evidence), Integration-failure Rate (failing to combine multiple cues), and Holistic-grounding Rate (extent to which models ground ratings in diverse, relevant cues). They benchmark 27 MLLMs (13 closed-source, 14 open-source) on this framework.
AI.result: Across the evaluated models, many can often predict correct Big Five trait scores but fail to ground these predictions in appropriate behavioral evidence. They find a large Prejudice Gap: 51% of correct ratings are not supported by correctly retrieved cues, implying that models frequently arrive at right answers for the wrong reasons. Holistic-grounding performance is very low overall, ranging from 0% to 33.5% across models, indicating poor use of multiple relevant cues. The detailed metrics reveal systematic weaknesses: over-reliance on superficial patterns, hallucinated justifications, and difficulty integrating multimodal signals.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current MLLMs show a substantial disconnect between outcome accuracy (correct personality ratings) and process validity (reasoning and evidence grounding). Simply measuring trait prediction accuracy is insufficient to assess genuine social understanding.
