Their GPR task, MM-OCEAN dataset, and multi-level metrics provide a more rigorous standard for evaluating and improving grounded social cognition in MLLMs. This framework can guide future model development toward systems that do not just guess personality correctly, but can justify their inferences using concrete, observable behavior in a transparent and psychologically plausible way.

title: Harder to Defend: Towards Chinese Toxicity Attacks via Implicit Enhancement and Obfuscation Rewriting
summary: Large language models (LLMs) require robust toxicity evaluation beyond explicit wording. This setting remains underexplored in Chinese, where toxicity may combine semantic indirectness with surface obfuscation. We introduce Chinese Implicit Toxicity Attack (CITA), a controlled red-team evaluation and defense-data generation framework, not a deployable evasion tool. CITA uses three stages: (i) Harmful Intent Learning, (ii) Implicit Toxicity Enhancement, and (iii) Obfuscation Variant Rewriting, to preserve harmful intent, increase implicitness, and add controlled surface variants. On CITA-generated evaluation samples, the seven tested detectors exhibit substantial missed-detection risks, reaching an average ASR of 69.48%; human evaluation further confirms preserved harmfulness and increased implicitness/evasiveness. As a downstream defense application, we fine-tune a Chinese Implicit Toxicity Defense model (CITD) with CITA-generated red-team data, showing that such data can improve robustness through additional training.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CITA, a framework to generate implicit and obfuscated Chinese toxic prompts for red-teaming LLM toxicity detectors and creating defense training data, revealing high miss rates and demonstrating robustness gains after fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing toxicity evaluations for LLMs focus mainly on explicit, surface-level toxicity and are underdeveloped for Chinese, where toxicity can be conveyed indirectly and via obfuscation. This gap leads to overestimated safety, as current detectors may miss harmful content that hides toxicity implicitly in semantics or in modified surface forms. There is a need for a systematic way to generate such challenging toxic inputs in Chinese for both evaluation and defense data creation.
AI.method: The authors design CITA (Chinese Implicit Toxicity Attack), a three-stage controlled generation framework. (1) Harmful Intent Learning: capture and preserve the underlying harmful intent in prompts. (2) Implicit Toxicity Enhancement: rewrite content to make toxicity more implicit and indirect while keeping the harmful semantics. (3) Obfuscation Variant Rewriting: add controlled surface-level obfuscations (e.g., variants, masked characters, altered wording) to increase evasiveness without losing intent. They then use CITA to produce evaluation sets, test seven existing toxicity detectors on them, run human evaluations to validate harmfulness and implicitness, and finally use the generated data to fine-tune a defense model, CITD.
AI.result: Across seven tested toxicity detectors, CITA-generated samples lead to high attack success rates, with an average ASR of 69.48%, indicating substantial missed detection of implicit/obfuscated toxicity. Human annotators confirm that the generated samples still carry harmful intent while being more implicit and evasive than baseline toxic expressions. When the CITA-generated data are used to fine-tune a Chinese Implicit Toxicity Defense model (CITD), the resulting model shows improved robustness to such attacks, demonstrating the utility of the framework for defense training.
AI.conclusion: CITA is an effective red-teaming and data-generation framework for Chinese implicit toxicity, revealing that current detectors substantially underperform on indirect and obfuscated toxic content. The framework can systematically produce challenging evaluation data and, when used for training, can enhance the robustness of toxicity defenses, as shown by the improved performance of the CITD model. This underscores the importance of modeling both semantic implicitness and surface obfuscation in safety evaluations for LLMs.

title: Efficient Agentic Reasoning Through Self-Regulated Simulative Planning
comment: Code and model artifacts are available at https://github.com/sailing-lab/sr2am
summary: How should an agent decide when and how to plan? A dominant approach builds agents as reactive policies with adaptive computation (e.g., chain-of-thought), trained end-to-end expecting planning to emerge implicitly. Without control over the presence, structure, or horizon of planning, these systems dramatically increase reasoning length, yielding inefficient token use without reliable accuracy gains. We argue efficient agentic reasoning benefits from decomposing decision-making into three systems: simulative reasoning (System II) grounding deliberation in future-state prediction via a world model; self-regulation (System III) deciding when and how deeply to plan via a learned configurator; and reactive execution (System I) handling fine-grained action. Simulative reasoning provides unified planning across diverse tasks without per-domain engineering, while self-regulation ensures the planner is invoked only when needed. To test this, we develop SR$^2$AM (Self-Regulated Simulative Reasoning Agentic LLM), realizing both as distinct stages within an LLM's chain-of-thought, with the LLM as world model. We explore two instantiations: recording decisions from a prompted multi-module system (v0.1) and reconstructing structured plans from traces of pretrained reasoning LLMs (v1.0), trained via supervised then reinforcement learning (RL). Across math, science, tabular analysis, and web information seeking, v0.1-8B and v1.0-30B achieve Pass@1 competitive with 120-355B and 685B-1T parameter systems respectively, while v1.0-30B uses 25.8-95.3% fewer reasoning tokens than comparable agentic LLMs. RL increases average planning horizon by 22.8% while planning frequency grows only 2.0%, showing it learns to plan further ahead rather than more often. More broadly, learned self-regulation instantiates a principle we expect to extend beyond planning to how agents govern their own learning and adaptation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SR^2AM, an LLM-based agent architecture that separates planning, self-regulation, and execution to achieve competitive performance with far fewer reasoning tokens.
AI.motivation: End-to-end reactive LLM agents with implicit planning (e.g., long chain-of-thought) often use many tokens inefficiently without corresponding accuracy gains, and offer little control over when or how planning occurs. The authors want a principled, more efficient agentic reasoning framework that explicitly controls planning behavior and token usage while remaining general across tasks.
AI.method: They decompose decision-making into three systems: (I) reactive execution for local actions, (II) simulative reasoning using a world model for explicit planning, and (III) self-regulation that decides when and how deeply to invoke the planner. They instantiate this as SR^2AM, where both simulative reasoning and self-regulation are realized as distinct stages inside an LLM’s chain-of-thought using the LLM itself as the world model. They build two versions: v0.1 based on a prompted multi-module system whose decisions are recorded, and v1.0 which reconstructs structured plans from traces of pretrained reasoning LLMs. Both are trained with supervised learning followed by reinforcement learning to optimize planning behavior.
AI.result: On tasks spanning math, science, table reasoning, and web information seeking, SR^2AM v0.1 with 8B parameters and v1.0 with 30B parameters reach Pass@1 performance comparable to much larger 120–355B and 685B–1T parameter systems. The v1.0-30B model uses 25.8–95.3% fewer reasoning tokens than other agentic LLMs at similar accuracy. RL training increases the average planning horizon by 22.8% but only raises planning frequency by 2.0%, indicating the model learns to plan deeper when planning is invoked rather than planning more often.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly separating simulative reasoning, self-regulation, and reactive execution enables LLM agents that plan efficiently and competitively with much larger models while using substantially fewer reasoning tokens. Learned self-regulation successfully controls when and how much to plan, extending planning depth without overusing planning. The authors suggest this self-regulation mechanism is a general principle for how future agents might autonomously govern not just planning but their broader learning and adaptation processes.

title: Pattern-and-root inflectional morphology: the Arabic broken plural
summary: We present a substantially implemented model of description of the inflectional morphology of Arabic nouns, with special attention to the management of dictionaries and other language resources by Arabic-speaking linguists. The breakthrough lies in the reversal of the traditional root-and-pattern Semitic model into pattern-and-root, giving precedence to patterns over roots. Our model includes broken plurals (BPs), i.e. plurals formed by modifying the stem. It is based on the traditional notions of root and pattern of Semitic morphology. However, as compared to traditional Arabic morphology, it keeps the formal description of inflection separate from that of derivation and semantics. As traditional Arabic dictionaries, the updatable dictionary is structured in lexical entries for lemmas, and the reference spelling is fully diacritized. In our model, morphological analysis of Arabic text is performed directly with a dictionary of words and without morphophonological rules. Our taxonomy for noun inflection is simple, orderly and detailed. We simplify the taxonomy of singular patterns by specifying vowel quantity as v or vv, and ignoring vowel quality. Root alternations and orthographical variations are encoded independently from patterns and in a factual way, without deep roots or morphophonological or orthographical rules. Nouns with a triliteral BP are classified according to 22 patterns subdivided into 90 classes, and nouns with a quadriliteral BP according to 3 patterns subdivided into 70 classes. These 160 classes become 300 inflectional classes when we take into account inflectional variations that affect only the singular. We provide a straightforward encoding scheme that we applied to 3 200 entries of BP nouns.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and implements a pattern‑first, dictionary‑based model for Arabic noun inflection (including broken plurals), offering a detailed yet practical classification and encoding scheme used on 3,200 noun entries.
AI.motivation: Existing descriptions of Arabic inflectional morphology—especially for broken plurals—are either too theory‑heavy (root‑and‑pattern focus, deep morphophonology) or not directly usable by working Arabic linguists for lexicon management and computational analysis. Traditional approaches also mix inflection, derivation, and semantics, and often rely on complex morphophonological rules rather than explicit lexical resources. The authors aim to build a model that is formally precise, easy to update, aligned with how Arabic lexicographers work, and suitable for direct morphological analysis without rule-heavy processing.
AI.method: They invert the traditional root‑and‑pattern model into a pattern‑and‑root model: patterns are primary, roots are secondary. They build an updatable, lemma‑based, fully‑diacritized dictionary of nouns, including broken plurals, and design a simple but fine‑grained taxonomy of inflectional patterns. Singular patterns are simplified by encoding only vowel quantity (v/vv) and ignoring vowel quality. Root alternations and orthographic variants are stored explicitly, separate from patterns and without generative morphophonological rules. Nouns with triliteral broken plurals are classified into 22 patterns (90 classes); quadriliteral broken plurals into 3 patterns (70 classes). Taking singular‑only inflectional variants into account yields 300 inflectional classes overall. They propose a straightforward encoding scheme and apply it to 3,200 BP noun entries.
AI.result: They obtain a fully implemented, dictionary‑based model of Arabic noun inflection, especially broken plurals, that does not rely on morphophonological rules. The model consists of a detailed but manageable taxonomy: 22 triliteral BP patterns (90 classes) and 3 quadriliteral BP patterns (70 classes), expanded to 300 inflectional classes when singular variation is included. They successfully encode 3,200 broken‑plural noun entries using their scheme, demonstrating its practical applicability for resource management and morphological analysis.
AI.conclusion: A pattern‑first, dictionary‑driven approach can capture Arabic noun inflection, including broken plurals, in a way that is both linguistically adequate and practically useful. Separating inflection from derivation and semantics, simplifying vowel representation, and encoding root/orthographic alternations factually (rather than via deep morphophonological rules) yields a clear, extensible taxonomy that Arabic linguists can maintain. The implemented model and encoding applied to thousands of entries show that robust morphological analysis of Arabic text can be performed directly from a well‑structured lexicon without complex rule systems.

title: Ratchet: A Minimal Hygiene Recipe for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
comment: 16 pages, 2 figures, 6 tables. Extends arXiv:2605.19576 with the SWE-bench Verified evaluation and a non-divergence analysis (Proposition 1)
summary: Self-evolving skill libraries, pioneered by Voyager, let frozen LLM agents accumulate reusable knowledge without weight updates, yet recent evaluation shows that LLM-authored skills deliver $+0.0$pp over no-skill baselines while human-curated ones deliver $+16.2$pp: the bottleneck is not skill authoring but lifecycle management. We introduce \textbf{Ratchet}, a single-agent loop in which a frozen LLM writes, retrieves, curates, and retires its own natural-language skills. Ratchet integrates four candidate hygiene mechanisms: outcome-driven retirement, a bounded active-cap, meta-skill authoring guidance, and pattern canonicalisation. On MBPP+ hard-100 with Claude Opus 4.7, Ratchet lifts held-out pass@1 from a $0.258 \pm 0.047$ baseline to a late-window rolling mean of $0.584$ (peak $0.658 \pm 0.042$) across 100 rounds and 3 seeds, a $+0.328 \pm 0.018$ rolling-mean gain where the no-skill control drifts at $+0.002 \pm 0.005$; the same recipe transfers to an agentic solver on SWE-bench Verified ($+0.22$ peak lift over 20 rounds). Eight ablations (A1--A8) reveal that the minimal working recipe is smaller than our design suggests: retirement and the meta-skill authoring prior are load-bearing, while explicit deduplication (canonicalisation, cover-guard) is subsumed by the meta-skill itself. A non-divergence proposition shows that bounded cap and retirement threshold together prevent expected performance from drifting below the no-skills floor.

AI.tldr: Ratchet is a single-LLM agent loop that self-manages a natural-language skill library and, through better lifecycle management (authoring, retrieval, curation, retirement), yields large performance gains over no-skill baselines on coding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Voyager-style self-evolving skill libraries allow frozen LLMs to accumulate reusable skills without updating model weights, but empirical results show that LLM-authored skills provide almost no benefit while human-authored ones help a lot. This indicates that the bottleneck is not the ability to write skills but to manage their lifecycle: which skills to keep, when to use them, and when to retire them, to avoid drift and bloat. The paper aims to design and analyze mechanisms that let a single frozen LLM autonomously maintain a high-quality, non-divergent skill library.
AI.method: The authors propose Ratchet, a single-agent loop where a frozen LLM iteratively solves tasks while writing new skills, retrieving existing ones, curating them, and retiring ineffective ones. Ratchet incorporates four hygiene mechanisms: (1) outcome-driven retirement that removes poorly performing skills; (2) a bounded cap on the number of active skills to limit bloat and enforce turnover; (3) meta-skill authoring guidance (a prior) that shapes how new skills are written and how they generalize; and (4) pattern canonicalisation and related deduplication mechanisms (cover-guard) to reduce redundancy. They evaluate Ratchet on MBPP+ hard-100 with Claude Opus 4.7 over 100 rounds and 3 random seeds, comparing against no-skill controls, and transfer the same protocol to an agentic SWE-bench Verified solver. They also run eight ablation experiments (A1–A8) to test the contribution of each hygiene component. Additionally, they provide a non-divergence proposition showing that combining a bounded skill cap with a retirement threshold keeps expected performance from dropping below the no-skills baseline.
AI.result: On MBPP+ hard-100, Ratchet raises held-out pass@1 from a baseline of 0.258 ± 0.047 to a late-window rolling mean of 0.584, with a peak of 0.658 ± 0.042 across 100 rounds and 3 seeds—a rolling-mean improvement of 0.328 ± 0.018, while the no-skill control shows almost no drift (0.002 ± 0.005). Applying the same Ratchet procedure to an agentic solver on SWE-bench Verified yields up to a +0.22 peak performance lift over 20 rounds. Ablations show that the key necessary components are outcome-driven retirement and the meta-skill authoring prior; explicit deduplication techniques like pattern canonicalisation and cover-guard are effectively handled implicitly by the meta-skill mechanism. The theoretical analysis demonstrates that, with an appropriate cap and retirement threshold, the system’s expected performance is guaranteed not to fall below that of an agent without any skill library.
AI.conclusion: Ratchet demonstrates that effective lifecycle management—particularly outcome-based retirement and guided meta-skill authoring—allows frozen LLM agents to build and maintain highly useful natural-language skill libraries, yielding substantial performance gains on coding benchmarks without updating model weights. Explicit deduplication mechanisms are less critical than initially assumed, as their benefits are largely captured by a well-designed meta-skill authoring prior. Theoretical guarantees further show that with bounded capacity and retirement thresholds, such self-evolving skill libraries do not diverge below baseline performance, making them a robust way to accumulate reusable knowledge in LLM agents.

title: TransitLM: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Map-Free Transit Route Generation
summary: Public transit route planning traditionally depends on structured map infrastructure and complex routing engines, and no existing dataset supports training models to bypass this dependency. We present TransitLM, a large-scale dataset of over 13 million transit route planning records from four Chinese cities covering 120,845 stations and 13,666 lines, released as a continual pre-training corpus and benchmark data for three evaluation tasks with complementary metrics. Experiments show that an LLM trained on TransitLM produces structurally valid routes at high accuracy and implicitly grounds arbitrary GPS coordinates to appropriate stations without any explicit mapping. These results demonstrate that transit route planning can be learned entirely from data, enabling end-to-end, map-free route generation directly from origin-destination information. The dataset and benchmark are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/GD-ML/TransitLM, with evaluation code at https://github.com/HotTricker/TransitLM.

AI.tldr: TransitLM introduces a large-scale dataset and benchmark enabling large language models to perform end-to-end, map-free public transit route planning directly from origin-destination information.
AI.motivation: Traditional public transit route planning requires structured map data and specialized routing engines, and there is no existing dataset that allows models to learn to plan routes without this infrastructure. The authors aim to show that route planning can be learned purely from data, bypassing explicit maps and routing engines.
AI.method: The authors construct TransitLM, a dataset of over 13 million real-world transit route planning records from four Chinese cities, covering 120,845 stations and 13,666 lines. They release it both as a continual pre-training corpus for LLMs and as a benchmark with three evaluation tasks and complementary metrics. They then train an LLM on this corpus and evaluate its ability to generate transit routes and map GPS coordinates to stations.
AI.result: An LLM trained on TransitLM can generate structurally valid public transit routes with high accuracy and can implicitly ground arbitrary GPS coordinates to appropriate transit stations, all without any explicit mapping information or traditional routing engines.
AI.conclusion: Transit route planning can be learned entirely from large-scale historical planning records, enabling end-to-end, map-free route generation from origin-destination inputs. TransitLM provides the data and benchmarks needed to develop and evaluate such models, potentially reducing reliance on traditional map infrastructure and routing engines.

title: IdleSpec: Exploiting Idle Time via Speculative Planning for LLM Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents solve complex tasks by leveraging multi-step reasoning with iterative tool calls and environment interactions, which incur idle time while waiting for observations. Despite the prevalence of idle time in most agentic scenarios, existing works treat it as an unavoidable overhead or propose restricted solutions that overlook varying computational budgets across different tool calls and future observation uncertainty, thereby leading to suboptimal utilization of idle time. In this paper, we introduce IdleSpec, a scalable and generic inference approach that leverages idle-time computation to improve agent performance while minimizing latency overhead. Specifically, IdleSpec iteratively generates plan candidates during idle periods and, once observations become available, aggregates them to guide the next reasoning step. For effective plan generation under observation uncertainty, IdleSpec samples between complementary drafting strategies (i.e., progressive and recovery) from a learned distribution that is updated via posterior feedback. Our experiments demonstrate that IdleSpec significantly improves agent performance in various agentic scenarios by effectively utilizing idle time. In particular, on the GAIA and FRAMES, IdleSpec achieves 55.6% average accuracy with Gemini-2.5-Flash, surpassing the vanilla baseline without idle-time usage by 5.1%. Furthermore, for MLE-Bench, which involves substantial delay from code executions, IdleSpec achieves performance gains of up to 9.1% on the Any Medal rate, highlighting its generalizability to long-horizon tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes IdleSpec, a method that exploits the LLM agent’s waiting (idle) time to pre-generate and refine plans, thereby improving accuracy with minimal extra latency.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents often wait for tool or environment responses, creating idle periods where the model is not doing useful computation. Existing methods largely ignore this time or use simplistic strategies that fail to account for different computation costs across tools and uncertainty about future observations, leading to inefficiencies and missed opportunities to improve performance during these idle periods.
AI.method: IdleSpec is a generic inference framework that uses idle time to iteratively draft multiple plan candidates before new observations arrive. When observations are received, it aggregates these candidates to guide the agent’s next reasoning step. To handle uncertainty about future observations, IdleSpec alternates between two complementary plan-drafting strategies—progressive (moving forward from current context) and recovery (anticipating corrections or failures)—by sampling from a learned strategy distribution that is updated using posterior feedback from actual outcomes.
AI.result: Across several agentic benchmarks (GAIA, FRAMES, and MLE-Bench), IdleSpec leads to substantial performance gains without significant latency overhead. With Gemini-2.5-Flash on GAIA and FRAMES, it reaches 55.6% average accuracy, a 5.1% improvement over a baseline that does not use idle-time computation. On MLE-Bench, where code execution delays are large, IdleSpec improves the Any Medal rate by up to 9.1%, showing effectiveness in long-horizon, tool-heavy settings.
AI.conclusion: IdleSpec demonstrates that intelligently exploiting idle time in LLM-based agents can meaningfully improve task performance while keeping latency low. By adaptively combining progressive and recovery planning during waiting periods and aggregating these drafts once new information arrives, the approach offers a scalable, model-agnostic way to enhance multi-step, tool-using agents, especially in long-horizon or tool-latency-dominated scenarios.

title: Modeling Pathology-Like Behavioral Patterns in Language Models Through Behavioral Fine-Tuning
summary:
Large language models are increasingly used as computational tools for modeling human-like behavior. We introduce a behavioral induction framework that modifies model policies through fine-tuning on structured decision-making tasks: using synthetic datasets inspired by maladaptive behavioral patterns, including depression and paranoia, we train transformer-based language models to consistently select specific classes of actions across diverse contexts. We then test whether this behavioral optimization produces systematic changes in generative distributions.
  Across two architectures, fine-tuned models show stable, context-general shifts in next-token probability distributions, including increased probability assigned to negative and threat-related interpretations in open-ended language tasks. These effects generalize beyond training contexts and are detectable in qualitative completions, psychometric-style evaluations, and quantitative distributional metrics such as Jensen-Shannon divergence.
  Induced behavioral profiles also show partial specificity. Models optimized for different behavioral patterns exhibit dissociable response tendencies across evaluation probes, suggesting that structured behavioral training produces differentiated policy-level biases rather than generic distributional skew.
  We interpret these findings as evidence that consistent behavioral optimization in LLMs can generate stable behavioral and distributional patterns consistent with altered latent priors, linking action selection and language generation. More broadly, the results support a view of LLMs as policy-based systems in which behavioral constraints shape emergent representational structure, highlighting their potential as controlled testbeds for studying the relationship between behavior, interpretation, and generative language in computational models of cognition.

AI.tldr: The paper fine-tunes LLMs on synthetic decision-making tasks that embody maladaptive behavioral patterns (e.g., depressive, paranoid) and shows that this behavioral optimization reliably shifts their language generation toward corresponding negative or threat-focused interpretations, even in general open-ended tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how shaping an LLM’s action policy on structured decision-making tasks can systematically alter its broader language generation behavior, in a way that parallels how consistent behavioral tendencies in humans (like depressive or paranoid styles) affect interpretation and expression. More broadly, to test whether LLMs can serve as controllable models for studying links between behavior, interpretation bias, and generative language in cognitive science.
AI.method: The authors build synthetic datasets of structured decision-making tasks that encode particular maladaptive behavioral patterns, such as consistently choosing pessimistic or threat-focused options. They then fine-tune transformer LLMs on these tasks so that the models reliably select the pattern-consistent actions. After fine-tuning, they evaluate the models on a range of out-of-distribution, open-ended language tasks and probes, including qualitative prompts, psychometric-style questionnaires, and quantitative analysis of next-token distributions. They measure shifts using metrics like Jensen–Shannon divergence and examine content such as negativity and threat-related interpretations.
AI.result: Fine-tuned models show robust, context-general shifts in their next-token distributions, assigning higher probability to negative and threat-related continuations even outside the training contexts. These induced changes are visible in free-form completions, in structured psychometric evaluations, and in distributional statistics. Furthermore, models optimized for different maladaptive patterns (e.g., depressive vs. paranoid) produce distinct, dissociable response profiles rather than a single generic negativity bias, indicating partial specificity of the induced behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Consistent behavioral optimization via fine-tuning can imprint stable, differentiated behavioral and distributional profiles onto LLMs, akin to altered latent priors that shape both action selection and language generation. This supports viewing LLMs as policy-based systems whose learned behavioral constraints influence their internal representational structure. Consequently, such models provide a promising controlled testbed for exploring how behavioral tendencies relate to interpretation biases and language production in computational accounts of cognition.

title: ST-SimDiff: Balancing Spatiotemporal Similarity and Difference for Efficient Video Understanding with MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face significant computational overhead when processing long videos due to the massive number of visual tokens required. To improve efficiency, existing methods primarily reduce redundancy by pruning or merging tokens based on importance or similarity. However, these approaches largely overlook a critical dimension of video content, i.e., changes and turning points, and they lack a collaborative model for spatio-temporal relationships. To address this, we propose a new perspective: similarity is for identifying redundancy, while difference is for capturing key events. Based on this, we designed a training-free framework named ST-SimDiff. We first construct a spatio-temporal graph from the visual tokens to uniformly model their complex associations. Subsequently, we employ a parallel dual-selection strategy: 1) similarity-based selection uses community detection to retain representative tokens, compressing static information; 2) temporal difference-based selection precisely locates content-changing points to preserve tokens that capture key dynamic shifts. This allows it to preserve both static and dynamic content with a minimal number of tokens. Extensive experiments show our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches while substantially reducing computational costs. Our code is available in https://github.com/bingjunluo/ST-SimDiff.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ST-SimDiff, a training-free token selection framework for MLLMs on long videos that jointly exploits similarity (to remove redundant static content) and temporal differences (to preserve key dynamic changes), achieving better performance with fewer visual tokens and lower computation.
AI.motivation: MLLMs incur heavy computation on long videos because they must process many visual tokens. Existing token reduction methods focus mostly on redundancy via similarity or importance, ignoring how changes and turning points in video content are critical and failing to jointly model spatio-temporal relationships. There is a need for an efficient approach that both removes redundant information and accurately preserves key static and dynamic events in videos.
AI.method: The authors build a training-free framework called ST-SimDiff. They first construct a spatio-temporal graph over visual tokens to model complex spatial and temporal associations uniformly. Then they apply a parallel dual-selection strategy: (1) similarity-based selection uses community detection on the graph to find and retain representative tokens, effectively compressing redundant static information; (2) temporal difference-based selection identifies content-changing points over time, preserving tokens that capture major dynamic shifts. The combination yields a compact set of tokens that still encodes static context and dynamic events.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments on long-video benchmarks, ST-SimDiff substantially reduces the number of visual tokens and thus computational cost for MLLMs, while significantly outperforming previous state-of-the-art token pruning/merging methods in accuracy and other evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: By treating similarity as a signal for redundancy and temporal difference as a signal for key events, and by modeling tokens with a spatio-temporal graph plus dual-selection, ST-SimDiff can preserve essential static and dynamic video content using far fewer tokens. This yields more efficient and more accurate MLLM processing of long videos than existing approaches, without requiring additional training.

title: Multi-Stage Training for Abusive Comment Detection in Indic Languages
summary: In recent years social media has become an increasingly popular tool for communication. People use it to share their ideas, exchange information, and discuss thoughts. Given its prevalence and widespread reach, social media must remain a safe space for people. Content generated on social media can be abusive and it has become increasingly important to detect such content. In this paper, we use a language-based preprocessing and an ensemble of several models and analyze their performance of abusive comment detection. Through extensive experimentation, we propose a pipeline that minimizes the false-positive rate (marking non-abusive as abusive) so that these systems can detect abusive comments without undermining the freedom of expression.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an abusive comment detection pipeline for social media that emphasizes minimizing false positives using language-based preprocessing and an ensemble of models.
AI.motivation: Abusive content on social media threatens user safety, yet overzealous automatic moderation risks censoring non-abusive speech and harming freedom of expression. There is a need for detection systems that effectively identify abusive comments while especially reducing the rate at which normal comments are incorrectly flagged as abusive.
AI.method: The authors design a language-based preprocessing stage for social media comments, then train and combine multiple models in an ensemble for abusive comment detection. They conduct extensive experiments comparing different model combinations and configurations, focusing on the impact of their preprocessing and ensembling on performance, particularly the false-positive rate.
AI.result: Their experiments show that the proposed combination of language-oriented preprocessing with an ensemble of several models yields improved abusive comment detection performance and, crucially, a lower false-positive rate compared with baseline or single-model approaches.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that a carefully designed pipeline integrating language-specific preprocessing and model ensembling can detect abusive comments more reliably while substantially reducing false positives, thereby supporting safer social media environments without excessively restricting legitimate expression.

title: Adapting the Interface, Not the Model: Runtime Harness Adaptation for Deterministic LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents are shaped not only by their language models, but also by the runtime harness that mediates observation, tool use, action execution, feedback interpretation, and trajectory control. While existing agent adaptation methods mainly update model parameters, many failures in deterministic, rule-governed domains stem from mismatches at the model--environment interface. We propose Life-Harness, a lifecycle-aware runtime harness that improves frozen LLM agents without changing model weights or evaluation environments. Life-Harness evolves from training trajectories by converting recurring interaction failures into reusable interventions across environment contracts, procedural skills, action realization, and trajectory regulation, and remains fixed during held-out evaluation. On seven deterministic environments from $τ$-bench, $τ^2$-bench, and AgentBench, Life-Harness improves 116 out of 126 model--environment settings across 18 model backbones, with an average relative improvement of 88.5%. Harnesses evolved only from Qwen3-4B-Instruct trajectories transfer to 17 other models, showing that Life-Harness captures reusable environment-side structure rather than model-specific behavior. These results position runtime interface adaptation as a complementary alternative to model-centric agent training. Code is available at GitHub.

AI.tldr: Life-Harness is a lifecycle-aware runtime harness that improves large language model (LLM) agents by adapting the agent–environment interface instead of changing model weights.
AI.motivation: Many failures of LLM-based agents in deterministic, rule-based environments arise not from the model’s internal reasoning, but from mismatches at the interface between the model and its environment—such as how observations, tools, and actions are represented, executed, and regulated over trajectories. Existing adaptation methods mostly focus on updating model parameters, leaving this interface under-optimized.
AI.method: The authors introduce Life-Harness, a lifecycle-aware runtime harness that sits between a frozen LLM and the environment. It analyzes training trajectories, detects recurring patterns of interaction failures (e.g., misusing tools, violating environment contracts, poor trajectory control), and turns them into reusable ‘interventions’. These interventions operate along several dimensions: environment contracts (how tasks and constraints are specified), procedural skills (how multi-step procedures are organized), action realization (how model outputs are converted into executable actions), and trajectory regulation (how to control, correct, or prune action sequences). The harness, once evolved from training data, is fixed and reused during held-out evaluation, without changing model weights or environments.
AI.result: On seven deterministic environments from τ-bench, τ²-bench, and AgentBench, Life-Harness improves performance in 116 out of 126 model–environment combinations across 18 different LLM backbones, achieving an average relative performance gain of 88.5%. A harness evolved solely from Qwen3-4B-Instruct trajectories transfers effectively to 17 other models, indicating that the learned interventions capture environment-side regularities rather than model-specific quirks.
AI.conclusion: Runtime interface adaptation via Life-Harness is an effective and general way to improve LLM agents in deterministic environments without fine-tuning model weights or altering evaluation setups. By systematically converting observed interaction failures into reusable, lifecycle-aware interventions, the harness provides substantial and transferable gains, positioning environment-side runtime design as a strong complement to traditional, model-centric training approaches.

title: Unified Data Selection for LLM Reasoning
summary: Effectively training Large Language Models (LLMs) for complex, long-CoT reasoning is often bottlenecked by the need for massive high-quality reasoning data. Existing methods are either computationally expensive or fail to reliably distinguish high- from low-quality reasoning samples. To address this, we propose High-Entropy Sum (HES), a training-free metric that quantifies reasoning quality by summing only the entropy of the top (e.g., 0.5\%) highest-entropy tokens in each reasoning sample. We validate HES across three mainstream training paradigms: Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT), Rejection Fine-tuning (RFT), and Reinforcement Learning (RL), with extensive results demonstrating its consistent effectiveness and significantly reduced computational overhead. In SFT, training on the top 20\% HES-ranked data matches full-dataset performance, while using the lowest-HES data degrades it. In RFT, our HES-based training approach significantly outperforms baseline methods. In RL, HES-selected successful trajectories enable the model to learn strong reasoning patterns, significantly surpassing other compared methods. Our findings establish HES as a robust, training-free metric that enables a unified, effective, and efficient method for developing advanced reasoning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces High-Entropy Sum (HES), a training-free metric that selects high-quality long-chain-of-thought reasoning data for LLMs, improving performance and efficiency across SFT, RFT, and RL training paradigms.
AI.motivation: Training LLMs for complex, long chain-of-thought reasoning requires large amounts of high-quality reasoning traces, but such data are expensive to obtain and hard to filter. Existing approaches to identify good reasoning are either computationally costly or unreliable at distinguishing strong from weak reasoning samples. The authors seek a simple, robust, and cheap signal that correlates with reasoning quality and can guide data selection and training across multiple learning paradigms.
AI.method: They propose High-Entropy Sum (HES), a training-free metric computed by summing the entropy of only the top fraction (e.g., 0.5%) of highest-entropy tokens within a reasoning trace. Intuitively, this focuses on the most uncertain, decision-critical points in the reasoning. They then sort reasoning samples by HES and use these rankings to select subsets of data for training. The method is evaluated within three paradigms—Supervised Fine-Tuning, Rejection Fine-Tuning, and Reinforcement Learning—by comparing models trained on high-HES, low-HES, and full datasets, and against baseline selection or training strategies.
AI.result: Across SFT, RFT, and RL, HES consistently serves as an effective signal of reasoning quality. In SFT, training on only the top 20% of samples ranked by HES achieves performance comparable to training on the full dataset, while using the lowest-HES samples harms performance, indicating that HES meaningfully distinguishes useful from harmful data. In RFT, using HES to guide which samples to keep or reject yields models that significantly outperform those trained with existing rejection or preference-based methods. In RL, selecting successful trajectories based on high HES allows the model to learn substantially stronger reasoning policies, outperforming alternative trajectory-selection or reward strategies. These gains come with far lower computational overhead than previous quality-estimation methods, since HES is fast and training-free.
AI.conclusion: HES is a simple, training-free, entropy-based metric that reliably correlates with high-quality reasoning in LLM chain-of-thought traces. By focusing on the most uncertain tokens, it offers a compact but powerful summary of reasoning quality that can guide data selection and training across SFT, RFT, and RL. The metric allows for large reductions in the amount of data used (e.g., 80% pruning in SFT) without sacrificing performance, and it improves over baseline methods in more complex training setups. Thus, HES provides a unified and computationally efficient tool for building LLMs with stronger, more advanced reasoning capabilities.

title: Measuring Cross-Modal Synergy: A Benchmark for VLM Explainability
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) map complex visual inputs to semantic spaces, but interpreting the cross-modal reasoning of VLMs currently relies on post-hoc explainers evaluated via unimodal perturbation metrics. We expose a limitation in this paradigm: because multimodal datasets contain language priors and modality biases, VLMs frequently exhibit cross-modal redundancy, allowing them to answer visual queries using text alone. Consequently, unimodal metrics penalize faithful explainers, triggering an evaluation collapse where visual and textual rankings fundamentally contradict each other. %(Kendall's $τ= -0.06$). To resolve this, we introduce Synergistic Faithfulness ($\mathcal{F}_{syn}$), a scalable metric rooted in the Shapley Interaction Index that strictly isolates the joint Harsanyi dividend between modalities, serving as a highly accurate surrogate ($ρ= 0.92$) while achieving a $24\times$ computational speedup. Evaluating 8 distinct XAI methods across 3 VLM architectures and 3 benchmark datasets, reveals that explainers proposed for VLMs heavily over-index on visual salience and significantly underperform adapted attention-based methods in capturing true cross-modal synergy. By decoupling visual plausibility from cross-modal faithfulness, this work provides a rigorous evaluation framework required to safely audit VLM reasoning in high-stakes deployments.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a major flaw in how we evaluate explanations for Vision-Language Models and proposes a new, efficient metric that specifically measures true cross-modal (vision+language) interaction, revealing that many existing VLM explainers are not actually faithful to how these models reason.
AI.motivation: Existing explanations for Vision-Language Models are judged mainly with unimodal perturbation metrics (e.g., removing pixels or words and seeing performance drops). But multimodal datasets have strong language priors and biases, so VLMs can often answer questions using only text, without really using the image. This cross-modal redundancy means that an explanation that correctly attributes importance to both image and text can be penalized by current metrics, making visual and textual evaluations disagree and leading to unreliable assessment of how models reason.
AI.method: The authors analyze how cross-modal redundancy causes contradictions between visual and textual explanation rankings and formally connect this to limitations of unimodal perturbation metrics. They then propose Synergistic Faithfulness (F_syn), a metric grounded in the Shapley Interaction Index and Harsanyi dividends, designed to isolate the true joint contribution (synergy) between vision and language features. They implement F_syn as a scalable surrogate of the full interaction index, achieving strong correlation with the exact measure while being much more computationally efficient. Using this metric, they systematically evaluate 8 XAI methods across 3 different VLM architectures and 3 benchmark datasets.
AI.result: They show that unimodal perturbation metrics can severely mis-rank explanations, with visual and textual rankings even becoming negatively correlated, evidencing evaluation collapse. The proposed Synergistic Faithfulness metric correlates strongly (ρ = 0.92) with the exact interaction measure, while being 24× faster to compute. Applying F_syn reveals that many VLM-specific explainers focus too much on visually salient regions and fail to capture the true cross-modal synergy, whereas adapted attention-based methods perform better in reflecting how VLMs actually integrate vision and language.
AI.conclusion: Unimodal perturbation-based evaluation is fundamentally insufficient for assessing explanations of Vision-Language Models because it ignores cross-modal redundancy and synergy, leading to misleading conclusions about faithfulness. The introduced Synergistic Faithfulness metric provides a principled, efficient way to measure genuine joint visual–textual contributions, separating visual plausibility from cross-modal faithfulness. This framework enables more reliable auditing of VLM reasoning, which is especially important in high-stakes applications where understanding how models use both modalities is critical.

title: DeferMem: Query-Time Evidence Distillation via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term Memory QA
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents still struggle with long-term memory question answering, where answer-supporting evidence is often scattered across long conversational histories and buried in substantial irrelevant content. Existing memory systems typically process memory before future queries are known, then retrieve the resulting units based on similarity rather than their utility for answering the query. This workflow leaves downstream answerers to denoise retrieved candidates and reconstruct query-specific evidence. We present DeferMem, a long-term memory framework that decouples this problem into high-recall candidate retrieval and query-conditioned evidence distillation. DeferMem uses a lightweight segment-link structure to organize raw history and retrieve broad candidates at query time. It then applies a memory distiller trained with DistillPO, our reinforcement learning algorithm for distilling the high-recall but highly noisy candidates into a set of faithful, self-contained, and query-conditioned evidence. DistillPO formulates post-retrieval evidence distillation as a structured action comprising message selection and evidence rewriting. It optimizes this action with a decomposed-and-gated reward pipeline and structure-aligned advantage assignment, gating reward components from validity to quality checks while exposing task-level correctness feedback early and assigning each reward to its responsible output span. On LoCoMo and LongMemEval-S, DeferMem surpasses strong baselines in QA accuracy and memory-system efficiency, achieving the highest QA accuracy with the fastest runtime and zero commercial-API token cost for memory operations.

AI.tldr: Introduces DeferMem, a long-term memory framework for LLM agents that separates high-recall retrieval from query-conditioned evidence distillation using a reinforcement learning-based distiller (DistillPO), achieving better QA accuracy and efficiency on long-context benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle with long-term memory QA because relevant evidence is sparsely distributed in long interaction histories and mixed with much irrelevant content. Existing memory systems pre-process and index memories without knowing future queries, then rely on similarity-based retrieval. This often returns noisy, redundant, or partially relevant content, forcing the downstream model to denoise and reconstruct the actual evidence needed to answer each query. The authors want a memory system that can adaptively form query-specific, faithful evidence from long histories while remaining efficient and cheap to run.
AI.method: They propose DeferMem, a two-stage long-term memory system. First, it organizes raw conversation history using a lightweight segment-link structure to support high-recall retrieval of broad candidate memory segments at query time. Second, it employs a memory distiller that takes these noisy candidates and produces a compact, self-contained, query-conditioned evidence set. The distiller is trained using DistillPO, a reinforcement learning algorithm that models post-retrieval evidence distillation as a structured action involving: (1) selecting which messages to keep and (2) rewriting them into clean evidence. DistillPO uses a decomposed-and-gated reward pipeline with structure-aligned advantage assignment: different reward components check validity, faithfulness, and quality, but are gated so that later, higher-level quality rewards apply only if basic validity is satisfied, and each reward signal is aligned with the specific output span (selection or rewrite) responsible for it. This encourages the distiller to produce accurate and useful evidence for answering queries.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo and LongMemEval-S benchmarks for long-context memory QA, DeferMem outperforms strong baselines in QA accuracy and memory-system efficiency. It achieves the best QA performance while also running faster and incurring no commercial API token costs for memory operations, indicating it is both effective and economical.
AI.conclusion: DeferMem demonstrates that deferring memory processing until query time and explicitly modeling post-retrieval evidence distillation can significantly improve long-term memory QA for LLM agents. By combining a simple high-recall retrieval structure with an RL-trained distiller that produces faithful, query-specific evidence, the framework yields better accuracy and efficiency than existing memory systems. This suggests that structured, query-conditioned distillation is a promising direction for scalable, low-cost long-term memory in LLM-based agents.

title: LLM-Metrics: Measuring Research Impact Through Large Language Model Memory
summary: Citation counts remain the dominant metric for assessing research impact, yet they suffer from well-documented limitations: temporal lag, disciplinary bias, and Matthew effects. Here we propose LLM-Metrics, a research-impact assessment metric derived from the parametric memory of large language models (LLMs). The central hypothesis is that high-impact papers receive greater exposure in the academic community, that this exposure enters LLM training data in textual form, and that models consequently form stronger parametric memory of these papers. We designed four types of multiple-choice probes, covering title recognition, author recognition, method recognition, and venue recognition, and evaluated 549 computer science papers published in 2023-2024 across 17 LLMs spanning 0.5B to 72B parameters from six vendors. Of the 17 models, 15 produced positive predictions, 9 of which were significant at p less than 0.05, with an overall Spearman correlation of rho = 0.1495 and p = 0.0004 against citation counts. Three additional findings support the proposed mechanism. First, the predictive signal was stronger for 2024 papers, rho = 0.1880, whose citation counts were near zero at model-training time, reducing the plausibility of a simple reverse-causality explanation. Second, author-recognition probes showed the strongest discriminative power, consistent with an exposure-driven memory mechanism. Third, model scale and predictive power were non-monotonic: a 3B-parameter model, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, with rho = 0.1829, outperformed most larger models, supporting a selective-memory hypothesis in which the limited capacity of smaller models can serve as an effective information filter. LLM-Metrics offers a real-time, cross-disciplinary, citation-independent paradigm for research assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LLM-Metrics, a new way to estimate research impact by testing how well large language models remember specific aspects of papers, finding a modest but significant correlation with citation counts and suggesting it can complement traditional metrics.
AI.motivation: Citation counts are slow to accumulate, biased across fields, and affected by rich-get-richer dynamics, creating demand for faster, fairer, and more nuanced impact metrics. Since high-impact work is more widely discussed and thus more likely to appear in text corpora used to train LLMs, the authors explore whether LLMs’ internal memory of papers can serve as a proxy for real-world scholarly visibility and impact.
AI.method: They operationalize “LLM memory” using four families of multiple-choice probes (title, author, method, and venue recognition) targeting 549 computer science papers from 2023–2024. They query 17 different LLMs (0.5B–72B parameters, six vendors), compute per-paper scores based on probe accuracy, and correlate these scores with citation counts using Spearman correlation. They further stratify results by publication year (2023 vs. 2024), probe type, and model size, and test statistical significance (p-values) to evaluate robustness and timing/causality issues.
AI.result: Across 17 models, 15 showed positive correlations between LLM-Metrics and citation counts, with 9 statistically significant, yielding an overall Spearman rho ≈ 0.15 (p = 0.0004). The correlation was stronger for 2024 papers (rho ≈ 0.19), whose citations were near zero during model training. Author-recognition probes were the most predictive. Model size did not monotonically increase predictive power; a 3B-parameter model (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) achieved rho ≈ 0.18 and surpassed most larger models.
AI.conclusion: LLM-Metrics captures a statistically significant, citation-aligned signal of research impact by leveraging LLMs’ parametric memory of papers and their authors. The stronger performance on very recent papers and the dominance of author-recognition probes support an exposure-driven mechanism rather than simple reverse causality. The non-monotonic relationship between model size and predictive power suggests smaller models may act as selective filters, potentially making them good impact sensors. Overall, LLM-Metrics is proposed as a real-time, citation-independent, cross-disciplinary complement—not a replacement—to traditional bibliometrics for research evaluation.

title: Assisted Counterspeech Writing at the Crossroads of Hate Speech and Misinformation
summary: Hate speech and misinformation frequently co-occur online, amplifying prejudice and polarization. Given their scale, using Large Language Models (LLMs) to assist expert counterspeech (CS) writing has gained interest, yet prior work has addressed these phenomena separately. We bridge this gap by studying CS generation in contexts where both hate and misinformation co-occur. We test three knowledge-driven generation strategies: first we prompt an LLM with fact-checkers' guidelines and fact-checking articles; secondly, with NGOs' guidelines and reports; thirdly, we create a mixed strategy that combines guidelines and documents from both. 23 experts revise the generated CS, which are assessed via human and automatic metrics. While LLMs produce adequate CS in 40% of cases, expert edits substantially improve naturalness, exhaustiveness, and adherence to guidelines. Based on the post-edited CS, the mixed strategy proves to be the most effective in crowdsourcing evaluation, pairing strong factual correction with stereotype mitigation and empathetic engagement. We release a dataset of hateful and misinformed claims with expert-verified CS and supporting knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to use LLMs, guided by external knowledge, to generate expert-quality counterspeech against online content that mixes hate speech and misinformation, and shows that combining fact-checking and NGO guidance plus expert post-editing yields the best results and a new dataset.
AI.motivation: Online hate speech and misinformation often appear together, reinforcing prejudice and polarization, but existing counterspeech and LLM research usually treats them separately. There is a practical need to support experts in crafting effective counterspeech at scale, particularly in complex scenarios where both factual correction and stereotype mitigation are needed.
AI.method: The authors define a counterspeech generation task for posts that contain both hate and misinformation. They design three prompting strategies for an LLM: (1) fact-checker-driven, using guidelines and fact-checking articles; (2) NGO-driven, using NGO guidelines and reports; and (3) a mixed strategy that combines both sources. The LLM generates counterspeech under these setups. Then, 23 domain experts revise (post-edit) the LLM outputs. Both original and edited counterspeech are evaluated using human judgments and automatic metrics on dimensions like naturalness, exhaustiveness, and adherence to guidelines. They also perform crowdsourced evaluation to compare the three strategies.
AI.result: LLMs, when prompted with these knowledge sources, produce adequate counterspeech in about 40% of the test cases without editing. However, expert post-editing substantially improves key quality aspects, including naturalness, exhaustiveness, and adherence to the provided guidelines. Among the prompting strategies, the mixed strategy that combines fact-checking and NGO materials performs best after post-editing, particularly in balancing strong factual correction with reduction of stereotypes and empathetic engagement. The authors compile and release a dataset of hateful and misinformed claims, along with expert-verified counterspeech and the supporting knowledge used in generation.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge-grounded LLMs can assist but not replace experts in generating counterspeech against content that combines hate and misinformation; expert post-editing remains crucial for quality. A mixed prompting strategy that draws on both fact-checking and NGO resources yields the most effective counterspeech from a crowdworker perspective, offering a practical recipe for future systems. The released dataset enables further research on counterspeech generation in realistic, complex scenarios where hate and misinformation co-occur.

title: Skill Weaving: Efficient LLM Improvement via Modular Skillpacks
summary: Large language models increasingly require specialization across diverse domains, yet existing approaches struggle to balance multi-domain capacities with strict memory and inference constraints. In this work, we introduce SkillWeave, a modular improvement framework that enables LLMs to specialize under fixed memory budgets. SkillWeave partitions full capabilities of a general-purpose model into skillpacks -- lightweight, domain-specific delta modules -- that reorganize and refine the model's internal knowledge. For efficient deployment, SkillWeave integrates SkillZip to compress skillpacks into compact and inference-ready format, enabling strong multi-domain performance with low-latency execution. On multi-task and agentic benchmarks, a 9B SkillWeave model outperforms several baselines and even surpasses a 32B monolithic LLM, while achieving up to 4x speedup.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SkillWeave, a modular framework that splits a general LLM into small domain-specific modules (“skillpacks”) and compresses them for fast, memory-efficient multi-domain specialization, achieving better performance than larger monolithic models.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are used across many domains, it becomes hard to support strong multi-domain capabilities under tight memory and inference-time constraints. Existing methods either maintain one large monolithic model or many separate fine-tuned models, both of which are inefficient. There is a need for a way to specialize models to multiple domains while keeping deployment lightweight and fast.
AI.method: The authors introduce SkillWeave, which partitions a general-purpose LLM’s abilities into modular, domain-specific delta modules called skillpacks. These modules reorganize and refine internal knowledge for each domain instead of duplicating full models. They also propose SkillZip, a compression mechanism that packages these skillpacks into a compact, inference-ready form. The framework is evaluated on multi-task and agentic benchmarks using a 9B-parameter base model and compared against baselines, including a larger 32B monolithic model.
AI.result: With SkillWeave and compressed skillpacks via SkillZip, a 9B model achieves strong multi-domain and agentic performance that outperforms several baselines and even exceeds the performance of a 32B monolithic LLM, while providing up to 4× faster inference.
AI.conclusion: Modular specialization via lightweight, compressed skillpacks allows LLMs to deliver strong multi-domain performance under fixed memory and latency budgets. SkillWeave demonstrates that carefully designed modular delta modules can surpass much larger monolithic models in both speed and quality, offering a practical path for scalable, efficient LLM deployment across diverse domains.

title: Cohesion-6K: An Arabic Dataset for Analyzing Social Cohesion and Conflict in Online Discourse
summary: The study of online discourse has become central to understanding societal polarization. While much research has focused on detecting overt toxicity, the subtle dynamics of social cohesion, meaning the interaction between divisive and unifying narratives, remain computationally underexplored (Bail, 2021; Gonzalez-Bailon and Lelkes, 2023). This paper presents Cohesion-6K, a manually and ChatGPT-assisted annotated dataset of six thousand Arabic public Facebook posts related to the Israeli Occupation of Palestine. Each post is assigned to one of five discourse categories that represent a continuum from conflict to cohesion: Conflict, Resolution, Community Engagement, Supportive Interactions, and Shared Values. The annotation process combines expert human judgment with model-assisted pre-labeling verified by trained annotators, achieving substantial inter-annotator agreement (Cohens kappa = 0.85). Quantitative analysis reveals a consistent engagement gap, where conflict-oriented posts receive between two and four times more user interaction than resolution-oriented ones (p < 0.01). This pattern illustrates how divisive discourse tends to attract disproportionate visibility in Arabic social media spaces. Cohesion-6K provides a transparent and reproducible resource for the study of online cohesion and polarization. The dataset, annotation guidelines, and preprocessing code will be released for research use under an open license, supporting future work in computational social science, digital communication, and Arabic natural language processing.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cohesion-6K, a 6,000-post Arabic Facebook dataset labeled along a five-level conflict–cohesion discourse continuum about the Israeli Occupation of Palestine, showing conflict posts get far more engagement than resolution-focused ones.
AI.motivation: Most computational work on online polarization targets overt toxicity, leaving the subtler balance between divisive and unifying narratives underexplored, especially in Arabic social media. There is a need for high-quality labeled data to study social cohesion and polarization beyond toxicity detection.
AI.method: The authors collect 6,000 Arabic public Facebook posts about the Israeli Occupation of Palestine and annotate each into one of five discourse categories ranging from conflict to cohesion: Conflict, Resolution, Community Engagement, Supportive Interactions, and Shared Values. Annotation combines expert human judgment with ChatGPT-assisted pre-labeling that is then verified and corrected by trained annotators. Inter-annotator agreement is measured using Cohen’s kappa, achieving 0.85. They then perform quantitative analyses of user engagement across discourse types.
AI.result: They produce the Cohesion-6K dataset with reliable labels (Cohen’s kappa = 0.85) for five discourse categories on a conflict–cohesion continuum. Analysis shows conflict-oriented posts receive 2–4 times more user interactions than resolution-oriented posts, a statistically significant difference (p < 0.01), indicating an engagement gap favoring divisive content in Arabic Facebook discourse on this topic.
AI.conclusion: Cohesion-6K is a transparent, reproducible resource that enables systematic study of online cohesion and polarization in Arabic social media. It empirically demonstrates that conflict-oriented discourse attains disproportionate visibility compared to cohesive, resolution-focused content. By releasing the dataset, guidelines, and code under an open license, the paper supports future research in computational social science, digital communication, and Arabic NLP focused on the nuanced dynamics of social cohesion and polarization.

title: CLORE: Content-Level Optimization for Reasoning Efficiency
summary: Reinforcement learning post-training has improved the reasoning ability of large language models, but often produces unnecessarily long, repetitive, or semantically opaque reasoning traces. Existing efficient reasoning methods mainly regulate response length through explicit budgets or length-aware rewards, leaving intermediate reasoning content weakly supervised. We propose CLORE, a content-level optimization framework that improves reasoning efficiency by editing correct on-policy rollouts. CLORE uses an external augmentation model to delete repetitive segments, illegible or task-irrelevant content, and superfluous reasoning after the solution is established, while preserving the final answer. The resulting augmented--original pairs are optimized with an auxiliary reference-free DPO objective alongside standard policy-gradient training. By restricting augmentation to correct trajectories and performing local deletion, CLORE keeps edited rollouts close to the policy distribution and mitigates off-policy mismatch. Experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and Qwen2.5-Math-7B across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that CLORE improves the accuracy--efficiency trade-off and remains compatible with GRPO, DAPO, Training Efficient, and ThinkPrune. Content-level analyses further show that CLORE reduces repetitive reasoning, illegible content, and post-answer exploration, supporting content-level supervision as a complementary direction to length-level control.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CLORE, a framework that edits and shortens correct reasoning traces of large language models to improve efficiency without harming accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement-learning post-training improves LLM reasoning but often yields long, repetitive, or opaque reasoning traces. Existing efficiency methods mainly control length with budgets or length-aware rewards, giving only weak supervision over the actual content of intermediate reasoning steps. The authors aim to more directly supervise and optimize the *content* of reasoning traces to make them concise and relevant while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.method: CLORE is a content-level optimization framework that works on correct on-policy rollouts (trajectories). An external augmentation model edits these rollouts by locally deleting: (1) repetitive segments, (2) illegible or task-irrelevant content, and (3) superfluous reasoning that occurs after the solution is already reached, while preserving the final answer. The model then learns from pairs of original vs. edited trajectories using an auxiliary reference-free DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) objective, combined with standard policy-gradient training. By restricting edits to correct trajectories and using only local deletions, CLORE keeps the edited traces close to the original policy distribution and reduces off-policy mismatch issues.
AI.result: On two base models (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and Qwen2.5-Math-7B) and five math reasoning benchmarks, CLORE improves the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency compared to baselines. It is also shown to be compatible with other efficient-reasoning techniques such as GRPO, DAPO, Training Efficient, and ThinkPrune. Content-level analyses indicate that CLORE significantly reduces repetitive reasoning, illegible segments, and post-answer exploration in the generated traces.
AI.conclusion: CLORE demonstrates that directly editing and supervising the *content* of reasoning traces—rather than only constraining their length—can yield more efficient and cleaner reasoning without sacrificing accuracy. The framework offers a complementary direction to length-level control, and its compatibility with multiple existing methods suggests it can be integrated into broader RL post-training pipelines to systematically refine reasoning behavior in LLMs.

title: From Correlation to Cause: A Five-Stage Methodology for Feature Analysis in Transformer Language Models
summary: We propose a five-stage methodology for causal feature analysis in transformer language models (probe design, feature extraction, causal validation, robustness testing, and deployment integration) and demonstrate it end-to-end on GPT-2 small performing the Indirect Object Identification (IOI) task. Activation patching recovers the canonical IOI circuit (layer-9 head 9 alone gives recovery +1.02). A sparse autoencoder recovers per-name selective features with effect sizes of 30 to 50 activation units. Causal validation finds these features specifically but only partially causal: ablating fifteen of them leaves the model accurate on 98% of prompts. Two NLA-inspired evaluations strengthen this picture: the fifteen selective features explain only 31% of activation variance versus the SAE's 99.7%, and selectivity ratio anticorrelates with causal force (r = -0.56). Robustness testing under three distribution shifts finds that the circuit transfers cleanly but feature ablation effects degrade substantially, exposing a gap between detection robustness and causal robustness. A cost-based deployment evaluation (assumed $50/FN, $0.42/FP, 2% error rate) finds an optimal monitor configuration yielding $8.96 per 1000 queries against a $1000 baseline, a 99.1% saving. Optimal composition strategy varies with cost ratio and base rate. The conjunction of stages produces findings no single stage would.

AI.tldr: A five-stage causal feature analysis pipeline is proposed and demonstrated on GPT-2 small for the IOI task, showing that circuits are robust but discovered features are only partially causal and fragile under distribution shift, yet still can enable highly cost-effective safety monitoring.
AI.motivation: Current interpretability work can identify circuits or features in transformer models, but it is unclear how to systematically connect these discoveries to actual causal influence on model behavior, robustness under distribution shift, and practical deployment decisions such as safety monitoring. There is a need for a structured, end-to-end methodology that goes from probing and feature discovery through causal testing, robustness analysis, and deployment-relevant evaluation.
AI.method: The paper defines a five-stage methodology: (1) probe design for a concrete capability (Indirect Object Identification in GPT-2 small), (2) feature extraction via tools like activation patching and sparse autoencoders, (3) causal validation through interventions/ablations on extracted features or heads, (4) robustness testing under multiple distribution shifts to compare circuit vs feature robustness, and (5) deployment integration via a cost-based evaluation of a monitoring system using these features. It uses activation patching to rediscover the IOI circuit, trains a sparse autoencoder to reveal per-name selective features, quantifies their causal impact via ablations, compares variance explained vs causal force, and performs NLA-inspired analyses of selectivity and causal strength. Finally, it runs a cost-benefit analysis of using these features in a monitoring configuration.
AI.result: Activation patching identifies a single attention head (layer-9 head 9) that nearly fully restores IOI performance (+1.02 recovery). The sparse autoencoder finds per-name selective features with large activation magnitudes (30–50 units). However, ablating 15 such selective features barely harms task performance (model still 98% accurate), and those features account for only 31% of activation variance compared to 99.7% captured by the SAE overall. Selectivity is negatively correlated with causal force (r = -0.56). Under three distribution shifts, the IOI circuit generalizes well, while ablation-based feature effects weaken, revealing that feature detection can be robust while causal influence is not. A deployment cost model with assumed error and cost parameters shows an optimal monitor yielding $8.96 expected cost per 1000 queries versus a $1000 no-monitor baseline, a 99.1% saving, with optimal strategy sensitive to cost ratios and error base rates.
AI.conclusion: An integrated five-stage pipeline for causal feature analysis yields a more nuanced understanding than any single technique alone: one can reliably detect circuits and highly selective features that explain most variance, yet these may be only weakly and non-robustly causal for behavior. Causal importance, variance explained, and feature selectivity can diverge substantially, and robustness under distribution shift is critical for trustworthy interpretability. Despite these limitations, carefully validated features can still be operationalized into highly cost-effective monitoring systems, but deployment strategies must be tuned to application-specific cost and prevalence parameters.

title: Towards a compositional semantics for quantitative confidence assessment in assurance arguments
comment: Accepted to the 21st European Dependable Computing Conference (EDCC 2026), Canterbury, UK
summary: Assurance arguments provide a clear and structured way to explain why stakeholders should trust that a system satisfies certain properties, yet widely used notations, e.g.Goal Structuring Notation (GSN), typically lack an operational semantics for deriving assurance confidence. Existing approaches address structure and soundness but largely reason over truth values, not over confidence in the justification of claims. Subjective Logic (SL) offers a calculus of belief, disbelief, and uncertainty with operators for combining opinions, enabling confidence propagation under incomplete, conflicting, or subjective evidence. However, existing SL-based approaches do not provide a uniform, compositional semantics that covers all argument elements and relations to enable overall confidence assessment. We propose a confidence semantics that represents argument elements as SL opinions and maps relations between elements to SL operators modelling how confidence flows, effectively turning the argument into an analyzable confidence network. The approach provides explicit warrants, principled handling of context, preserved provenance, and compatibility with GSN, along with practical guidance using an exemplary assurance confidence assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Subjective Logic–based, compositional confidence semantics for assurance arguments (e.g., in GSN), treating argument elements as opinions and relations as operators to enable rigorous, network-style confidence propagation.
AI.motivation: Although assurance arguments and notations like GSN help structure justifications that a system satisfies critical properties, they lack a formal operational semantics for quantifying and propagating confidence. Existing work mainly checks structural soundness or truth but does not systematically handle degrees of belief, uncertainty, and conflict across the entire argument. There is a need for a uniform, mathematically grounded way to assess overall assurance confidence from heterogeneous, incomplete, or subjective evidence.
AI.method: The authors use Subjective Logic (SL) as a foundation and define a confidence semantics where each assurance argument element is represented as an SL opinion (belief, disbelief, uncertainty). They systematically map the different relations between argument elements (e.g., refinement, evidence, context) to specific SL operators that model how confidence propagates through the structure.
