This yields a compositional, network-like model in which confidence can be computed and analyzed over the whole argument, including handling context and preserving provenance.
AI.result: The paper delivers a uniform, compositional semantics for assurance arguments by fully embedding them in an SL-based confidence network. It demonstrates how warrants and context are explicitly represented, how SL operators support principled combination of partial or conflicting evidence, and shows that the semantics is compatible with Goal Structuring Notation. An exemplary case study illustrates practical application to assurance confidence assessment.
AI.conclusion: By treating assurance arguments as networks of SL opinions connected via well-chosen operators, the proposed approach enables systematic, quantitative reasoning about confidence rather than just truth or structure. It enhances transparency (through explicit warrants and provenance), supports nuanced treatment of uncertainty and conflict, and integrates with existing notations like GSN, making confidence assessment more rigorous and operational in practice.

title: In Silico Modeling of the RAMPHO Buffer: Dissociating Informational and Energetic Masking via Phonetic Entropy in Deep Neural Networks
summary: The fundamental challenge of listening in multi-talker environments is a cognitive bottleneck, defined by the Ease of Language Understanding (ELU) model as a failure within the RAMPHO episodic buffer. Current deep neural networks for speech enhancement optimize purely for physical acoustics, failing to account for the cognitive penalty of informational masking. Here, we present an in silico simulation of the RAMPHO buffer using the frame-by-frame phonetic entropy of a self-supervised acoustic model (wav2vec 2.0). By contrasting a semantically intact distractor with a phase-decorrelated distractor (the Concentration Shield) across a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) sweep, we successfully dissociate the cognitive penalty of informational distraction from the physical penalty of energetic decay. The simulation reveals a cognitive-acoustic Pareto optimization problem: destroying a distractor's semantic payload provides a release from informational masking at high SNRs, but fundamentally degrades temporal glimpsing cues at low SNRs.

AI.tldr: They use phonetic entropy from a self-supervised model to simulate a cognitive buffer (RAMPHO) and show a trade-off: degrading a distractor’s semantics helps at high SNR but hurts temporal cues at low SNR.
AI.motivation: Listening in multi-talker settings is limited not only by acoustics (energetic masking) but also by cognitive factors (informational masking), as described by the ELU model via failures in the RAMPHO buffer. Existing neural speech enhancement focuses on signal quality and ignores this cognitive bottleneck. The authors want a way to quantitatively link acoustic manipulations and cognitive load in order to understand and optimize speech processing in realistic listening conditions.
AI.method: They build an in silico simulation of the ELU model’s RAMPHO episodic buffer using frame-by-frame phonetic entropy derived from wav2vec 2.0, a self-supervised acoustic model. They compare two types of background distractors across a range of SNRs: (1) a semantically intact competing talker and (2) a phase-decorrelated version ("Concentration Shield") that preserves energy but disrupts semantic content. By analyzing how phonetic entropy changes with SNR for these conditions, they infer the relative contributions of informational vs energetic masking.
AI.result: The simulation cleanly separates the cognitive cost of informational masking from the physical effects of energetic masking. When the distractor’s semantics are destroyed (phase-decorrelated), informational masking is reduced at high SNRs, suggesting a release from cognitive load. However, this manipulation also reduces temporal glimpsing cues at low SNRs, worsening performance when the target signal is weak. The outcome reveals distinct regimes where semantic disruption is beneficial vs detrimental.
AI.conclusion: There is an inherent cognitive-acoustic Pareto trade-off in multi-talker listening. Manipulations that reduce a distractor’s semantic intelligibility can lower informational masking at higher SNRs but simultaneously impair temporal glimpsing at lower SNRs. Therefore, speech enhancement systems should not optimize only for physical acoustics; they must also model and balance cognitive constraints, as approximated here via phonetic entropy in an ELU/RAMPHO-inspired framework.

title: SGR-Bench: Benchmarking Search Agents on State-Gated Retrieval
comment: Work in Progress. 23 pages, 7 figures, preprint
summary: Recent advances in large language models and tool-using agents have expanded the range of benchmarked web tasks. Yet an important class of specialized retrieval tasks remains undercharacterized. On many specialized data-retrieval websites, answer-bearing evidence becomes accessible only after establishing the correct site-specific retrieval state through filters, views, hierarchies, or scopes. We term this capability state-gated retrieval (SGR). We introduce SGR-Bench, a benchmark for this setting containing 100 expert-curated tasks spanning six source families and 12 public data ecosystems. Each task requires discovering the appropriate website and configuring its site-specific retrieval state to produce a structured answer. SGR-Bench pairs constraint-guided and goal-oriented formulations of the same underlying problems, enabling controlled comparisons between explicit and implicit guidance for state-gated retrieval. We evaluate eight CLI-based agentic LLM systems and three commercial search-agent products. On SGR-Bench, the strongest system reaches only 66.18% item-level F1, while row-level F1 remains much lower. A manual audit of 156 analyzable failed CLI trajectories shows why: agents often reach a relevant web source, but establish the wrong site-specific retrieval state. Retrieval-scope drift (37.2%) and criterion mismatch (27.6%) dominate, whereas final answer composition accounts for only 10.3%. The dataset and single-case evaluation instructions are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/PKUAIWeb/SGR-BENCH.

AI.tldr: Introduces SGR-Bench, a benchmark for testing how well LLM agents handle state-gated retrieval on specialized data websites, revealing current systems struggle mainly with configuring the right retrieval state.
AI.motivation: Existing web benchmarks largely focus on open search and simple navigation, but many real-world information tasks require interacting with specialized sites where relevant data only appears after configuring complex filters, scopes, or views. This specialized, state-dependent retrieval ability is important for practical agent use but is underexplored and under-measured, motivating a dedicated benchmark and analysis.
AI.method: Define and formalize the notion of state-gated retrieval (SGR), then construct SGR-Bench: 100 expert-designed tasks drawn from six website families and 12 public data ecosystems. Each task requires an agent to identify the correct site and configure its retrieval state (filters, hierarchies, scopes) to answer in structured form. The benchmark provides both constraint-guided and goal-oriented variants of each task. The authors evaluate eight CLI-based LLM-agent systems plus three commercial search-agent products, and manually audit 156 failed CLI trajectories to categorize error types.
AI.result: On SGR-Bench, the best-performing system achieves 66.18% item-level F1 and substantially lower row-level F1, indicating significant performance gaps. Error analysis of failed runs reveals most failures are not due to final answer formatting but earlier in the process: 37.2% from retrieval-scope drift, 27.6% from criterion mismatch, and only 10.3% from answer composition problems.
AI.conclusion: State-gated retrieval is a distinct and challenging capability for tool-using LLM agents, and existing systems fall short primarily in correctly configuring site-specific retrieval states on specialized websites. SGR-Bench provides a systematic way to evaluate and compare explicit versus implicit guidance for such tasks, and the released dataset and instructions support further research on more robust agentic retrieval strategies.

title: Polite on the Surface, Wrong in Practice: A Curated Dataset for Fixing Honorific Failures in Multilingual Bangla Generation
summary: Recent advances in Multilingual Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly enhanced cross-lingual conversational capabilities, yet modeling culturally nuanced and context-dependent communication remains a critical bottleneck. Specifically, existing state-of-the-art models exhibit a severe pragmatic gap when handling structural variations, regional idioms, and honorific consistencies in low-resource contexts like Bangla. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel, culturally aligned instruction-tuning dataset for \textbf{BangLa Application and DialoguE generation - BLADE} and benchmarking framework comprising $4,196$ meticulously curated interaction pairs. We leverage this resource to systematically fine-tune and evaluate leading open-weight architectures, including DeepSeek-8B and LLaMA-3.2-3B, utilizing parameter-efficient fine-tuning via LoRA adapters in a 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) quantization framework. Our empirical evaluations demonstrate that models fine-tuned on our dataset yield substantial improvements in structural fidelity and honorific alignment, providing a rigorous benchmark for bridging pragmatic disparities in low-resource multilingual text generation. Code and dataset: https://github.com/ashuvo25/Bangla_Application_LLM/tree/main

AI.tldr: They build BLADE, a culturally aligned Bangla instruction-tuning and dialogue dataset, and show that fine-tuning popular MLLMs on it greatly improves Bangla pragmatic and structural behavior.
AI.motivation: Multilingual LLMs are good at cross-lingual conversation but still fail on subtle, culturally grounded aspects of language use such as honorifics, idioms, and structural patterns, especially for low-resource languages like Bangla. There is a lack of high-quality, culturally aligned datasets and benchmarks to train and evaluate pragmatic competence in such settings.
AI.method: The authors construct BLADE, a Bangla Application and Dialogue generation dataset with 4,196 carefully curated interaction pairs emphasizing structural variation, idiomatic usage, and honorific consistency. They then fine-tune leading open-weight models (e.g., DeepSeek-8B, LLaMA-3.2-3B) using parameter-efficient LoRA adapters on 4-bit NF4 quantized models, and benchmark their performance on tasks measuring structural fidelity and pragmatic alignment.
AI.result: Fine-tuned models using BLADE show substantial gains in correctly handling Bangla structure (e.g., syntax, formality) and honorific usage compared with their base versions and existing state-of-the-art baselines, confirming that the dataset effectively narrows the pragmatic gap for Bangla in multilingual text generation.
AI.conclusion: A relatively small but carefully designed, culturally aware Bangla instruction-tuning and dialogue dataset can significantly improve the pragmatic and structural quality of MLLM outputs. BLADE serves both as a training resource and as a benchmark for future work on pragmatic, low-resource multilingual generation, helping bridge cultural and contextual gaps in LLM communication.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models as Live Strategic Agents: Provider Performance, Hybrid Decomposition, and Operational Gaps in Timed Risk Play
comment: 13 pages, 7 figures. Code and tracked notes: https://github.com/hcekne/risk-game . Public runtime artifact index: https://github.com/hcekne/risk-game/blob/main/docs/article-plans/public_experiment_artifacts.md
summary: Static benchmarks capture only part of how large language models behave in practice. Real systems place models inside repeated loops with time limits, formatting constraints, and failure modes. We study this setting in a timed multi-phase Risk environment with explicit victory targets and repeated planning and execution cycles. In a replicated 32-game cross-provider championship under frozen rules, gemini-3.1-pro-preview won 20 of 32 games against gpt-5.1, claude-opus-4-7, and kimi-k2.6, and the pooled winner distribution differs strongly from an equal-strength null (p approx 1.5 x 10^-5). We then separate planning from execution by standardizing execution on a cheaper Gemini Flash scaffold. Under this design, a pooled 32-game planner bakeoff is consistent with near-equality (p approx 0.821), which indicates that much of the earlier provider spread came from end-to-end system behavior rather than planning alone. To study mechanism, we analyze saved planning and execution traces from the provider championship. Gemini refers to the terminal objective far more often than the other models and increases that focus as victory approaches. Gemini also converts more turns into deep conquest chains, even though it is not the cleanest runtime. These results show that live-agent performance depends on objective tracking, execution conversion, cost, and runtime reliability, and they support evaluating LLMs as components in bounded workflows rather than as isolated benchmark respondents.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates large language models (LLMs) not just on static benchmarks but as live agents playing a timed, multi-phase Risk game with repeated planning and execution cycles, showing that system-level behavior (objective tracking, execution reliability, and cost/runtime tradeoffs) strongly affects performance beyond raw planning quality.
AI.motivation: Most LLM evaluations rely on static benchmarks that test single-shot or short-context abilities, which do not reflect how LLMs are used in real applications: embedded in iterative loops with time limits, tool calls, and failure modes. The authors want a more realistic way to compare LLMs as agents in complex, time-bounded decision processes and to understand what aspects of their behavior (planning vs execution, objective tracking, runtime reliability) actually drive performance differences between providers.
AI.method: They design a tournament-style environment based on the board game Risk, with explicit win conditions, multiple phases per turn, time limits, and repeated planning–execution cycles. Multiple commercial models from different providers play 32 cross-play games under fixed rules. In the first phase, each provider’s model plans and executes its own moves end-to-end. In a second phase, they factor out execution by standardizing it: all planners use the same cheaper Gemini Flash-based execution scaffold, isolating planning quality. They log and later analyze traces of both planning and execution, including references to the terminal objective, depth of conquest chains, and runtime behavior.
AI.result: In the 32-game cross-provider championship, gemini-3.1-pro-preview wins 20/32 games against gpt-5.1, claude-opus-4-7, and kimi-k2.6, a distribution very unlikely under an equal-strength assumption (p ≈ 1.5×10^-5). However, when execution is standardized with a common Gemini Flash scaffold and only planners differ, a pooled 32-game tournament yields results consistent with nearly equal planner strength (p ≈ 0.821). Trace analysis shows Gemini emphasizes the terminal victory objective more often than other models and increases this focus as it nears winning. It also converts more turns into deep conquest chains, despite not having the lowest latency or cleanest runtime.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end performance of LLM agents in live environments cannot be inferred reliably from static benchmarks or from planning quality alone. Differences between providers arise substantially from system-level factors: how consistently the model tracks the ultimate objective, how effectively it converts plans into multi-step execution, and how cost and runtime reliability interact with these behaviors. The authors argue that LLMs should be evaluated as components in bounded, iterative workflows (with timing and execution constraints) rather than as isolated respondents on static benchmarks.

title: BeLink: Biomedical Entity Linking Meets Generative Re-Ranking
summary: Despite recent progress, Biomedical Entity Linking (BEL) with large language models (LLMs) remains computationally inefficient and challenging to deploy in practical settings. In this work, we demonstrate that instruction-tuning of open-source generative models can offer an effective solution when applied at the re-ranking stage of the BEL pipeline. We propose a set-wise instruction-tuning formulation that enables fast and accurate candidate selection. Our method demonstrates strong performance on multiple BEL benchmarks, yielding significant improvements in linking accuracy (3%-24%) while reducing inference time compared to the state-of-the-art. We integrate our generative re-ranker into BeLink, a modular, end-to-end system designed for practical real-world BEL applications.

AI.tldr: They present a faster, more accurate biomedical entity linking system by using instruction-tuned open-source LLMs as a generative re-ranker within a BEL pipeline, integrated into a practical system called BeLink.
AI.motivation: Biomedical entity linking with LLMs is slow, computationally expensive, and hard to deploy in real-world applications, so there is a need for methods that improve both accuracy and efficiency using practical, open-source models.
AI.method: They use instruction-tuning on open-source generative language models in a set-wise re-ranking formulation: given candidate entities from an initial retrieval step, the tuned model jointly considers the set of candidates to select the best match, and this re-ranker is integrated into the modular BeLink BEL system.
AI.result: On multiple biomedical entity linking benchmarks, their generative re-ranker improves linking accuracy by about 3%–24% over existing methods while also reducing inference time relative to the state of the art.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-tuned, set-wise generative re-ranking with open-source LLMs is an effective and efficient approach for biomedical entity linking, and the resulting BeLink system is suitable for practical, real-world deployment.

title: Unlocking Proactivity in Task-Oriented Dialogue
summary: Proactive task-oriented dialogue (TOD), such as outbound sales, demands a persuasive agent that actively probes the user's concerns and steers the conversation toward acceptance within a bounded number of turns. Yet post-trained LLMs are inherently conservative, and reward-shaping RL (e.g., GRPO) struggles since it only re-weights what an already passive policy samples. We show that conditioning on the user's latent concerns unlocks proactive capability that no amount of sampling can undermine, establishing these concerns as a pivotal training-time signal. To operationalize this finding, we build the \textbf{Cognitive User Simulator}, which models each user as a stratified persona comprising observable external traits and hidden internal concerns. The simulator produces faithful and diverse interactions, while emitting per-turn state dynamics that track persuasion progress. We then introduce \textbf{Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization}, which converts the modeled concerns and the simulation state transition into complementary training objectives: (1) \emph{Asymmetric On-Policy Self-Distillation} that transfers concern-aware behavior from a privileged view of the same policy into its deployable, conversation-only view; and (2) \emph{State-Transition Policy Refinement} ...

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to make task-oriented dialogue agents more proactively persuasive by conditioning on users’ latent concerns and training with a cognitive user simulator plus asymmetric-view policy optimization.
AI.motivation: Post-trained large language models used in task-oriented dialogue are typically conservative and passive, which is problematic for proactive scenarios like outbound sales where the agent must actively probe, address user concerns, and drive toward acceptance in few turns. Standard reinforcement learning with reward shaping (e.g., GRPO) mainly reweights existing passive behaviors instead of teaching truly proactive strategies, limiting effectiveness. The authors identify that explicitly modeling and conditioning on users’ latent concerns during training can unlock proactive capabilities that sampling and reward shaping alone cannot achieve.
AI.method: The authors build a Cognitive User Simulator that represents each user as a layered persona with observable external traits and hidden internal concerns. This simulator generates diverse, realistic dialogues and outputs per-turn state information that measures persuasion progress. They then propose Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization, which uses simulator-provided internal states as privileged training signals. The framework has two main components: (1) Asymmetric On-Policy Self-Distillation, where a concern-aware policy with access to privileged user-state information teaches a deployable policy that only sees conversation history; and (2) State-Transition Policy Refinement, which uses the simulator’s modeled state transitions to further optimize the policy with respect to how actions move the underlying persuasion state.
AI.result: The abstract claims that conditioning on latent user concerns enables proactive dialogue behavior that cannot be obtained merely by changing sampling or simple reward shaping, implying improved persuasion and efficiency in proactive task-oriented dialogue. The Cognitive User Simulator is said to generate faithful, diverse interactions and provide high-quality state signals, which in turn support more effective policy training via the proposed asymmetric-view optimization framework. Specific quantitative results are not detailed in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Latent user concerns are a crucial training-time signal for proactive task-oriented dialogue agents. By modeling users cognitively and leveraging privileged internal state information through asymmetric self-distillation and state-transition-based refinement, the proposed method can train dialogue policies that are more proactive and persuasive while remaining deployable with only standard conversational inputs. This establishes a practical approach for overcoming the inherent conservativeness of post-trained LLMs in proactive dialogue settings.

title: Scene Abstraction for Lexical Semantics: Structured Representations of Situated Meaning
summary: Coffee and tea share many properties, yet they evoke strikingly different situations, atmospheres, and affective associations. These situated dimensions of word meaning are real and systematic, but they remain implicit in most computational representations of lexical meaning. We propose Scene Abstraction, a framework for constructing structured representations of the interpretive scenes that words participate in across usage contexts. Each scene consists of a Contextual Scene (Events, Entities, Setting) and an expression-centered Expression Profile (Engaged events, Generalizable properties, Evoked emotions), operationalized through few-shot prompting of a large language model. Our contributions are three-fold: (1) a structured representation framework for situated lexical meaning; (2) COCA-Scenes, a dataset of 520 usage instances across 26 keywords for distinct scene identification; and (3) empirical evidence from two experiments suggesting that scenes are reliably identifiable across human observers (82.4% accuracy, +11.8 pp over text-only embeddings) and that our scene profiles more closely align with human interpretation of words in context than ATOMIC-based alternatives (86.4% preference across three semantic dimensions).

AI.tldr: Introduces Scene Abstraction, a structured, LLM-based way to represent how words evoke situational scenes, and shows it aligns better with human judgments than standard embeddings or ATOMIC-style resources.
AI.motivation: Standard lexical meaning representations (e.g., static or contextual embeddings) largely miss how words evoke rich, situated scenes—events, entities, settings, and emotions (e.g., coffee vs. tea). These situational nuances are systematic and cognitively real but remain mostly implicit and unstructured in current computational models. The authors aim to make these dimensions explicit, structured, and usable for modeling meaning in context.
AI.method: They define a Scene Abstraction framework in which each word-in-context is represented as a structured ‘scene’ with two parts: (a) Contextual Scene capturing Events, Entities, and Setting; and (b) Expression Profile capturing Engaged events, Generalizable properties, and Evoked emotions. They operationalize this by using few-shot prompting of a large language model to extract these elements for specific usage instances. They also construct COCA-Scenes: 520 annotated scenes across 26 target keywords. Two experiments then evaluate: (1) how reliably humans can identify the correct scene compared with text-only embeddings, and (2) how well their scene profiles match human interpretations compared to ATOMIC-based representations, across three semantic dimensions.
AI.result: They create COCA-Scenes, a dataset of 520 usage instances for 26 keywords, each annotated with scene structures. In Experiment 1, humans identify scenes with 82.4% accuracy, which is an 11.8 percentage-point improvement over text-only embedding baselines for distinct scene identification, indicating that their structures capture meaning distinctions humans recognize. In Experiment 2, human evaluators prefer the proposed scene profiles over ATOMIC-based alternatives 86.4% of the time across three semantic dimensions, suggesting that Scene Abstraction better reflects human interpretation of words in context.
AI.conclusion: Scene Abstraction yields structured, interpretable representations of situated lexical meaning that go beyond what standard embeddings or ATOMIC-style resources capture. The framework shows that interpretive scenes associated with word usage are systematic and identifiable, and that explicitly modeling events, entities, settings, properties, and emotions leads to representations that align more closely with human judgments. This opens a path for richer, more cognitively plausible lexical semantics in computational models.

title: SciCore-Mol: Augmenting Large Language Models with Pluggable Molecular Cognition Modules
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are central to the one-for-all intelligent paradigm, but they face a fundamental challenge when dealing with heterogeneous scientific data such as molecules: the inherent gap between discrete linguistic symbols and topological molecular or continuous reaction data leads to significant information loss and semantic noise in text-based reasoning. We propose SciCore-Mol, a modular framework that bridges this gap through three deeply integrated pluggable cognitive modules: a topology-aware perception module, a latent diffusion-based molecular generation module, and a reaction-aware reasoning module. Each module is coupled to the LLM backbone through learned representation interfaces, enabling richer information exchange than is possible with text-only tool feedback. Our experiments on diverse chemical tasks demonstrate that SciCore-Mol achieves strong comprehensive performance across molecular understanding, generation, reaction prediction, and general chemistry knowledge, with an 8B-parameter open-source system that is competitive with and in several dimensions surpasses proprietary large models. This work provides a systematic blueprint for equipping LLMs with scientific expertise through decoupled, pluggable, and flexibly orchestrated modules, with direct implications for drug design, chemical synthesis, and broader scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: SciCore-Mol augments an 8B LLM with three tightly coupled molecular/reaction modules to overcome text-only limitations and achieve competitive performance on diverse chemistry tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with heterogeneous scientific data like molecular graphs and reaction conditions because mapping everything into text causes information loss and noisy reasoning. There is a need for a principled way to let LLMs natively perceive, reason about, and generate structures such as molecules and reactions without forcing them through purely linguistic channels.
AI.method: The authors design SciCore-Mol, a modular framework around an LLM backbone with three pluggable cognitive modules: (1) a topology-aware perception module that encodes molecular structures; (2) a latent diffusion-based molecular generation module for creating new molecules; and (3) a reaction-aware reasoning module for handling reaction-related data and predictions. Each module communicates with the LLM via learned representation interfaces instead of plain text, enabling richer, structured information flow. The modules are decoupled yet orchestrated to support multiple chemistry tasks.
AI.result: On a range of chemical benchmarks—molecular understanding, molecular generation, reaction prediction, and general chemistry knowledge—SciCore-Mol delivers strong, well-rounded performance. Despite having only 8B parameters and being open source, it matches or exceeds several proprietary large models on multiple dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLMs with specialized, pluggable scientific modules via learned representation interfaces can effectively close the gap between symbolic language and structured scientific data. SciCore-Mol serves as a blueprint for building scientific expert systems on top of LLMs and has practical implications for applications such as drug discovery, chemical synthesis planning, and broader automated scientific discovery.

title: One prompt is not enough: Instruction Sensitivity Undermines Embedding Model Evaluation
summary: Instruction embedding models have become common among state-of-the-art models, however are evaluated using a single prompt per task. The single-point evaluation ignores a main problem of the instruction-based approach namely: sensitivity to the phrasing of the instruction. We present an empirical study of prompt sensitivity across 6 embedding models, 11 datasets, and 15 task-specific prompts per dataset, a total of 990. We show that reported scores misrepresent the distribution of scores over plausible prompts. The default prompt can both systematically understate or overstate performance. Furthermore, we show that the leaderboard ranking is not robust to prompt selection: by choosing prompts favorably, any model in our study can be promoted to first place. Our findings suggest that single-prompt evaluation is insufficient for instruction-tuned embedding models and that benchmarks should incorporate prompt robustness, either by evaluating over multiple prompts or by reporting sensitivity alongside point estimates.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that current evaluation of instruction-based embedding models using a single prompt per task is unreliable because models are highly sensitive to prompt wording.
AI.motivation: Instruction-tuned embedding models are widely used and typically evaluated with only one fixed instruction per task. This overlooks a key weakness: their performance can vary substantially with different but reasonable phrasings of the same task, which threatens the validity of reported benchmark scores and leaderboards.
AI.method: The authors conduct an empirical study of prompt sensitivity across 6 different instruction embedding models, using 11 datasets. For each dataset, they create 15 plausible task-specific prompts, leading to 990 (model, dataset, prompt) combinations. They then measure performance for each combination, analyze the distribution of scores per model and task, and study how rankings across models change when different prompts are selected.
AI.result: They find that performance across prompts varies considerably: the standard single default prompt often misrepresents the underlying distribution of scores. Depending on that prompt, a model’s reported performance can be systematically inflated or deflated. They also show that leaderboard rankings are unstable: by selecting favorable prompts, any of the examined models can appear as the top-ranked model.
AI.conclusion: Single-prompt evaluation is inadequate for instruction-tuned embedding models because it hides substantial prompt sensitivity and yields unstable rankings. Benchmarks should explicitly incorporate prompt robustness, for example by averaging over multiple prompts per task or by reporting performance variability across prompts in addition to a point estimate.

title: Cross-domain benchmarks reveal when coordinated AI agents improve scientific inference from partial evidence
summary: Scientific evidence often spans instruments, databases, and disciplines, so no single source records the full phenomenon. This makes it difficult to determine when coordinated AI agents add value over simpler scientific workflows. We evaluate this question with a cross-domain benchmark spanning four scientific tasks: mapping molecular structure into musical representations, detecting historical paradigm shifts in science, identifying vector-borne disease emergence, and vetting transiting-exoplanet candidates. Each case uses a frozen evaluation panel, predefined scoring protocols, explicit baselines, ablations or null controls, and stated limitations. The results define three operating regimes. When different disciplines each capture only part of the phenomenon, cross-channel composites improve over single-channel baselines: climate-vector emergence reaches AUROC 0.944 and exoplanet vetting reaches AUROC 0.955. However, the exoplanet workflow is effectively tied with a strong combined-summary baseline, showing that decomposition does not always improve top-line performance. When one signal dominates, as in paradigm-shift detection, coordination mainly improves interpretation and traceability. For molecular sonification, the gain is representational rather than predictive. ScienceClaw x Infinite provides the auditable artifact and provenance layer for this evaluation. The benchmark therefore assigns value to coordination only when the corresponding performance, provenance, or representation claim is supported by explicit comparators.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark to test when coordinated AI agents actually help in scientific workflows across multiple domains, finding that coordination is only valuable when it demonstrably improves performance, provenance, or representation over clear baselines.
AI.motivation: Scientific questions often require integrating partial evidence spread across different instruments, databases, and disciplines, but it is unclear when complex, coordinated AI-agent workflows are actually better than simpler baselines. The authors want a rigorous way to measure if and when such coordination provides real scientific value rather than just added complexity.
AI.method: They build a cross-domain benchmark with four distinct scientific tasks: mapping molecular structures to musical representations (molecular sonification), detecting historical paradigm shifts in science, identifying vector-borne disease emergence from climate and related signals, and vetting transiting exoplanet candidates. For each task, they set up frozen evaluation panels, predefined scoring protocols, explicit baselines, ablations or null controls, and they document limitations. They use a system called ScienceClaw x Infinite as the artifact and provenance layer for evaluating coordinated AI-agent workflows against these baselines.
AI.result: They find three regimes of value for coordination. (1) When different data channels or disciplines each contain only part of the signal, coordinated cross-channel workflows outperform single-channel baselines—for example, AUROC 0.944 for climate–vector disease emergence and AUROC 0.955 for exoplanet vetting. (2) When a single signal dominates, as in paradigm-shift detection, coordination does not materially improve predictive metrics but helps with interpretability and traceability. (3) In molecular sonification, the main benefit is a richer representation rather than better prediction. They also show that in exoplanet vetting, coordination can be tied with a strong combined-summary baseline, indicating that decomposition is not inherently superior.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark shows that coordinated AI agents in science should only be credited when they provide measurable benefits compared to well-defined baselines—either improved predictive performance, better provenance and auditability, or more useful representations. It cautions that decomposing workflows and adding coordination does not automatically improve outcomes and that rigorous, comparator-based evaluation is necessary to justify their use.

title: SynAE: A Framework for Measuring the Quality of Synthetic Data for Tool-Calling Agent Evaluations
summary: Today, tool-calling agents are commonly evaluated or tested on static datasets of execution traces, including input commands, agent responses, and associated tool calls. However, internal production datasets are often insufficient or unusable for testing; for example, they may contain sensitive or proprietary data, or they may be too sparse to support comprehensive testing (especially pre-deployment). In these settings, practitioners are increasingly replacing or augmenting real datasets with synthetic ones for evaluation purposes. A key challenge is quantifying the relation between these synthetic datasets and the real data. We introduce SynAE, an evaluation framework for assessing how well synthetic benchmarks for multi-turn, tool-calling agents replicate and augment the characteristics of real data trajectories. SynAE assesses the validity, fidelity, and diversity of synthetic data across four metric categories: (i) task instructions and intermediate responses, (ii) tool calls, (iii) final outputs, and (iv) downstream evaluation. We evaluate SynAE using recent agent benchmarks and test common synthetic data failure modes via realistic and controlled generation schemes. SynAE detects fine-grained variations in data validity, fidelity and diversity, and shows that no single metric is sufficient to fully characterize synthetic data quality, motivating a multi-axis evaluation of synthetic data for agent testing. A demo of SynAE is available at https://synae-2026-synae-demo.static.hf.space/index.html, with code at https://github.com/wsqwsq/SynAE.

AI.tldr: SynAE is a framework to evaluate how well synthetic multi-turn tool-calling agent datasets match real-world trajectories, focusing on validity, fidelity, and diversity across instructions, tool calls, outputs, and downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Tool-using language agents are often evaluated on internal production traces, but these real datasets can be sensitive, proprietary, or too sparse, especially before deployment. Practitioners therefore turn to synthetic data for testing, yet it is unclear how representative and useful this synthetic data is compared to real trajectories. There is a need for a principled way to quantify how well synthetic benchmarks replicate and augment real data characteristics so that evaluations based on them are trustworthy.
AI.method: The authors propose SynAE, an evaluation framework with a set of metrics that compare synthetic and real agent trajectories along multiple axes. SynAE analyzes (i) task instructions and intermediate agent responses, (ii) tool-calling behavior, (iii) final outputs, and (iv) downstream evaluation performance. They design metrics for validity (are examples well-formed and executable?), fidelity (how closely do synthetic trajectories resemble real ones?), and diversity (do they cover a broad range of behaviors and tasks?). They then apply SynAE to existing agent benchmarks and to synthetic datasets generated under controlled schemes that introduce realistic failure modes, in order to test SynAE’s sensitivity and diagnostic power.
AI.result: Experiments show that SynAE can detect fine-grained changes in synthetic datasets, such as reduced realism in instructions, altered tool-call patterns, degraded output quality, or mode collapse that harms diversity. The framework reveals that different synthetic data pathologies manifest differently across its metric categories and axes. SynAE’s results also demonstrate that single metrics can be misleading: datasets that look good by one criterion can be clearly deficient by others, highlighting the complexity of synthetic data quality for agent evaluation.
AI.conclusion: SynAE provides a structured, multi-dimensional way to assess synthetic benchmarks for tool-using agents, enabling practitioners to gauge how well these datasets replicate and extend real data trajectories. The study concludes that no single metric is sufficient for evaluating synthetic agent data; instead, a multi-axis approach that jointly considers validity, fidelity, and diversity across instructions, tool use, outputs, and downstream effects is necessary. SynAE’s public demo and code aim to facilitate broader adoption and further research on robust evaluation of synthetic data for agent testing.

title: Evaluation of Pipelines for Data Integration into Knowledge Graphs
summary: Integrating new data into knowledge graphs (KG) typically involves different tasks that are executed within workflows or pipelines There are many possible pipelines for a specific integration problem but there is not yet a general approach to evaluate the overall quality and performance of such pipelines to be able to determine the best choices. We therefore propose a new benchmark KGI-Bench to evaluate integration pipelines that ingest different kinds of input data into an existing KG. We evaluate pipelines by analyzing their output, i.e., the updated KG, with the three complementary quality metrics coverage, correctness and consistency. We also provide benchmark datasets (seed KG, overlapping input data of three formats, reference KG as a ground truth) for the movie domain. To demonstrate the applicability and usefulness of the proposed benchmark, we comparatively evaluate 12 pipelines and analyze their behavior across different input data formats and design choices.

AI.tldr: The paper presents KGI-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating end-to-end pipelines that integrate heterogeneous data into existing knowledge graphs, using standardized datasets and three key quality metrics.
AI.motivation: There are many different possible workflows and pipelines for integrating new data into existing knowledge graphs, but currently no general, systematic way to compare and select among them based on overall quality and performance. Existing evaluations typically focus on individual components or tasks rather than the end-to-end effect on the resulting KG. This gap makes it difficult for practitioners to choose appropriate integration strategies and to understand trade-offs between different design options or input data formats.
AI.method: The authors design KGI-Bench, a benchmark framework for knowledge graph integration (KGI) pipelines. It defines (1) a standardized integration scenario in the movie domain with a seed KG, multiple overlapping input datasets in three different formats, and a reference KG serving as ground truth; and (2) an evaluation methodology that assesses pipelines based on the quality of the updated KG they produce. The evaluation is organized around three complementary metrics: coverage (how completely new information is integrated), correctness (accuracy of the integrated data vs. ground truth), and consistency (logical and structural coherence of the resulting KG). They then instantiate multiple concrete pipelines with varying design choices and input formats and run them through the benchmark.
AI.result: Using KGI-Bench, the authors comparatively evaluate 12 different integration pipelines that ingest heterogeneous movie-related input data into an existing KG. They obtain quantitative measurements for each pipeline according to coverage, correctness, and consistency, and observe differing behaviors and trade-offs depending on the chosen pipeline design and input data format. These results illustrate that pipelines perform differently across metrics and settings, and that no single configuration is uniformly best.
AI.conclusion: KGI-Bench provides a general, reusable way to evaluate and compare KG integration pipelines based on the actual quality of the updated knowledge graph, rather than isolated component metrics. By offering ready-to-use datasets and clearly defined quality measures, it enables systematic benchmarking, exposes trade-offs among coverage, correctness, and consistency, and helps practitioners and researchers select and optimize integration workflows. The empirical study with 12 pipelines demonstrates that the benchmark is applicable in practice and yields informative insights about pipeline behavior across different input formats and design choices.

title: LANG: Reinforcement Learning for Multilingual Reasoning with Language-Adaptive Hint Guidance
summary: Reinforcement learning has proven effective for enhancing multi-step reasoning in large language models (LLMs), yet its benefits have not fully translated to multilingual contexts. Existing methods struggle with a fundamental trade-off: prioritizing input-language consistency severely hampers reasoning quality, while prioritizing reasoning often leads to unintended language drift toward English. We address this challenge with LANG, a novel framework that leverages language-conditioned hints to guide exploration in non-English reasoning tasks. Our method incorporates two key mechanisms to prevent dependency on these hints: a progressive decay schedule that gradually withdraws scaffolding, and a language-adaptive switch that tailors learning horizons to specific language difficulties. Empirical results on challenging multilingual mathematical benchmarks reveal that LANG substantially enhances reasoning performance without compromising language consistency. Moreover, we show that our framework generalizes beyond mathematics, fostering more consistent language alignment across model layers

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LANG, a reinforcement learning framework that improves multilingual reasoning in LLMs while keeping responses in the input language.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning improves multi-step reasoning in LLMs, but current approaches in multilingual settings either preserve the input language at the cost of reasoning quality or improve reasoning but cause models to drift into English. The authors aim to remove this trade-off so that LLMs can reason well in many languages while staying faithful to the user’s language.
AI.method: They propose LANG, which uses language-conditioned hints during RL to guide exploration for non-English reasoning tasks. To avoid the model becoming dependent on these hints, LANG includes: (1) a progressive decay schedule that gradually reduces and removes the hints (scaffolding), and (2) a language-adaptive switch that adjusts the effective learning horizon based on the difficulty of each language, so training is tailored per language.
AI.result: On difficult multilingual math benchmarks, LANG significantly improves reasoning performance while preserving alignment with the input language (i.e., reducing language drift to English). The framework also works beyond math tasks, improving language consistency across intermediate model layers for a broader set of tasks.
AI.conclusion: By conditioning RL exploration on language-specific hints and then systematically phasing them out with language-aware training controls, LANG overcomes the trade-off between reasoning quality and language consistency. It enables LLMs to perform strong multi-step reasoning in multiple languages without drifting to English and appears broadly applicable beyond mathematics.

title: Meta-Soft: Leveraging Composable Meta-Tokens for Context-Preserving KV Cache Compression
summary: The KV cache used in large language models has linearly growing time complexity, so LLMs face memory blow-up and reduced decoding efficiency when they process long contexts.Current KV Cache eviction has become an important research direction; however, existing methods based on fixed Soft Tokens (e.g., Judge Q) rely on a static parameter set as the query to evaluate the importance of KV pairs, so they cannot adapt dynamically to different input prompts, and they cannot precisely capture complex and changing task relevance.Also, evicted KV pairs are discarded permanently, so this causes irreversible information loss and context breaks. To address this problem, we propose Meta-Soft, a dynamic compression framework based on probe-driven context integration. Specifically, we build a meta-library with a learnable orthogonal basis matrix $\mathcal{L}$, and we use a selector network with Gumbel-Softmax to produce differentiable sparse combination weights, so we dynamically synthesize the most targeted $k$ Soft Tokens from the input prompt features.We append these Soft Tokens to the end of the input sequence to probe key information. We also introduce an attention-flow based integration mechanism, which redistributes the semantic information of removed tokens into retained tokens, and this keeps the dropped context information effectively.Experiments on multiple datasets show that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art eviction methods and provides a new solution for KV Cache compression.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Meta-Soft, a dynamic KV-cache compression and eviction framework that uses learned soft tokens and attention-flow based integration to reduce memory and computation while preserving task-relevant information in long-context LLM decoding.
AI.motivation: KV caches in large language models grow linearly with sequence length, causing memory blow-up and slower decoding for long contexts. Existing KV eviction methods typically rely on a fixed set of soft tokens or static parameters to score and discard KV pairs. These static designs cannot adapt to different prompts or tasks and tend to lose important, context-dependent information irreversibly when tokens are evicted. The paper aims to design an adaptive, task-aware KV compression method that both selects more relevant information and mitigates information loss when evicting tokens.
AI.method: The authors introduce Meta-Soft, a dynamic compression framework built around probe-driven context integration. They construct a meta-library, represented as a learnable orthogonal basis matrix L, that serves as a set of basis vectors for synthesizing soft tokens. A selector network, trained with Gumbel-Softmax, generates differentiable sparse combination weights over this basis, allowing the model to dynamically compose the top-k soft tokens best suited to the current input prompt. These synthesized soft tokens are appended to the end of the input sequence, acting as probes to capture salient contextual information. In parallel, they design an attention-flow based integration mechanism that, instead of simply discarding evicted KV pairs, redistributes their semantic information into the retained tokens using attention patterns, attempting to preserve the dropped context within the remaining cache. The whole framework is trained and evaluated as a KV-cache eviction and compression module for LLMs.
AI.result: On multiple benchmark datasets, Meta-Soft achieves better performance than existing state-of-the-art KV cache eviction and compression methods. It improves the trade-off between memory/computation savings and task accuracy, demonstrating that dynamic, prompt-adaptive soft token synthesis and attention-flow based integration can more effectively preserve important contextual information under tight KV cache budgets.
AI.conclusion: Meta-Soft shows that a dynamic, probe-driven approach to KV cache compression can outperform fixed soft-token eviction strategies for long-context LLMs. By learning a shared meta-library and using a selector network to synthesize task-specific soft tokens, the method can adaptively focus on the most relevant information for each prompt. The attention-flow based integration further reduces information loss by folding the content of removed tokens into those that remain. This provides a promising new direction for scalable, memory-efficient long-context inference in large language models via smarter KV cache management.

title: Beyond Temperature: Hyperfitting as a Late-Stage Geometric Expansion
summary: Recent work has identified a counterintuitive phenomenon termed "Hyperfitting", where fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) to near-zero training loss on small datasets surprisingly enhances open-ended generation quality and mitigates repetition in greedy decoding. While effective, the underlying mechanism remains poorly understood, with the extremely low-entropy output distributions suggesting a potential equivalence to simple temperature scaling. In this work, we demonstrate that this phenomenon is fundamentally distinct from distribution sharpening; entropy-matched control experiments reveal that temperature scaling fails to replicate the diversity gains of hyperfitting. Furthermore, we falsify the hypothesis of static vocabulary reweighting, showing through ablation studies that hyperfitting relies on a dynamic, context-dependent rank reordering mechanism. Layer-wise analysis localizes this effect to a "Terminal Expansion" in the final transformer block, where a substantial geometric expansion of the feature space (Delta Dim approx +80.8) facilitates the promotion of deep-tail tokens. Additionally, we introduce Late-Stage LoRA, a targeted fine-tuning strategy that updates only the final 5 layers, yielding robust generation with minimal parameter updates

AI.tldr: The paper investigates “Hyperfitting,” where driving training loss near zero on small datasets unexpectedly improves LLM generation quality and reduces repetition, and shows it is mechanistically distinct from simple temperature scaling or static vocabulary reweighting, arising instead from a dynamic, context-dependent reshaping of the final-layer feature space and logits.
AI.motivation: Although prior work showed that aggressively fine-tuning LLMs on small datasets to near-zero loss can improve open-ended text generation and mitigate repetition under greedy decoding, the mechanism behind this effect was unclear. Because hyperfitted models exhibit very low-entropy output distributions, it was tempting to attribute the effect to something trivial, like temperature scaling or static logit adjustment. The authors are motivated to understand whether hyperfitting is genuinely a new phenomenon, what structural changes in the model cause it, and how to harness it efficiently with minimal parameter updates.
AI.method: The authors compare hyperfitted models with temperature-scaled baselines under entropy-matched conditions to test whether hyperfitting is just distribution sharpening. They also run ablation studies to test the hypothesis that hyperfitting can be explained as a static vocabulary reweighting, examining whether fixed, context-independent logit adjustments reproduce the behavior. To pinpoint where in the network the effect arises, they perform layer-wise analysis of the representation geometry, looking for changes in dimensionality and token rank ordering across layers. Finally, they propose and evaluate Late-Stage LoRA, a fine-tuning scheme that applies low-rank adaptations only to the last five transformer layers, and assess its impact on generation robustness and parameter efficiency.
AI.result: Entropy-controlled comparisons show that temperature scaling does not recover the increased diversity and reduced repetition observed with hyperfitting, so the effect is not just sharper distributions. Ablation experiments falsify the static vocabulary reweighting hypothesis: the key behavior instead comes from a dynamic, context-dependent reordering of token ranks in the output distribution. Layer-wise geometric analysis locates a pronounced “Terminal Expansion” in the final transformer block during hyperfitting, characterized by a substantial increase in effective feature-space dimensionality (Delta Dim ≈ +80.8) that boosts the probabilities of deep-tail tokens. The Late-Stage LoRA strategy, which updates only the last five layers, successfully reproduces robust, non-repetitive generation while modifying a relatively small fraction of the model’s parameters.
AI.conclusion: Hyperfitting is a distinct phenomenon from simple temperature scaling or static, global logit reweighting; it emerges from dynamic, context-dependent reshaping of the final-layer representation space and output token rankings. The final transformer block undergoes a significant geometric “Terminal Expansion” that enhances the presence of rare, deep-tail tokens, thereby improving diversity and mitigating repetition in greedy decoding. Practically, this understanding enables targeted fine-tuning strategies such as Late-Stage LoRA, which achieve most of the benefits of hyperfitting while updating only a small set of late layers, offering a parameter-efficient way to enhance LLM generation quality.

title: Scaling Observation-aware Planning in Uncertain Domains
summary: Deciding which sensing capabilities to deploy on an agent in uncertain domains is a fundamental engineering challenge, in which one balances task achievability against the high costs of hardware and processing. This problem has previously been formalized as the Optimal Observability Problem (OOP), based on the well-known Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) model for decision-making. This work studies (sub-)symbolic techniques to scale solving of decidable fragments of the OOP, namely the Sensor Selection Problem (SSP) and the Positional Observability Problem (POP). Besides improving the original approach based on parameter synthesis, we develop a new solving method that identifies sensible observation functions via decomposition of POMDPs, improving performance by 3 and 5 orders of magnitude for instance size and runtime, respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles how to choose sensors for agents in uncertain environments efficiently, improving scalability of solving the Optimal Observability Problem by new (sub-)symbolic methods.
AI.motivation: In uncertain domains, agents require sensors to perceive the environment, but sensing hardware and processing are expensive. Engineers must balance having enough observability to achieve tasks against these costs. Existing formalization as the Optimal Observability Problem based on POMDPs is hard to solve at scale. There is a need for techniques that make deciding which sensing capabilities to deploy computationally tractable on larger instances.
AI.method: The authors focus on decidable fragments of the OOP: the Sensor Selection Problem (SSP) and the Positional Observability Problem (POP). They enhance a previous parameter synthesis-based approach and introduce a new solving method. This new method decomposes POMDPs and searches for good observation functions using (sub-)symbolic techniques. Decomposition and more efficient representation allow them to scale better in both state space and observation space.
AI.result: The new decomposition-based solving method can handle significantly larger instances of SSP and POP than prior work. It improves instance size scalability by about 3 orders of magnitude and runtime performance by about 5 orders of magnitude over the original parameter synthesis-based approach, while still identifying sensible observation functions for agents.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging decomposition and (sub-)symbolic techniques, the paper makes concrete progress on scaling the Optimal Observability Problem for POMDP-based agents. The proposed methods enable much more efficient sensor selection and observability design, which can guide engineering choices about sensing capabilities in uncertain domains. This demonstrates that careful structural exploitation of POMDPs can dramatically improve solvability of observability design problems.

title: Agentic CLEAR: Automating Multi-Level Evaluation of LLM Agents
summary: Agentic systems are becoming more capable: agents define strategies, take actions, and interact with different environments. This autonomy poses serious challenges for overseeing and assessing agent behavior. Most current tools are limited, focusing on observability with basic evaluation capabilities or imposing static, hand-crafted error taxonomies that cannot adapt to new domains. To address this gap, we present Agentic CLEAR, an automatic, dynamic, and easy-to-use evaluation framework. It produces textual insights into the agent behavior on three levels of granularity: system, trace, and node. Agentic CLEAR operates above the observability layer, enabling seamless integration and featuring an intuitive UI that makes agent evaluation highly accessible. In our experiments on four benchmarks, seven agentic settings, and tens of thousands of LLM calls, we show that Agentic CLEAR produces high-quality, data-driven, insightful feedback. Our analysis shows strong alignment with human-annotated errors and the ability to predict task success rate.

AI.tldr: Agentic CLEAR is an automatic, dynamic evaluation framework that provides multi-level textual analysis of autonomous agent behavior and aligns well with human judgments while predicting task success.
AI.motivation: Existing tools for evaluating increasingly autonomous agentic systems are inadequate: they mostly provide raw observability with limited evaluation or rely on rigid, hand-crafted error taxonomies that do not generalize to new domains. There is a need for a flexible, data-driven, and accessible evaluation framework that can interpret and assess agent behavior across diverse environments.
AI.method: The authors design Agentic CLEAR, an evaluation framework that sits above the observability layer of agent systems. It automatically generates textual feedback about agent behavior at three granularities—system-level, trace-level, and node-level. The framework integrates with different agent setups via a simple interface and offers an intuitive UI to facilitate use. They test the framework across four benchmarks, seven agentic configurations, and tens of thousands of LLM calls, and compare its feedback and error detection with human annotations and task outcomes.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks and agent settings, Agentic CLEAR generates high-quality, data-driven evaluations of agent behavior. Its error identification and feedback strongly correlate with human-annotated errors, and it can effectively predict task success rates from observed behaviors. The experiments validate its robustness and utility at scale over tens of thousands of LLM calls.
AI.conclusion: Agentic CLEAR addresses key limitations of previous agent evaluation tools by providing an automatic, adaptive, and user-friendly framework that generates multi-level textual insights into agent behavior. Its strong alignment with human judgments and predictive power for task success demonstrate its effectiveness as a practical tool for understanding and improving agentic systems across domains.

title: Epicure: Navigating the Emergent Geometry of Food Ingredient Embeddings
summary: We present Epicure, a family of three sibling skip-gram ingredient embeddings retrained from scratch on a multilingual recipe corpus. We aggregate 4.14M recipes from 11 sources spanning seven languages, English, Chinese, Russian, Vietnamese, Spanish, Turkish, Indonesian, German, and Indian-English, and normalise the raw ingredient strings to 1,790 canonical entries via an LLM-augmented pipeline. A 203,508-edge ingredient-ingredient NPMI graph and an 80,019-edge typed FlavorDB ingredient-compound graph, 2,247 typed compound nodes across 15 categories, seed three Metapath2Vec variants that share architecture and hyperparameters and differ only in the random-walk schema: Cooc walks the co-occurrence graph only, Chem walks the typed compound metapaths only, and Core blends both via injected ingredient-ingredient walks at controlled mixing, placing each model at a distinct point on the chemistry-vs-recipe-context spectrum.

AI.tldr: Epicure introduces three related ingredient embedding models trained on a large, multilingual recipe corpus using different graph-walk strategies to balance food chemistry and recipe co-occurrence information.
AI.motivation: To create robust, multilingual ingredient embeddings that capture both how ingredients co-occur in real recipes and how they are related through underlying chemical flavor compounds, enabling richer computational food applications than text-only or chemistry-only approaches.
AI.method: The authors compile and normalize a large corpus of 4.14M recipes across seven languages into 1,790 canonical ingredients using an LLM-assisted pipeline. They construct two graphs: (1) an ingredient–ingredient co-occurrence graph weighted by NPMI, and (2) a typed ingredient–compound graph based on FlavorDB with 2,247 compound nodes in 15 categories. They then apply three Metapath2Vec-based skip-gram models that share architecture and hyperparameters but differ in random-walk patterns: Cooc uses only the co-occurrence graph, Chem uses only typed chemistry metapaths, and Core mixes ingredient–ingredient walks into the chemistry graph at controlled ratios, yielding embeddings along a spectrum from purely contextual to more chemically grounded.
AI.result: They obtain three sibling embedding spaces (Cooc, Chem, Core) for the same set of canonical ingredients, each reflecting different balances of recipe co-occurrence vs. chemical similarity, built from a 203,508-edge co-occurrence graph and an 80,019-edge typed chemistry graph derived from a large multilingual corpus and FlavorDB.
AI.conclusion: Epicure provides a structured family of ingredient embeddings anchored in both large-scale multilingual recipe usage and formal flavor chemistry relations, with controllable emphasis via walk schemas, enabling downstream food computing tasks to choose or compare representations depending on whether contextual or chemical similarity is more important.

title: Chinese sensorimotor and embodiment norms for 3,000 lexicalized concepts
summary: Understanding how conceptual knowledge is grounded in bodily experience, and to what extent machine systems can acquire such knowledge without direct sensorimotor experience, are central questions in both cognitive science and embodied artificial intelligence research. Large-scale normative resources are essential for investigating these questions empirically, yet such resources remain sparse for non-Indo-European languages. We present a novel normative database for 3,000 lexicalized concepts in Mandarin Chinese, comprising 11-dimensional sensorimotor ratings and unidimensional embodiment ratings collected from 378 native Mandarin speakers. The ratings demonstrate high reliability and strong cross-norm validity with existing Chinese resources, each of which covers fewer words and a subset of the 11 sensorimotor dimensions. In a validation study, we tested new variables derived from a theoretically motivated metric, Perceptual Strength of Embodiment (PSE) (Huang et al., 2025), together with seven common composite variables, on lexical decision tasks. The results suggest that PSE-Sensorimotor and Minkowski-3 are the strongest composite predictors of lexical decision performance, capturing the facilitatory effects of sensorimotor information on lexical processing. A further exploratory study showed that sensorimotor ratings are substantially recoverable from purely linguistic representations using simple regression models (mean Spearman r = .62 across dimensions), though recovery varied markedly: visual and auditory dimensions yielded higher correspondence than chemosensory ones. Representational similarity analysis further showed that the relational geometry of the sensorimotor space is also partially recoverable (r = .540), consistent with the view that distributional language use encodes aspects of embodied conceptual structure.

AI.tldr: They build a big Mandarin Chinese sensorimotor/embodiment ratings database, validate it behaviorally, and test how well language models can recover these ratings from text-only representations.
AI.motivation: Embodied cognition research needs large, reliable sensorimotor norms to study how concepts are grounded in bodily experience. Existing resources are mostly for Indo-European languages and often small or low-dimensional, especially for Mandarin. There is also an open question about whether purely linguistic (distributional) information can approximate embodied, sensorimotor aspects of meaning.
AI.method:
1) Construct a database: collect 11-dimensional sensorimotor strength ratings plus a single embodiment rating for 3,000 Mandarin lexicalized concepts from 378 native speakers; assess reliability and compare with prior Chinese norms.
2) Derive composite variables from these dimensions, including a new theoretically motivated metric, Perceptual Strength of Embodiment (PSE), plus seven standard composite indices.
3) Run lexical decision experiments and use regression/psycholinguistic modeling to test which composite variables best predict response times/accuracy.
4) Train simple regression models from text-based linguistic representations (e.g., distributional word embeddings) to predict sensorimotor ratings and compute correlations.
5) Use representational similarity analysis (RSA) to compare the geometry of the empirical sensorimotor rating space with that induced by linguistic representations.
AI.result:
- Produced a high-reliability Mandarin database of 3,000 concepts with 11 sensorimotor dimensions and embodiment ratings; ratings show strong cross-norm validity with previous, smaller Chinese datasets.
- In lexical decision, the PSE-Sensorimotor metric and a Minkowski-3 composite emerge as the strongest predictors of performance, indicating robust facilitatory effects of sensorimotor richness on word recognition.
- Simple regression models recover sensorimotor ratings from linguistic representations with mean Spearman r ≈ .62, with better prediction for visual and auditory dimensions, poorer for chemosensory dimensions.
- RSA shows that the overall relational structure of the sensorimotor space is partially recoverable from linguistic data (r ≈ .54).
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a large, validated Mandarin sensorimotor/embodiment norms database and shows that theoretically grounded composites (PSE-Sensorimotor, Minkowski-3) best explain lexical processing effects. It further demonstrates that text-only linguistic distributions encode a substantial, though incomplete, amount of embodied sensorimotor structure, especially for visual and auditory aspects, supporting a nuanced view in which language use partially reflects embodied conceptual organization while not fully replacing direct sensorimotor grounding.

title: S2ED: From Story to Executable Descriptions for Consistency-Aware Story Illustration
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Accepted by IEEE ICME 2026
summary: Multi-frame story illustration requires long-horizon coherence beyond single-image text-to-image generation, including narrative decomposition and persistent character identity, layout, and affect across frames. We propose Story-to-Executable Descriptions (S2ED), a training-free, model-agnostic, prompt-layer framework that converts a full story into a sequence of explicit, editable executable descriptions for more consistent rendering. S2ED coordinates three agents to segment the narrative, ground canonical character attributes, and enrich spatial and affective cues, enabling interpretable prompt-carried state propagation and local edits to repair drift without retraining the generator. Experiments on Flintstones and Shakoo Maku show that S2ED improves sequence-level consistency and character fidelity over strong prompting, large-model planning, and a reference training-based method, under both automatic metrics and human judgments. We also deploy S2ED in an end-to-end story-to-storybook system for children's illustrated stories, with a supplementary video.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces S2ED, a prompt-layer framework that turns long stories into structured, executable prompts to generate multi-frame illustrations with consistent characters and layouts, without retraining image models.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-image systems struggle with long-horizon narrative coherence in multi-frame story illustration, especially maintaining consistent characters, layouts, and emotions across many images. The authors want a way to get coherent, editable visual stories from standard image generators without expensive model retraining or custom architectures.
AI.method: They design S2ED, a training-free, model-agnostic framework that works at the prompt level. S2ED uses three coordinated agents: (1) a narrative segmentation agent that breaks a full story into frame-level units; (2) a character grounding agent that establishes canonical character attributes to ensure persistent identity; and (3) a spatial/affective enrichment agent that inserts explicit layout and emotional cues. These agents output a sequence of structured, executable descriptions (prompts) whose state can be propagated across frames and locally edited to correct drift, all while using an underlying off-the-shelf image generator.
AI.result: On the Flintstones and Shakoo Maku datasets, S2ED achieves higher sequence-level visual consistency and character fidelity than strong prompting baselines, large-model planning approaches, and a reference training-based method, as measured by automatic metrics and human evaluations. It also demonstrates practical viability in a deployed end-to-end system that converts children’s stories into illustrated storybooks.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-level structuring of stories into executable descriptions, as done by S2ED, is an effective alternative to retraining image models for multi-frame narrative illustration. By coordinating specialized agents to manage narrative segmentation, character identity, and spatial/affective details, S2ED yields more coherent and editable story illustrations and can be integrated into real applications such as storybook generation.

title: More Context, Larger Models, or Moral Knowledge? A Systematic Study of Schwartz Value Detection in Political Texts
summary: Detecting Schwartz values in political text is difficult because implicit cues often depend on surrounding arguments and fine-grained distinctions between neighboring values. We study when context and explicit moral knowledge help sentence-level value detection. Using the ValuesML/Touch{é} ValueEval format, we compare sentence, window, and full-document inputs; no-RAG and retrieval-augmented settings with a curated moral knowledge base; supervised DeBERTa-v3-base/large encoders; and zero-shot LLMs from 12B to 123B parameters. The results show that more context is not uniformly better: full-document context improves supervised DeBERTa encoders by 3.8--4.8 macro-F1 points over sentence-only input, but does not consistently help zero-shot LLMs. Retrieved moral knowledge is more consistently useful in matched comparisons, improving each tested model family and context condition under early fusion. However, scaling from DeBERTa-v3-base to large and from 12B to larger LLMs does not guarantee gains, and simple early fusion outperforms the tested late-fusion and cross-attention RAG variants for encoders. Per-value analyses show that context and retrieval help most for socially situated or conceptually confusable values. These findings suggest that value-sensitive NLP should evaluate context, knowledge, and model family jointly rather than treating longer inputs or larger models as universal improvements.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how contextual text and external moral knowledge affect automatic detection of Schwartz moral values in political text.
AI.motivation: Schwartz value detection in political texts is challenging because cues are often implicit and depend on nuanced context, including surrounding arguments and subtle differences between related values. Existing NLP work may over-assume that more context or larger models always help, without systematically comparing context ranges and the use of explicit moral knowledge. The authors aim to understand when and how additional textual context and retrieval of moral knowledge improve sentence-level value classification, helping to design better value-sensitive NLP systems.
AI.method: The authors adopt the ValuesML/Touché ValueEval framework for sentence-level value detection and systematically vary three factors: (1) the amount of textual context around a target sentence (sentence-only, local window, and full document); (2) the use or non-use of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with a curated moral knowledge base; and (3) the model family and scale. They fine-tune supervised DeBERTa-v3-base and DeBERTa-v3-large encoders on the task, and test zero-shot large language models ranging from 12B to 123B parameters. They compare no-RAG baselines to RAG settings using different fusion strategies (early fusion, late fusion, and cross-attention variants), and assess macro-F1 overall and per value category to identify where context and retrieval help most.
AI.result: Full-document context yields notable improvements for supervised DeBERTa encoders, providing 3.8–4.8 macro-F1 gains over sentence-only inputs. However, zero-shot LLMs do not consistently benefit from more context, showing that longer inputs are not uniformly advantageous. Incorporating retrieved moral knowledge is more robustly beneficial across models: under early fusion, RAG improves each tested model family and context condition. Nonetheless, increasing model size—both from DeBERTa-v3-base to large and from 12B to bigger LLMs—does not reliably improve performance. Among fusion strategies for encoders, simple early fusion of retrieved knowledge outperforms the examined late-fusion and cross-attention RAG variants. Per-value analysis reveals that context and retrieval particularly help for values that are socially situated or easily confused conceptually with neighboring values.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that successful value detection in political text depends on the interplay of context length, explicit moral knowledge, and model architecture/scale. More context or larger models are not automatic improvements; instead, carefully selected context windows and targeted retrieval of moral knowledge, especially via early fusion, can yield more reliable gains. The findings recommend that value-sensitive NLP research jointly evaluate context, knowledge integration, and model family, rather than assuming that full-document input or model scaling alone will optimize value detection performance.

title: KAPPS: A knowledge-based CPPS Architecture for the Circular Factory
comment: Submitted to Journal of Manufacturing Systems (JMS)
