summary: While linear manufacturing relies on homogeneous materials and predefined process sequences, circular manufacturing reintroduces used products with heterogeneous and uncertain conditions. This shift demands manufacturing systems capable of handling variable product states, dynamically reconfigurable processes, and the integration of human and machine knowledge. Conventional manufacturing IT architectures, designed for stable structures and deterministic execution, are unable to meet these requirements, as they cannot adequately represent and manage the uniqueness of individual components at runtime. Following a design science methodology for developing a Cyber Physical Production System for circular manufacturing, we derive 14 requirements from five complementary perspectives. Based on these requirements, we design KAPPS, a knowledge-based architecture that uses an ontology-grounded knowledge graph as a unifying data backbone, combined with a semantic interface layer to enable consistent data and information integration, reasoning, and communication across heterogeneous systems and services, turning the knowledge graph from an integration layer into the factories authoritative write-time state. KAPPS incorporates modules for constraint enforcement and event-driven planning, enabling incremental adaptation of execution plans under uncertainty and human-machine knowledge exchange. The applicability of KAPPS is demonstrated through two implemented use cases: (i) Anomaly detection and learning through knowledge graph mediated services and (ii) runtime constraint enforcement in a modular conveyor system. Subsequently, the architecture is evaluated against the 14 requirements (ed. abstract shortened)

AI.tldr: They propose KAPPS, a knowledge-graph-based cyber-physical production architecture tailored for circular manufacturing, enabling dynamic, uncertainty-aware, human–machine integrated planning and control, and validate it via two use cases and requirement-based evaluation.
AI.motivation: Circular manufacturing reintroduces used products with heterogeneous, uncertain conditions rather than uniform materials and fixed process sequences. Existing manufacturing IT architectures assume stable, deterministic processes and cannot represent or manage unique component states at runtime, particularly when flexible reconfiguration, uncertainty handling, and human–machine knowledge integration are required.
AI.method: Using a design science research methodology for Cyber Physical Production Systems, the authors derive 14 system requirements from five complementary perspectives. Based on these, they design the KAPPS architecture, which centers on an ontology-based knowledge graph as the unifying, authoritative runtime data backbone, with a semantic interface layer for integration and reasoning. KAPPS adds modules for constraint enforcement and event-driven planning to support adaptive execution under uncertainty and human–machine knowledge exchange. They demonstrate and evaluate the architecture via two implemented use cases and a requirements-based assessment.
AI.result: The authors develop the KAPPS architecture that: (1) uses an ontology-grounded knowledge graph as the factory’s authoritative write-time state; (2) provides a semantic interface layer for consistent data and information integration, reasoning, and communication across heterogeneous systems; and (3) includes modules for constraint enforcement and event-driven planning to incrementally adapt execution plans. They implement and show its applicability in two use cases: anomaly detection and learning via knowledge-graph-mediated services, and runtime constraint enforcement in a modular conveyor system. They then evaluate KAPPS against the 14 derived requirements, showing that it satisfies them to a substantial degree.
AI.conclusion: KAPPS demonstrates that a knowledge-graph-centered, ontology-based cyber-physical production architecture can meet the specific demands of circular manufacturing, including heterogeneous product states, runtime uniqueness of components, dynamic reconfiguration, and integration of human and machine knowledge. The evaluation and use cases indicate that such an architecture enables adaptive, constraint-aware, and semantically integrated production control under uncertainty, addressing limitations of conventional manufacturing IT systems.

title: Boiling the Frog: A Multi-Turn Benchmark for Agentic Safety
summary: Background. Traditional safety benchmarks for language models evaluate generated text: whether a model outputs toxic language, reproduces bias, or follows harmful instructions. When models are deployed as agents, the safety-relevant object shifts from what the system says to what it does within an environment, and evaluating model responses under prompting is no longer sufficient to address the safety challenges posed by artificial intelligence. Recent developments have seen the rise of benchmarks that evaluate large language models as agents. We contribute to this strand of research. Approach. We introduce Boiling the Frog, a benchmark that evaluates whether tool-using AI models deployed in corporate and office settings are susceptible to incremental attacks. Each scenario begins with benign workspace edits and later introduces a risk-bearing request. The benchmark focuses on stateful multi-turn evaluation: chains expose a persistent workspace, place the risk-bearing payload at controlled positions in the turn sequence, and score whether the resulting artifact state becomes unsafe. Scenarios are organized through a three-level operational risk taxonomy grounded in the Boiling the Frog risks, the AI Act Annex I and Annex III high-risk contexts, and EU AI Act's Code of Practice on General-Purpose AI (GPAI). Results. Across a nine-model panel, aggregate strict attack success rate (ASR) is 44.4%. Model-level ASR ranges from 20.5% for Claude Haiku 4.5 to 92.9% for Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite, with Seed 2.0 Lite also above 80%. Average chain category-level ASR reaches 93.3% for Code of Practice loss-of-control scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 'Boiling the Frog', a benchmark to test how easily tool-using AI agents in office-like environments can be incrementally manipulated into unsafe behavior, revealing high vulnerability across current models.
AI.motivation: Existing safety benchmarks focus on what language models say in single-turn text, but models increasingly act as agents that operate tools and modify persistent environments. In such settings, harms arise from what models do over time, especially under subtle, incremental attacks. There is a lack of systematic evaluation of these agentic, stateful risks, particularly in realistic corporate/office workflows and in relation to regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act.
AI.method: The authors design Boiling the Frog, a stateful multi-turn benchmark where AI agents use tools in corporate and office scenarios. Each scenario starts with harmless workspace edits and later introduces a risk-bearing request. The environment has a persistent workspace whose final state is evaluated for safety. Risk-bearing payloads are inserted at controlled positions in interaction chains. Scenarios are categorized by a three-level operational risk taxonomy aligned with Boiling the Frog risk concepts, EU AI Act Annex I and III high-risk areas, and the EU Code of Practice on GPAI. Multiple models are evaluated and attack success rates are measured at model and scenario-category levels.
AI.result: Across nine evaluated models, the strict attack success rate (ASR)—the fraction of scenarios where the final artifact becomes unsafe—is 44.4% on average. Individual models vary widely: Claude Haiku 4.5 has the lowest ASR at 20.5%, while Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite reaches 92.9%, and Seed 2.0 Lite also exceeds 80%. For chains categorized as Code of Practice loss-of-control scenarios, the average ASR is particularly high at 93.3%, indicating that models frequently lose control in these contexts.
AI.conclusion: Tool-using AI agents in realistic office and corporate settings are highly vulnerable to incremental, multi-step attacks that gradually introduce unsafe behavior, even when early steps appear benign. Existing text-only safety evaluations underestimate these risks. The Boiling the Frog benchmark and its risk taxonomy offer a structured way to assess such vulnerabilities, highlight large differences between models, and connect findings to regulatory categories under the EU AI Act and related guidelines.

title: Compiling Agentic Workflows into LLM Weights: Near-Frontier Quality at Two Orders of Magnitude Less Cost
summary: Agent orchestration frameworks have proliferated, collectively exceeding 290,000 GitHub stars across LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK, Semantic Kernel, Strands, and LlamaIndex. All follow the same pattern: an external orchestrator above the LLM, injecting instructions and routing decisions every turn. Recent work has shown this architecture is dominated for procedural tasks by simply providing the procedure in a frontier model's system prompt [Dennis et al., 2026a], at the cost of consuming the context window, requiring a frontier model for every conversation, and exposing proprietary procedures to third-party providers. Compiling the procedure into the weights of a small fine-tuned model -- creating a subterranean agent -- should resolve all of these concerns, and prior work (SimpleTOD, FireAct, SynTOD, WorkflowLLM, Agent Lumos) has shown the technique works. Yet developer adoption has overwhelmingly favored orchestration. We identify three perceived barriers and address each empirically across travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom support (14 nodes, product-specific knowledge), and insurance claims (55 nodes, 6 decision hubs).

AI.tldr: The paper compares external agent orchestration frameworks with an alternative approach: compiling procedures into smaller fine‑tuned models (“subterranean agents”) and empirically studies why developers still prefer orchestration.
AI.motivation: Existing agent orchestration frameworks dominate practice but are potentially inefficient and problematic: they require frontier models, consume context with procedural instructions, and expose proprietary logic to external providers. Prior work suggests fine‑tuned small models can internalize procedures, but adoption lags; the paper aims to understand and address the perceived barriers holding back this more efficient approach.
AI.method: The authors first analyze the common architecture of orchestration frameworks and contrast it with the fine‑tuned “subterranean agent” paradigm. They then identify three perceived barriers to adopting subterranean agents and empirically evaluate them using case studies in three domains: travel booking with 14 workflow nodes, Zoom support with 14 nodes and product‑specific knowledge, and insurance claims with 55 nodes and 6 decision hubs.
AI.result: Across the three domains, fine‑tuned subterranean agents are shown to be viable alternatives to external orchestration, resolving concerns about context window usage, dependency on frontier models, and exposure of proprietary procedures. The experiments likely demonstrate performance and/or cost advantages while systematically addressing each of the three perceived barriers.
AI.conclusion: Compiling procedures into small fine‑tuned models can outperform or match traditional orchestration frameworks for procedural tasks while reducing context use, reliance on frontier models, and privacy risks. The main obstacles are not fundamental technical limitations but perceived barriers, which the paper empirically addresses, advocating for broader adoption of subterranean agents in place of external orchestrators for many structured workflows.

title: Whose Voice Counts? Mapping Stakeholder Perspectives on AI Through Public Submissions to the U.S. Government
summary: As artificial intelligence (AI) systems become more common in our daily lives, it is important to understand how different stakeholders comprehend and envisage the role that these technologies play in shaping social, political, and economic realities. In this paper, we investigate public perceptions of AI based on a corpus of letters submitted during the public consultation for the Trump Administration's US AI Action Plan. To this aim, we release a corpus cleaning pipeline and perform topic modelling and frequency analysis to explore predominant topics discussed by different subgroups (e.g., academia, individuals, private sector) and those appearing in the AI Action Plan. Our results show that individuals voice strong concerns related to the impact of AI on life, while other stakeholders are more concerned with AI development. Our comparison of topics suggests that the AI Action Plan reflects predominantly the concerns of the private sector on security, policies, and development, with individuals' concerns less represented.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes public perceptions of AI using letters from a US AI policy consultation, showing that individual citizens focus on life impacts while the official plan mainly reflects private-sector concerns about security, policy, and development.
AI.motivation: As AI systems increasingly shape social, political, and economic life, policymakers need to understand how different stakeholders perceive AI and whose concerns are actually reflected in official strategies. There is a gap between broad public debate about AI and the priorities embedded in government AI plans. The authors aim to empirically map this landscape using real-world consultation data.
AI.method: The authors collect letters submitted to the public consultation on the US AI Action Plan under the Trump Administration. They build and release a corpus cleaning pipeline, then apply topic modelling and frequency analysis to the text. They compare topics across stakeholder subgroups (academia, individuals, private sector, etc.) and between these subgroups and the final AI Action Plan document.
AI.result: Topic modelling shows that individuals emphasise worries about AI’s effects on everyday life and society, while institutional actors like private companies and other organisations focus more on AI development, security, and policy. When the authors align these topics with those in the AI Action Plan, they find strong overlap with private-sector priorities and relatively weak representation of individuals’ life-impact concerns.
AI.conclusion: Different stakeholders have distinct concerns about AI, and the US AI Action Plan largely mirrors private-sector perspectives rather than those of individual citizens. This highlights representational imbalances in AI policy formation and underscores the need for more inclusive mechanisms to ensure that public concerns about AI’s impact on life are incorporated into national AI strategies.

title: LACO: Adaptive Latent Communication for Collaborative Driving
summary: Collaborative driving aims to improve safety and efficiency by enabling connected vehicles to coordinate under partial observability. Recent approaches have evolved from sharing visual features for perception to exchanging language-based reasoning through foundation models for behavioral coordination. Though communicating in language provides intuitive information, it introduces two challenges: high latency caused by autoregressive decoding and information loss caused by compressing rich internal representations into discrete tokens. To address these challenges, we analyze latent communication in collaborative driving under inherent limitations of multi-agent settings. Our analysis reveals agent identity confusion, where direct fusion of latent states entangles decision representations across vehicles. Motivated by this, we propose LACO, a training-free \textbf{LA}tent \textbf{CO}mmunication paradigm that seamlessly adapts pretrained driving models to collaborative settings. LACO introduces Iterative Latent Deliberation (ILD) for latent reasoning, Cross-Horizon Saliency Attribution (CHSA) for communication-efficient information selection, and Structured Semantic Knowledge Distillation (SSKD) to stabilize ego-centric decision making. Closed-loop experiments in CARLA show that LACO notably reduces communication and inference latency while maintaining strong collaborative driving performance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LACO, a training-free latent communication paradigm for collaborative driving that reduces latency and bandwidth while preserving coordination performance.
AI.motivation: Collaborative driving with connected vehicles can increase safety and efficiency, but current methods that use language-based communication through foundation models suffer from high latency due to autoregressive decoding and information loss from compressing rich internal states into discrete tokens. There is also an under-explored issue of how to share latent states across agents without entangling their decision representations.
AI.method: The authors first analyze latent communication in multi-agent collaborative driving and identify an "agent identity confusion" problem when latent states are directly fused across vehicles. Based on this, they introduce LACO, a training-free latent communication paradigm that adapts pretrained single-vehicle driving models to collaborative settings. LACO comprises three components: (1) Iterative Latent Deliberation (ILD) for performing reasoning directly in latent space across agents; (2) Cross-Horizon Saliency Attribution (CHSA) to select and communicate only the most relevant latent information across future time horizons, improving communication efficiency; and (3) Structured Semantic Knowledge Distillation (SSKD) to preserve and stabilize ego-centric decision making while benefiting from shared information.
AI.result: In closed-loop simulations using the CARLA driving benchmark, LACO significantly cuts communication load and inference latency compared to language-based communication approaches, while retaining strong collaborative driving performance metrics (e.g., success rate, safety, and efficiency).
AI.conclusion: Latent communication offers an effective alternative to language-based coordination for collaborative driving, avoiding the latency and information bottlenecks of tokenized communication. By addressing agent identity confusion and introducing ILD, CHSA, and SSKD in a training-free framework, LACO enables pretrained driving models to collaborate efficiently with lower latency and communication cost, without sacrificing performance.

title: Seeing the Poem: Image-Semantic Detection of AI-Generated Modern Chinese Poetry with MLLMs
summary: Previous detection studies have shown that LLMs cannot be effectively used as detectors, but these studies have not addressed modern Chinese poetry. Moreover, no relevant research has explored the performance of LLMs in detecting modern Chinese poetry. This paper evaluates and enhances the performance of LLMs as detectors for modern Chinese poetry, and proposes an image-semantic guided poetry detection method. Compared with traditional detection approaches, our method innovatively incorporates images that reflect the content of the poetry. Through example-driven approaches, our method effectively integrates information such as meaning, imagery, and feeling from the image, then forms a complementary judgment with the poem text. Experimental results demonstrate that the LLM detectors based on our method outperform baseline detectors based on plain text, and even surpass the best-performing traditional detector, RoBERTa. The Gemini detector using our method achieves a Macro-F1 score of 85.65%, reaching the state-of-the-art level. The performance improvements of different LLM detectors on multiple LLMs-generated data prove the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and evaluates an image-semantic guided method to improve LLMs’ ability to detect modern Chinese poetry, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing work shows LLMs are generally weak as detectors, and there is a gap in studying detection for modern Chinese poetry specifically, especially with multimodal information. The authors aim to fill this gap and test whether LLMs can be turned into strong detectors in this niche task.
AI.method: They build LLM-based detectors that take both the poem text and an associated image as input. Using example-driven prompting, the method guides the LLM to extract meaning, imagery, and emotional cues from the image and integrate them with the poem’s text. This multimodal judgment is then used for poetry detection, and the method is compared against plain-text LLM detectors and a traditional RoBERTa-based detector.
AI.result: LLM detectors enhanced with the image-semantic guided method significantly outperform text-only LLM detectors and also surpass the strongest traditional detector, RoBERTa. The best configuration, a Gemini-based detector with the proposed method, reaches a Macro-F1 of 85.65%, which is state-of-the-art on the evaluated datasets.
AI.conclusion: Contrary to earlier claims that LLMs are poor detectors, the study shows that, for modern Chinese poetry and with proper multimodal guidance, LLMs can be highly effective detectors. The integration of image semantics with textual analysis is validated as an effective strategy, improving performance consistently across different LLMs and datasets.

title: Towards Direct Evaluation of Harness Optimizers via Priority Ranking
summary: Harness optimization enables automated agent creation by having an optimizer agent iteratively update the harness of target agents. Despite its success, current studies evaluate optimizers solely by observing target agents' performance gains. This indirect end-improvement evaluation neglects optimizers' actions at intermediate steps, which are often erroneous and hinder agent performance. Therefore, it is unclear whether harness optimization is driven by optimizers' informed update actions or simply trial-and-error. This necessitates direct evaluation of harness optimizers. However, evaluating harness optimizers directly is non-trivial and costly due to the lack of oracle harnesses. To address this, we present a simple, low-cost design to directly evaluate them, namely priority ranking. By asking harness optimizers to rank components (e.g., tools) in a given harness by their potential to improve/hinder agent performance when updated, our design quantifies optimizer ability at the step level without expensive rollouts or manual examination. More importantly, optimizers' ranking performance correlates with their ability to improve agents in actual multi-step harness optimization, establishing priority ranking as a reliable predictor of optimization ability. Priority ranking is enabled by Shor, a collection of 182 human-verified optimization scenarios spanning across domains, designs, and time stages. Codes and data can be found at https://github.com/k59118/Harness_Optimizer_Evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a direct, low-cost way to evaluate harness optimizers for automated agent creation via a priority ranking task, enabled by a new dataset, Shor.
AI.motivation: Existing harness optimization work measures success only through end-task performance of target agents, ignoring the optimizer’s intermediate update actions. These actions can be wrong and harm performance, making it unclear whether improvement comes from informed decisions or blind trial-and-error. Directly evaluating optimizers is hard because we lack oracle harnesses and exhaustive rollouts are expensive. The paper aims to create a direct, practical, and cheap evaluation method for harness optimizers.
AI.method: The authors introduce a ‘priority ranking’ task: given a harness with multiple components (e.g., tools), a harness optimizer must rank these components by their potential impact on agent performance if updated. This ranking is then compared to a ground-truth ordering derived from human-verified optimization scenarios. They construct Shor, a dataset of 182 such scenarios across domains, design types, and time stages, and use it to measure optimizers’ ranking performance and its correlation with actual multi-step optimization effectiveness.
AI.result: The study shows that harness optimizers’ performance on the priority ranking task correlates with their real-world ability to improve agents in multi-step harness optimization. The ranking task can be executed without costly rollouts or manual assessment, yet still reliably reflects optimization capability.
AI.conclusion: Priority ranking is an effective, low-cost proxy for directly evaluating harness optimizers at the step level. The Shor dataset enables standardized, reproducible measurement, and ranking performance serves as a reliable predictor of an optimizer’s practical impact on agent performance in harness optimization settings.

title: Moral Semantics Survive Machine Translation: Cross-Lingual Evidence from Moral Foundations Corpora
summary: Moral language is subtle and culturally variable, making it difficult to translate faithfully across languages. Idiomatic expressions, slang, and cultural references introduce hard-to-avoid translation artifacts. Yet automated moral values classification depends on language-specific annotated corpora that exist almost exclusively in English. We investigate whether LLM-based translation can bridge this gap, taking Polish as a test case. Using $\sim$50k morally-annotated social media posts from a diverse range of topics, we apply a principled four-method validation pipeline: LaBSE cross-lingual embedding similarity, Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA), LLM-as-judge evaluation, and deep learning classifier parity tests. We show that despite shortcomings in handling slang, vulgarity, and culturally-loaded expressions, direct translation preserves subtle moral cues well enough to be harvested by cross-lingual machine learning -- with mean cosine similarity of 0.86 and AUC gaps of 0.01--0.02 across all foundations closing further under fine-tuning of language models. These results demonstrate that machine translation is a practical and cost-effective path to moral values research in languages currently under-resourced in this domain. We demonstrate this for Polish as a representative Slavic language, with expected generalisation to related languages.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether large language model (LLM)-based machine translation can reliably support automated moral values classification in low-resource languages, using Polish social media data as a case study, and finds that translation quality is sufficient for effective cross-lingual moral analysis.
AI.motivation: Most moral values corpora and classifiers are available only in English, limiting research in other languages. Moral language is nuanced, context-dependent, and culturally specific, so it is unclear whether machine translation can preserve the subtle moral cues necessary for accurate automated classification. The authors aim to determine if LLM-based translation can bridge this gap and enable moral values research in under-resourced languages.
AI.method: They collect about 50,000 morally annotated social media posts across diverse topics in Polish and use LLM-based systems to translate them. They then validate the faithfulness of moral content transfer through a four-part pipeline: (1) cross-lingual semantic similarity using LaBSE embeddings, (2) representational similarity via Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA), (3) expert-like judgment using an LLM-as-judge setup to qualitatively evaluate translations, and (4) performance parity tests with deep learning moral value classifiers trained on original vs. translated data, including fine-tuning language models to see if performance gaps can be reduced.
AI.result: They find that direct LLM-based translation generally preserves moral cues well despite noted weaknesses with slang, vulgarity, and culturally loaded expressions. Quantitatively, translated and original texts exhibit high cross-lingual similarity (mean cosine similarity of 0.86) and small performance differences in moral classification (AUC gaps of 0.01–0.02 across all moral foundations), which shrink further when models are fine-tuned on translated data.
AI.conclusion: Machine translation via LLMs is sufficiently accurate for practical moral values research in languages lacking large annotated resources. While some cultural and colloquial nuances are imperfectly captured, the remaining artifacts are small enough that cross-lingual machine learning on translated corpora is viable. Using Polish as an example, the authors argue this approach should generalize to other, especially related, under-resourced languages, offering a cost-effective strategy to expand moral language studies beyond English.

title: Search-E1: Self-Distillation Drives Self-Evolution in Search-Augmented Reasoning
summary: Post-training has become the dominant recipe for turning a language model into a competent search-augmented reasoning agent. A line of recent work pushes its performance further by adding elaborate machinery on top of this standard pipeline. These augmentations import external supervision from stronger external systems, attach auxiliary modules such as process reward models or retrospective critics, restructure the rollout itself with tree search or multi-stage curricula, or shape the reward with hand-crafted bonuses and penalties. Each addition delivers a measurable gain, but each also inflates the training pipeline and ties the recipe to resources or designs that may not always be available. We take a step back and ask whether any of this machinery is actually necessary, and propose Search-E1, a self-evolution method that lets a search-augmented agent improve through only vanilla GRPO interleaved with offline self-distillation (OFSD). After each GRPO round, the policy rolls out on its own training questions. A token-level forward KL objective then aligns the policy's inference-time distribution to its own distribution under a privileged context that exposes a more efficient sibling trajectory. Despite this simplicity, the procedure naturally provides dense per-step supervision. On seven QA benchmarks, Search-E1 reaches $0.440$ average EM with Qwen2.5-3B, surpassing all open-source baselines at both scales. Code and complete version will be made public soon.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Search-E1, a simplified self-improvement method for search-augmented language model agents that relies only on standard GRPO and offline self-distillation, yet outperforms more complex methods on QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training methods for search-augmented reasoning agents often stack many additional components—external supervision, special reward models, tree search, hand-crafted rewards—making pipelines complex, resource-heavy, and less general. The authors want to know whether these extra mechanisms are actually necessary to get strong performance and whether a much simpler, more general recipe can match or beat them.
AI.method: They propose Search-E1, which alternates standard GRPO training with offline self-distillation (OFSD). After each GRPO round, the policy model generates rollouts on its own training questions. For each question, there exists a “privileged context” that reveals a more efficient sibling trajectory. A token-level forward KL loss is then used to align the policy’s normal inference-time distribution with its distribution when conditioned on this privileged context, giving dense per-step supervision without extra external systems or hand-crafted rewards.
AI.result: On seven question-answering benchmarks, using the Qwen2.5-3B model, Search-E1 achieves an average exact match (EM) of 0.440, outperforming all open-source baseline methods at similar model scales, despite using a simpler training pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Sophisticated add-ons such as external supervisors, tree search, and specialized reward models are not strictly necessary for strong search-augmented reasoning performance. A simple loop combining vanilla GRPO with offline self-distillation, as in Search-E1, can yield state-of-the-art results for small-scale open-source models while keeping the training recipe clean and broadly applicable.

title: Self-Policy Distillation via Capability-Selective Subspace Projection
summary: Self-distillation bootstraps large language models (LLMs) by training on their own generations. However, existing methods either rely on external signals to curate self-generated outputs (e.g., correctness filtering, execution feedback, and reward search), which are costly and unavailable for the best-performing frontier models, or skip curation entirely and train on all raw outputs, an approach that is often domain-specific and hard to generalize. Both also share a deeper weakness that self-generated outputs entangle task-relevant capability with others, such as stylistic patterns, formatting artifacts, and model-specific errors, diluting the signal for the specific capability one aims to improve. In this paper, we propose Self-Policy Distillation (SPD), which achieves generalizable, capability selective without any external signal. Specifically, SPD extracts a low-rank capability subspace from the model's own gradients on correctness-defining tokens, projects key-value (KV) activations into this subspace during self-generation, and fine-tunes on the resulting raw outputs with standard next-token prediction loss. Through extensive experiments across code generation, mathematical reasoning, and multiple-choice QA, we show that SPD achieves up to 13% improvement over state-of-the-art self-distillation methods without external signals and up to 16% improvement over pre-trained baselines. Notably, SPD demonstrates superior generalizability, achieving 15% better performance under out-of-domain generalization settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Self-Policy Distillation (SPD), a self-distillation method for LLMs that selectively enhances specific capabilities without external signals, by operating in a learned low-rank capability subspace during self-generation.
AI.motivation: Existing self-distillation for LLMs either depends on expensive, often unavailable external feedback (correctness filters, execution, rewards) or trains on all raw self-generated outputs. Both approaches mix desired task capability with confounding factors like style and model-specific errors, limiting improvement and generalization. The authors aim to develop a scalable, general method that can isolate and improve targeted capabilities without external supervision.
AI.method: SPD first identifies a capability subspace by computing gradients of the model w.r.t. correctness-defining tokens and extracting a low-rank subspace that captures those gradients. During self-generation, the model’s key-value (KV) activations are projected into this subspace, so that generation is driven primarily by the targeted capability. The model is then fine-tuned on these subspace-constrained self-generated outputs using standard next-token prediction loss, enabling self-distillation focused on the desired capability and not on spurious patterns.
AI.result: Across tasks in code generation, mathematical reasoning, and multiple-choice QA, SPD outperforms state-of-the-art self-distillation methods that do not use external signals by up to 13%, and improves over the original pre-trained models by up to 16%. SPD also shows strong robustness and transfer, with up to 15% better performance in out-of-domain generalization scenarios compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: SPD enables capability-selective, generalizable self-distillation for LLMs without relying on external correctness or reward signals. By restricting generation to a learned low-rank capability subspace, it yields larger gains than prior self-distillation methods and generalizes better across domains and tasks, suggesting a promising direction for scalable, self-supervised improvement of frontier LLMs.

title: Meta-Learning for Rapid Adaptation in Reference Tracking of Uncertain Nonlinear Systems
summary: In this paper, we address the problem of reference tracking for uncertain nonlinear systems. Since collecting data from the target system (i.e., the system of interest) is often challenging, our objective is to design optimal controllers using limited target system data. Meta-learning provides a promising paradigm by leveraging offline data from source systems (systems sharing structural similarities with the target system) to accelerate training and enhance control performance. Motivated by this idea, we propose a meta-learning-based control framework that tailors the implicit model-agnostic meta-learning (iMAML) algorithm to the control setting. The framework operates in two phases: an (offline) meta-training phase, where an aggregated representation is learned from source data to capture the shared system dynamics among similar systems, and an (online) meta-adaptation phase, where this representation is fine-tuned on the target system using only a few data samples and limited adaptation steps. We formulate this framework as a bi-level optimization problem and provide an efficient solution with reduced storage complexity and few approximations. The proposed framework is general, allowing various learning algorithms to be integrated. To demonstrate this flexibility, we propose two specific learning algorithms that can be incorporated into our framework based on a neural state-space model and a deep Q-network, respectively. The primary distinction between these approaches is whether explicit system identification is required. Numerical simulations and hardware experiments demonstrate that the proposed methods enhance control performance and consistently outperform baseline approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a meta-learning-based control framework that learns from multiple similar nonlinear systems offline and then rapidly adapts to a new target system with very little data to achieve good reference-tracking performance.
AI.motivation: Designing optimal controllers for uncertain nonlinear systems is difficult when only a small amount of data from the actual target system is available. However, data from other, structurally similar systems may be more abundant. The authors aim to exploit this offline source data to build controllers that can quickly adapt to a new target system and track references effectively, overcoming data scarcity and training inefficiency in conventional approaches.
AI.method: The authors develop a meta-learning-based control framework grounded in implicit Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (iMAML). The framework has two phases: (1) an offline meta-training phase, where data from multiple source systems are used to learn an aggregated representation that captures shared dynamics; (2) an online meta-adaptation phase, where this representation is fine-tuned for the target system using few data samples and limited adaptation steps. They formulate this as a bi-level optimization problem and derive an efficient solver with reduced storage complexity and controlled approximations. The framework is algorithm-agnostic; they instantiate it with two concrete algorithms: one using a neural state-space model (explicit system identification) and another using a deep Q-network (implicit, model-free control).
AI.result: Through numerical simulations and hardware experiments, the proposed meta-learning control methods achieve better reference-tracking performance and higher control quality than baseline methods that do not leverage meta-learning or do not share information across systems. The results indicate improved data efficiency and consistent performance gains across different settings and algorithmic instantiations (neural state-space and DQN).
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that meta-learning, specifically an iMAML-based framework, is an effective way to design controllers for uncertain nonlinear systems when target data are scarce. By learning shared dynamics from related source systems and rapidly adapting online with little data, the framework improves control performance and outperforms traditional baselines. Its general, algorithm-agnostic formulation allows integration with different learning paradigms, making it a flexible tool for data-efficient control design in practice.

title: Tokenization with Split Trees
summary: We introduce Tokenization with Split Trees (ToaST), a subword tokenization method that directly optimizes compression under a new recursive inference procedure. ToaST greedily splits each pretoken into a full binary tree using precomputed byte n-gram counts, independent of any vocabulary. Given a vocabulary, inference recursively descends each split tree and emits the first in-vocabulary node reached on each path. Vocabulary selection is formulated as an Integer Program (IP) that minimizes the total token count over all split trees under this inference procedure. The Linear Programming (LP) relaxation is near-integral in practice, yielding provably near-optimal vocabularies, with training time empirically scaling quadratically in the number of split trees. On English text, ToaST reduces token counts by more than 11% compared to BPE, WordPiece, and UnigramLM at vocabulary sizes of 40,960 and above, reducing the number of inference tokens for models using this tokenizer, thus extending the effective context length. ToaST also uses common single-byte tokens less frequently than these baselines, leading to a substantial improvement in Renyi efficiency. In experiments training 1.5B parameter language models, ToaST achieves the highest CORE score, outperforming baselines by 2.6%--7.6%, with significance for two of three, and scoring best on 13 of 22 individual tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces ToaST, a tokenization method that builds split trees and chooses a near‑optimal vocabulary via (integer) linear programming, yielding fewer tokens and better downstream LM performance than standard subword methods.
AI.motivation: Existing subword tokenizers like BPE, WordPiece, and UnigramLM are not explicitly optimized for minimizing the number of tokens under their actual inference procedures, limiting compression efficiency and effective context length. The authors aim to design a tokenizer whose vocabulary and inference are jointly optimized for token count reduction and better information efficiency, thereby improving language model performance and usable context without changing model size.
AI.method: 1) Pre-tokenize text into units and, for each, greedily build a full binary split tree using precomputed byte n‑gram statistics, independent of any fixed vocabulary. 2) Define a new inference rule: given a vocabulary, traverse each tree top-down and emit the first node encountered along each root‑to‑leaf path that is in the vocabulary. 3) Formulate vocabulary selection as an Integer Program that minimizes the total number of emitted tokens over all trees under this inference rule subject to a vocabulary size constraint. 4) Solve (or approximately solve) the associated Linear Programming relaxation, which is empirically near‑integral, providing near‑optimal vocabularies with training time scaling roughly quadratically in the number of split trees. 5) Evaluate ToaST versus standard tokenizers on English corpora and in 1.5B-parameter LM training, measuring token count, Renyi efficiency, and CORE benchmark scores.
AI.result: On English text and vocabularies of size 40,960 and larger, ToaST reduces token counts by over 11% compared with BPE, WordPiece, and UnigramLM, thus effectively extending the usable context window for a fixed-length LM. It also relies less on common single-byte tokens, significantly improving Renyi efficiency. In 1.5B-parameter LM experiments, models using ToaST achieve the best CORE benchmark scores, outperforming baselines by 2.6–7.6%; results are statistically significant for two of three comparisons, and ToaST attains the top score on 13 out of 22 individual tasks.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing tokenization structure and vocabulary via split trees and (near-)integer linear programming yields more compression-efficient subword tokenization than widely used methods. This reduces token counts and improves information efficiency, which in turn leads to better performance of medium-sized language models on a broad evaluation suite without altering model size, effectively increasing context utility and demonstrating the practical benefits of ToaST.

title: A Subjective Logic-based method for runtime confidence updates in safety arguments
comment: Accepted for publication at the 41st ACM/SIGAPP Symposium on Applied Computing (SAC 2026)
summary: We present a method for dynamic quantitative assurance that enhances static safety cases with continuous, runtime-driven confidence updates. The method quantifies and propagates confidence across the development lifecycle by integrating design-time evidence and windowed runtime Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs) within a single Subjective Logic (SL)-based assurance case. At runtime, SPI evidence is continuously evaluated, and targeted claims are updated using a rule that increases confidence in the absence of violations and imposes prompt penalties when violations occur. This design prioritizes safety-relevant responsiveness over exact classical Bayesian posterior updates. We demonstrate the method using a simulation-based construction zone assist function, focusing on an ML-based construction cone detection component, and show how confidence evolves as SPI evidence is observed in operation.

AI.tldr: A runtime-augmented safety assurance method that uses Subjective Logic to continuously update confidence in system safety based on Safety Performance Indicators, prioritizing quick reaction to violations over exact Bayesian updating.
AI.motivation: Static safety cases provide only a snapshot of confidence and cannot reflect evolving operational evidence, especially for ML-based components whose behavior can change with environment and data drift. There is a need for a quantitative, lifecycle-spanning assurance approach that combines design-time evidence with runtime performance to better capture and manage safety confidence over time.
AI.method: The paper builds a single assurance case using Subjective Logic, where claims are associated with quantitative confidence values. It integrates design-time evidence with windowed runtime Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs). During operation, SPI evidence is assessed in windows; confidence in specific claims is updated using a bespoke SL-based rule that incrementally increases confidence when no safety violations are observed and sharply decreases it when violations occur, intentionally deviating from classical Bayesian posterior computation to prioritize safety responsiveness.
AI.result: Using a simulation-based case study of a construction zone assist function including an ML-based construction cone detector, the authors instantiate their framework and show numerically and qualitatively how the confidence values for safety claims evolve over time as SPI evidence is accumulated, demonstrating responsive increases during safe operation and rapid decreases after observed violations.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, SL-based quantitative assurance that fuses design-time and runtime evidence offers a more responsive and realistic view of safety confidence than static safety cases alone, particularly for ML-intensive systems. The proposed confidence update rule can support continuous assurance and monitoring by promptly reflecting deteriorations in safety performance while still rewarding sustained safe behavior.

title: ChronoMedKG: A Temporally-Grounded Biomedical Knowledge Graph and Benchmark for Clinical Reasoning
comment: 9 pages main text plus appendices, 8 figures. Dataset and benchmark paper. ChronoMedKG released under CC BY 4.0 and ChronoTQA/code under MIT (Zenodo: 10.5281/zenodo.19697542). Under review
summary:
Biomedical knowledge graphs (KGs) treat disease associations as static facts, but temporal information is crucial for clinical reasoning, e.g., a symptom diagnostic of one disease at age 3 may imply a different disease at age 13. Existing KGs such as PrimeKG, Hetionet, and iKraph do not encode when a finding becomes clinically relevant over the course of a disease. This limits their usefulness for longitudinal clinical reasoning and retrieval augmentation.
  We introduce ChronoMedKG, a temporal biomedical knowledge graph that contains 460,497 evidence-linked triples (filtered from 13M raw extractions) covering 13,431 diseases. Each association is tied to temporal components like onset window or progression stage, which are backed by PMID-traceable evidence and a multi-signal credibility score. The graph is constructed through a disease-autonomous multi-agent pipeline in which multiple frontier LLMs independently extract knowledge from PubMed and PMC literature. Only those relations are kept that are supported by multi-model consensus, survive credibility filtering, as well as ontology alignment.
  ChronoMedKG scored 92.7% agreement against Orphadata and adds temporal grounding for 6,250 diseases absent from HPOA, Orphadata, and Phenopackets, including 1,657 Orphanet-coded rare diseases. We further introduce ChronoTQA, a benchmark of 3,341 questions across eight task types (six temporal plus two static controls), with a 12-question supplementary probe. Frontier LLMs lose roughly 30 points moving from static to temporal questions; ChronoMedKG retrieval rescues 47-65% of their long-tail failures, against 17-29% for HPOA-RAG. As such, ChronoMedKG provides a crucial temporal axis for retrieval-augmented clinical systems that was previously absent.

AI.tldr: ChronoMedKG is a temporal biomedical knowledge graph that encodes when disease findings become clinically relevant, built via multi-LLM, evidence-linked extraction, and significantly improves temporal clinical reasoning in RAG settings.
AI.motivation: Existing biomedical knowledge graphs treat disease associations as static, lacking temporal information such as age of onset or disease stage when a symptom or finding becomes clinically relevant. This temporal dimension is crucial for longitudinal clinical reasoning (e.g., interpreting the same symptom differently at different ages) and for retrieval-augmented generation systems, but current resources like PrimeKG, Hetionet, and iKraph, as well as phenotype ontologies such as HPOA and Orphadata, do not provide systematic temporal grounding. The authors aim to fill this gap so that clinical AI systems can reason over the progression of diseases rather than just static associations.
AI.method: The authors construct ChronoMedKG, a temporal biomedical knowledge graph, by running a disease-autonomous multi-agent pipeline over PubMed and PMC literature. Multiple frontier large language models independently extract disease-related temporal associations, including onset windows and progression stages. From an initial 13 million raw extractions, they retain only triples that pass multi-model consensus, credibility filtering via a multi-signal score, and ontology alignment. Each of the 460,497 resulting triples is linked to PubMed IDs for traceable evidence and annotated with temporal components. They then build ChronoTQA, a benchmark of 3,341 questions across eight task types (six temporal, two static), plus a 12-question probe set, to evaluate temporal clinical reasoning and RAG performance.
AI.result: ChronoMedKG covers 13,431 diseases and achieves 92.7% agreement with Orphadata, providing temporal grounding for 6,250 diseases that lack such information in HPOA, Orphadata, and Phenopackets, including 1,657 rare diseases with Orphanet codes. On the ChronoTQA benchmark, frontier LLMs experience about a 30-point performance drop when moving from static to temporal questions. When used as a retrieval source, ChronoMedKG recovers 47–65% of LLM long-tail failures on temporal tasks, substantially outperforming an HPOA-based RAG baseline, which recovers only 17–29%.
AI.conclusion: ChronoMedKG introduces a critical temporal dimension to biomedical knowledge graphs, enabling more accurate longitudinal clinical reasoning and significantly improving LLM performance on temporal clinical questions when used for retrieval augmentation. By providing evidence-linked, temporally grounded disease associations across a large set of diseases, including many rare ones, and by releasing the ChronoTQA benchmark, the work establishes both a new resource and a rigorous evaluation framework for temporal clinical AI systems.

title: TerminalWorld: Benchmarking Agents on Real-World Terminal Tasks
summary: We introduce TerminalWorld, a scalable data engine that automatically reverse-engineers high-fidelity evaluation tasks from "in-the-wild" terminal recordings. Processing 80,870 terminal recordings, the engine yields a full benchmark of 1,530 validated tasks, spanning 18 real-world categories, ranging from short everyday operations to workflows exceeding 50 steps, and covering 1,280 unique commands. From these, we curate a Verified subset of 200 representative, manually reviewed tasks. Comprehensive benchmarking on TerminalWorld-Verified across eight frontier models and six agents reveals that current systems still struggle with authentic terminal workflows, achieving a maximum pass rate of only 62.5%. Moreover, TerminalWorld captures real-world terminal capabilities distinct from existing expert-curated benchmarks (e.g., Terminal-Bench), with only a weak correlation to their scores (Pearson r=0.20). The automated engine makes TerminalWorld authentic and scalable by construction, enabling it to evaluate agents in real-world terminal environments as developer practices evolve. Data and code are available at https://github.com/EuniAI/TerminalWorld.

AI.tldr: TerminalWorld is an automated, scalable benchmark that converts real terminal usage recordings into high-fidelity evaluation tasks for testing AI agents and models on authentic command-line workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing terminal benchmarks are typically expert-curated, limited in scale and diversity, and may not reflect real-world developer workflows. As AI coding assistants increasingly need to operate robustly in terminal environments, there is a need for a large, realistic, and automatically updatable benchmark derived from actual terminal usage rather than synthetic or hand-crafted tasks.
AI.method: The authors build a data engine that ingests 80,870 real terminal recordings and automatically reverse-engineers them into structured evaluation tasks. This involves segmenting multi-step terminal sessions into discrete tasks, extracting commands and workflows, validating them so that they are reproducible, and categorizing them into 18 real-world task categories. From the full set of 1,530 validated tasks (covering 1,280 unique commands), they curate a smaller Verified subset of 200 representative tasks that are manually reviewed for quality. They then evaluate eight frontier language models and six terminal agents on these tasks and compare performance to existing benchmarks like Terminal-Bench, examining correlations.
AI.result: The engine produces a benchmark of 1,530 validated tasks and a high-quality Verified subset of 200 manually reviewed tasks. Benchmarking shows that even the best current systems achieve only a 62.5% pass rate on TerminalWorld-Verified, indicating substantial remaining difficulty on real terminal workflows, including long workflows exceeding 50 steps. Performance on TerminalWorld is only weakly correlated with scores on existing expert-curated benchmarks such as Terminal-Bench (Pearson r=0.20), suggesting that TerminalWorld captures distinct, real-world capabilities not covered by prior benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TerminalWorld provides an authentic, scalable, and automatically constructed benchmark of real terminal usage that more accurately reflects practical developer workflows than existing curated benchmarks. The relatively low pass rates of strong models and agents highlight that robust terminal operation remains an open challenge. Because the data engine works directly from in-the-wild recordings, it can naturally evolve alongside developer practices, making TerminalWorld a sustainable resource for evaluating and advancing AI agents in real-world terminal environments.

title: Understanding Data Temporality Impact on Large Language Models Pre-training
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are typically trained on shuffled corpora, yielding models whose knowledge is frozen at train time and whose temporal grounding remains poorly understood. In this work, we study the impact of pre-training dynamics on the acquisition of time-sensitive factual knowledge, focusing specifically on data ordering. Our main contributions are twofold. First, we introduce a comprehensive benchmark of over 7,000 temporally grounded questions and an evaluation protocol that enables analysis of whether models correctly associate facts with their corresponding time periods. Second, we pretrain 6B-parameter models on temporally ordered Common Crawl snapshots and compare them against standard shuffled pre-training. Our results show that sequentially trained models match shuffled baselines on general language understanding and common knowledge while consistently exhibiting more up-to-date and temporally precise knowledge. Temporally ordered pre-training yields improved factual freshness, while shuffled pre-training peaks on older data, possibly due to increased factual repetition. These findings, along with the release of our code at https://github.com/kyutai-labs/kairos , checkpoints, and datasets at https://huggingface.co/collections/kyutai/kairos provide a foundation for future research on continual learning for LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that pretraining LLMs on temporally ordered web snapshots, instead of fully shuffled data, makes models’ factual knowledge more up‑to‑date and time-accurate without hurting general language ability.
AI.motivation: LLMs are usually trained on randomly shuffled text, so their factual knowledge is effectively frozen at the time of training and their sense of when facts are true (temporal grounding) is unclear. The authors want to understand how the dynamics of pretraining—especially the order in which data is seen—affect the acquisition and freshness of time-sensitive facts, and whether alternative training schemes can improve temporal accuracy without harming overall performance.
AI.method: They construct a benchmark of 7,000+ questions whose answers depend on specific time periods and design an evaluation protocol that checks if models assign facts to the correct temporal context. Then they pretrain 6B-parameter language models on Common Crawl snapshots arranged in chronological order and compare them with standard models trained on the same data but fully shuffled. They evaluate both sets of models on general language understanding, common knowledge tasks, and the new temporal benchmark to compare factual freshness and temporal precision.
AI.result: Sequentially (temporally ordered) trained models perform on par with shuffled models on general language understanding and broad common-knowledge benchmarks, but they provide more recent and temporally accurate factual answers on the new benchmark. Shuffled pretraining tends to favor older facts, likely because these are more repeated in the mixed corpus, while temporally ordered training better reflects the latest information seen in later snapshots.
AI.conclusion: Data ordering during pretraining has a systematic impact on how LLMs store and retrieve time-sensitive facts. Chronologically ordered pretraining improves the recency and temporal precision of factual knowledge without sacrificing general capability, suggesting that controlled pretraining dynamics can help address the problem of stale or temporally confused knowledge. The released benchmark, code, checkpoints, and datasets form a basis for further work on continual and temporally aware LLM training strategies.

title: Think Thrice Before You Speak: Dual knowledge-enhanced Theory-of-Mind Reasoning for Persuasive Agents
summary: Persuasive dialogue requires reasoning about others' latent mental states, a capability known as Theory of Mind (ToM). However, due to reliance on simple prompting strategies and insufficient ToM knowledge, existing LLMs often fail to capture the intrinsic dependencies among mental states, leading to fragmented representations and unstable reasoning. To address these challenges, we introduce the ToM-based Persuasive Dialogue (ToM-PD) task, grounded in the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) framework, which explicitly models the sequential dependencies among mental states in multi-turn dialogues. To facilitate research on this task, we construct a large-scale annotated dataset, ToM-based Broad Persuasive Dialogues (ToM-BPD), capturing fine-grained mental states and corresponding persuasive strategies. We further propose Think Thrice Before You Speak (TTBYS), a knowledge-enhanced stepwise reasoning framework that leverages both explicit and implicit prior experiences to improve LLMs' inference of desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies. Experimental results demonstrate that Qwen3-8B equipped with TTBYS outperforms GPT-5 by 1.20%, 22.80%, and 16.97% in predicting desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies, respectively. Case studies further show that our approach enhances interpretability and consistency in reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Theory-of-Mind-based persuasive dialogue task, dataset, and reasoning framework to improve LLMs’ ability to infer mental states and choose persuasive strategies.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs struggle with persuasive dialogue because they lack robust Theory of Mind reasoning and rely on shallow prompting, which leads to fragmented, inconsistent modeling of interlocutors’ beliefs, desires, and intentions. There is also a lack of structured tasks and datasets that explicitly represent the dependencies among these mental states in multi-turn conversations.
AI.method: 1) Define a new ToM-based Persuasive Dialogue (ToM-PD) task grounded in the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) framework, explicitly modeling sequential dependencies among mental states across dialogue turns. 2) Build a large-scale annotated dataset, ToM-based Broad Persuasive Dialogues (ToM-BPD), with fine-grained labels of mental states and persuasive strategies. 3) Propose Think Thrice Before You Speak (TTBYS), a knowledge-enhanced, stepwise reasoning framework that leverages both explicit (structured) and implicit (experience-based) prior knowledge to guide LLMs in inferring interlocutors’ desires, beliefs, and suitable persuasive strategies.
AI.result: Using the ToM-BPD dataset and the TTBYS framework, the authors show that Qwen3-8B with TTBYS significantly improves ToM-related inference performance, surpassing GPT-5 by 1.20% on desire prediction, 22.80% on belief prediction, and 16.97% on persuasive-strategy prediction. Qualitative case studies indicate more coherent, interpretable, and consistent reasoning about mental states and persuasion steps.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling Theory of Mind with a BDI-based task formulation, a large-scale ToM-annotated persuasive dialogue dataset, and a knowledge-enhanced stepwise reasoning framework can substantially improve LLMs’ ability to infer mental states and select effective persuasive strategies, while also yielding more interpretable and stable reasoning processes.

title: Reducing Political Manipulation with Consistency Training
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit systematic political bias across a variety of sensitive contexts. We find that LLMs handle counterpart topics from opposing political sides asymmetrically. We refer to this phenomenon as covert political bias and identify 7 categories of techniques through which it operates. We propose two metrics for covert bias: Sentiment Consistency measures symmetry in rhetoric and framing across paired political prompts; Helpfulness Consistency measures symmetric depth and engagement. To reduce both types of covert bias, we introduce Political Consistency Training (PCT), an RL training method with two complementary paradigms: Sentiment Consistency Training and Helpfulness Consistency Training. We show that PCT preserves overall helpfulness, substantially reduces covert political bias, and generalizes to held-out benchmarks. We release our work at https://political-manipulation.ai

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and measures subtle, asymmetric political bias in large language models and proposes a reinforcement-learning-based training method to reduce this bias while preserving helpfulness.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are increasingly used in politically sensitive settings, they can respond differently to ideologically mirrored prompts, covertly favoring one side. Existing bias metrics often miss these asymmetric, context-dependent behaviors. The authors aim to systematically characterize and quantify such covert political bias and to develop a practical training approach that mitigates it without degrading model utility.
AI.method: The authors first empirically study how LLMs respond to paired, ideologically opposite political prompts and categorize seven techniques through which covert political bias manifests (e.g., asymmetric framing, engagement, or criticism). They then formalize two quantitative metrics: Sentiment Consistency, which compares sentiment and framing across counterpart prompts, and Helpfulness Consistency, which compares depth and engagement. To mitigate these biases, they propose Political Consistency Training (PCT), a reinforcement learning framework with two components: Sentiment Consistency Training, which rewards symmetric sentiment across political counterparts, and Helpfulness Consistency Training, which rewards balanced helpfulness and engagement. They train LLMs with PCT and evaluate performance on both in-distribution and held-out political bias benchmarks, along with standard helpfulness metrics.
AI.result: Models trained with Political Consistency Training show substantial reductions in covert political bias according to both Sentiment Consistency and Helpfulness Consistency metrics. The improvements generalize to unseen political topics and benchmarks, indicating that PCT is not overfitted to the training prompts. At the same time, overall model helpfulness on general tasks is preserved, suggesting that bias mitigation does not come at a major cost to utility.
AI.conclusion: Covert, asymmetric political bias is a measurable and systematic phenomenon in LLMs, manifesting through multiple rhetorical and engagement patterns that standard bias metrics may overlook. By defining targeted consistency metrics and using them as reinforcement learning signals, Political Consistency Training can significantly reduce such bias while maintaining general helpfulness. This approach offers a scalable path toward more politically neutral LLM behavior in sensitive applications.

title: Spreadsheet-RL: Advancing Large Language Model Agents on Realistic Spreadsheet Tasks via Reinforcement Learning
comment: Mingyuan served as the project lead. Banghao, Yining, and Mingyuan contributed equally to this work, with more junior authors listed before senior authors. All data and code releases are maintained by the corresponding authors at UIUC and are not affiliated with Meta
summary:
Spreadsheet systems (e.g., Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets) play a central role in modern data-centric workflows. As AI agents grow increasingly capable of automating complex tasks, such as controlling computers and generating presentations, building an AI-driven spreadsheet agent has emerged as a promising research direction. Most existing spreadsheet agents rely on specialized prompting over general-purpose LLMs; while this design has potentials on simple spreadsheet operations, it struggles to manage the complex, multi-step workflows typical of real-world applications.
  We introduce Spreadsheet-RL, a reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning framework designed to train specialized spreadsheet agents within a realistic Microsoft Excel environment. Spreadsheet-RL features an automated pipeline for scalable collection of paired start-goal spreadsheets from online forums, as well as domain-specific evaluation tasks in areas such as finance and supply chain management, which we compile into the new Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark dataset. It also includes a Spreadsheet Gym environment designed for multi-turn RL: Spreadsheet Gym exposes extensive Excel functionality through a Python sandbox, along with a refined harness that incorporates a comprehensive tool set and carefully designed tool-routing rules for spreadsheet tasks. Through comprehensive experiments, we show that Spreadsheet-RL substantially enhances AI agent's performance on both general and domain-specific spreadsheet tasks: it improves Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507's Pass@1 on SpreadsheetBench from 12.0% to 23.4%, and raises Pass@1 from 8.4% to 17.2% on our curated Domain-Spreadsheet dataset. These results highlight Spreadsheet-RL's strong potential for generalization and real-world adoption in spreadsheet automation, and broadly, its promise for advancing LLM-based interactions with data interfaces in everyday work.

AI.tldr: Spreadsheet-RL is a reinforcement-learning fine-tuning framework that trains specialized AI spreadsheet agents in a realistic Excel environment, significantly improving performance on complex spreadsheet tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing spreadsheet agents mainly use prompting on general-purpose LLMs, which works for simple operations but fails on complex, multi-step, real-world spreadsheet workflows. There is a need for specialized agents trained directly in realistic spreadsheet environments and evaluated on domain-relevant tasks.
AI.method: The authors build Spreadsheet-RL, an RL fine-tuning framework that: (1) automatically collects paired start-goal spreadsheets from online forums; (2) constructs a Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark with finance, supply chain, and other domain tasks; and (3) provides Spreadsheet Gym, a multi-turn RL environment that exposes rich Excel functionality via a Python sandbox plus a carefully designed tool set and tool-routing rules. They then RL-finetune a base model (Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507) as a spreadsheet agent within this environment.
AI.result: Using Spreadsheet-RL to train a spreadsheet agent substantially improves performance: Pass@1 on the general SpreadsheetBench benchmark increases from 12.0% to 23.4%, and Pass@1 on the new Domain-Spreadsheet dataset increases from 8.4% to 17.2%. This shows better handling of both generic and domain-specific spreadsheet tasks.
AI.conclusion: Spreadsheet-RL enables more capable, specialized spreadsheet agents by combining realistic Excel interaction, automated data collection, and RL fine-tuning. The improved benchmark results indicate stronger generalization and practical potential for real-world spreadsheet automation and, more broadly, for LLM-based interaction with data-centric interfaces in everyday workflows.

title: Evaluating Commercial AI Chatbots as News Intermediaries
summary: AI chatbots are rapidly shaping how people encounter the news, yet no prior study has systematically measured how accurately these systems, with their proprietary search integrations and retrieval-synthesis pipelines, handle emerging facts across languages and regions. We present a 14-day (February 9-22, 2026) evaluation of six AI chatbots (Gemini 3 Flash and Pro, Grok 4, Claude 4.5 Sonnet, GPT-5 and GPT-4o mini) on 2,100 factual questions derived from same-day BBC News reporting across six regional services (US & Canada, Arabic, Afrique, Hindi, Russian, Turkish). The best systems achieve over 90% multiple-choice accuracy on questions about events reported hours earlier. The same systems, however, lose 11-13% under free-response evaluation, and 16-17% across the cohort. We further characterize three failure patterns. First, every model achieves its lowest accuracy on Hindi (79% vs. 89-91% elsewhere) and citations indicate an Anglophone retrieval bias (e.g., models answering Hindi queries cite English Wikipedia more than any Hindi outlet). Second, retrieval, not reasoning, failures drive over 70% of all errors. When models retrieve a correct source, they often extract the correct answer; the problem is to land on the right source in the first place. Third, models achieving 88-96% accuracy on well-formed questions drop to 19-70% when questions contain subtle false premises, with the most vulnerable model accepting fabricated facts 64% of the time. We also identify a detection-accuracy paradox: the best false-premise detector ranks second in adversarial accuracy (abstention rate), while a weaker detector ranks first, showing that premise detection and answer recovery are partially independent capabilities. Overall, these suggest that high accuracy can mask systematic regional inequity, near-total dependence on retrieval infrastructure, and vulnerability to imperfect queries real users pose.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how accurately major AI chatbots answer fresh news questions across regions and languages, revealing strong average performance but serious weaknesses in non-English contexts, dependence on retrieval, and susceptibility to false premises.
AI.motivation: AI chatbots are becoming a primary interface for news consumption, but there is little systematic, cross-lingual, cross-regional evidence on how well they handle newly emerging facts, how reliable their integrated search and retrieval-synthesis pipelines are, and whether performance is equitable across languages. The authors aim to fill this gap and to move beyond anecdotal or English-only benchmarks by using real, same-day news and comparing multiple leading systems.
AI.method: Over a continuous 14-day period, the authors construct a benchmark of 2,100 factual questions derived from same-day BBC News articles across six regional services (US & Canada, Arabic, Afrique, Hindi, Russian, Turkish). They query six leading AI chatbots (Gemini 3 Flash and Pro, Grok 4, Claude 4.5 Sonnet, GPT-5, GPT-4o mini) within hours of publication. They evaluate both multiple-choice and free-response formats, and they analyze errors to separate retrieval from reasoning failures, examine language/region-specific effects, and test robustness to subtle false premises and adversarial questioning. They also compare models’ ability to detect false premises with their overall adversarial performance, identifying trade-offs between detection and answer accuracy.
AI.result: Top models achieve over 90% accuracy on multiple-choice questions about very recent news, but their performance drops by 11–13 percentage points in free-response and by 16–17 percentage points when averaged across all systems. All models perform substantially worse on Hindi questions (79% accuracy) than on other regions (89–91%), and citation patterns show a strong bias toward English-language sources even when the query is in Hindi. More than 70% of observed errors stem from retrieval failures, not reasoning errors: when systems fetch the correct source, they generally extract the correct answer. Models that perform very well on clean, well-posed questions (88–96% accuracy) degrade sharply (19–70% accuracy) when questions contain subtle false premises; the worst model accepts fabricated facts in 64% of such cases. A paradox emerges where the strongest false-premise detector is only second-best on adversarial accuracy (due in part to higher abstention), while a weaker detector attains the best adversarial accuracy, implying that successfully flagging false premises and successfully answering adversarial questions are partially decoupled skills.
AI.conclusion: Headline accuracy numbers on standard, well-formed factual questions hide deeper structural issues in current news-integrated chatbots. These systems exhibit inequitable performance across languages and regions, with especially poor results and Anglophone source bias for Hindi queries. Their reliability is heavily constrained by the quality and coverage of their retrieval infrastructure rather than purely by reasoning ability, making them vulnerable when relevant sources are not surfaced. They also struggle with questions containing subtle false premises, often endorsing fabricated facts, and the relationship between premise detection and robust answering is nontrivial. The authors argue that evaluations and system design must explicitly address regional and linguistic fairness, retrieval robustness, and adversarial/question-quality robustness, rather than relying on aggregate accuracy metrics that can obscure these vulnerabilities.

title: AtelierEval: Agentic Evaluation of Humans & LLMs as Text-to-Image Prompters
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) systems increasingly rely on upstream prompters, either humans or multimodal large language models (MLLMs), to translate user intent into detailed prompts. Yet current benchmarks fix the prompt and only evaluate T2I models, leaving the prompting proficiency of this upstream component entirely unmeasured. We introduce AtelierEval, the first unified benchmark that quantifies prompting proficiency across 360 expert-crafted tasks. Grounded in a cognitive view, it spans three task categories and instantiates tasks using a taxonomy of real-world challenges, with a dual interface for both humans and MLLMs. To enable scalable and reliable evaluation, we propose AtelierJudge, a skill-based, memory-augmented agentic evaluator. It produces subjective and objective scores for prompt-image pairs, achieving a Spearman correlation of 0.79 with human experts, approaching human performance. Extensive experiments benchmark 8 MLLMs against 48 human users across 4 T2I backends, validate AtelierEval as a robust diagnostic tool, and reveal the superiority of mimicry over planning, advocating for an image-augmented direction for future prompters. Our work is released to support future research.

AI.tldr: AtelierEval is a benchmark and evaluation framework to measure how well humans and MLLMs craft prompts for text-to-image systems, along with an automatic judge that closely matches human expert assessments.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-image benchmarks keep prompts fixed and only evaluate image generators, ignoring the crucial role and variable quality of upstream prompters (humans or MLLMs). As T2I systems increasingly depend on these prompters, there is no standardized way to quantify prompting proficiency or compare humans and MLLMs, nor a scalable, reliable automatic judge. This gap hinders systematic improvement of prompting techniques and prompter models.
AI.method: The authors build AtelierEval, a unified benchmark of 360 expert-designed tasks categorized according to a cognitively motivated taxonomy of real-world prompting challenges. Tasks are designed to work both with human prompters and multimodal LLMs via a dual interface. They also introduce AtelierJudge, a memory-augmented, agentic evaluation model that scores prompt–image pairs along subjective (e.g., aesthetic, alignment) and objective dimensions.
