The judge is trained/tuned and validated against human expert annotations, and extensive experiments compare 8 MLLMs and 48 human users across 4 different T2I backends using this benchmark and judge.
AI.result: AtelierJudge attains a Spearman correlation of 0.79 with human experts when scoring prompt–image pairs, a level close to inter-human agreement, enabling scalable automatic evaluation. Applying AtelierEval, the authors benchmark 8 different MLLMs and 48 human users interacting with 4 T2I models. The benchmark demonstrates that AtelierEval provides stable, diagnostic measurements of prompting skill and reveals that prompting strategies based on mimicry (using visual examples, imitation of reference images) outperform more abstract planning-style prompting. The study further shows that leveraging images in the prompting process significantly boosts performance of MLLM-based prompters.
AI.conclusion: AtelierEval and AtelierJudge together provide a practical, high-fidelity framework for evaluating the prompting proficiency of both humans and MLLMs in text-to-image pipelines, filling a missing component in current evaluation ecosystems. The findings suggest that future prompter designs should emphasize image-augmented, example-driven (mimicry) approaches rather than purely textual planning. The released benchmark and tools are positioned as foundational infrastructure for future research on prompt optimization and prompter models for T2I systems.

title: Tokenisation via Convex Relaxations
summary: Tokenisation is an integral part of the current NLP pipeline. Current tokenisation algorithms such as BPE and Unigram are greedy algorithms -- they make locally optimal decisions without considering the resulting vocabulary as a whole. We instead formulate tokeniser construction as a linear program and solve it using convex optimisation tools, yielding a new algorithm we call ConvexTok. We find ConvexTok consistently improves intrinsic tokenisation metrics and the bits-per-byte (BpB) achieved by language models; it also improves downstream task performance, but less consistently. Furthermore, ConvexTok allows the user to certify how far their tokeniser is from optimal, with respect to a certain objective, via a lower bound, and we empirically find it to be within 1\% of optimal at common vocabulary sizes.

AI.tldr: Introduces ConvexTok, a tokeniser built via linear programming that outperforms greedy methods like BPE/Unigram on intrinsic metrics and bits-per-byte, is near-globally optimal, and sometimes boosts downstream task performance.
AI.motivation: Existing tokenisation algorithms (e.g., BPE, Unigram) use greedy, locally optimal steps that ignore global properties of the resulting vocabulary. This can lead to suboptimal token sets for language modelling and downstream tasks, and offers no way to quantify how far we are from the true optimum. There is a need for a principled, globally optimised tokeniser with theoretical guarantees and measurable optimality gaps.
AI.method: Formulate the tokeniser construction problem as a linear program whose objective captures an information-theoretic or compression-related criterion (e.g., bits-per-byte). Use convex optimisation tools to solve this LP and obtain a token vocabulary, yielding the ConvexTok algorithm. The method also yields a dual solution providing a lower bound on the optimal objective, enabling certification of near-optimality. Compare ConvexTok against BPE and Unigram on intrinsic tokenisation metrics, bits-per-byte of trained language models, and a range of downstream NLP tasks.
AI.result: ConvexTok consistently outperforms greedy tokenisers (BPE, Unigram) on intrinsic tokenisation metrics and reduces the bits-per-byte achieved by language models across evaluated setups. It often improves downstream task performance, although the gains there are less uniform. The LP-based formulation produces a dual lower bound, and experiments show ConvexTok’s achieved objective is within about 1% of this certified optimum for typical vocabulary sizes.
AI.conclusion: Global, LP-based tokeniser construction via ConvexTok is a viable and empirically strong alternative to greedy methods, offering better compression-oriented metrics and occasional downstream gains, while also providing explicit optimality guarantees. This suggests that moving from heuristic, locally optimal tokenisation to convex-optimisation-based global design can meaningfully improve tokenisers for modern NLP models.

title: Claw AI Lab: An Autonomous Multi-Agent Research Team
comment: Project page and code are available at https://github.com/Claw-AI-Lab/Claw-AI-Lab
summary: We present Claw AI Lab, a lab-native autonomous research platform that advances automated research from a hidden prompt-to-paper pipeline into an interactive AI laboratory. Rather than centering the system around a single agent or a fixed serial workflow, we allow users to instantiate a full research team from one prompt, with customizable roles, collaborative workflows, real-time monitoring, artifact inspection, and rollback/resume control through a unified dashboard. The platform also supports distinct research modes for exploration, multi-agent discussion, and reproduction, making autonomous research substantially more steerable and laboratory-like in practice. A key practical contribution of Claw AI Lab lies in its Claw-Code Harness, which connects local codebases, datasets, and checkpoints to runnable experiments and feeds execution artifacts back into the research loop. As a result, the harness improves not only execution integration, but also experimental completion and result integrity: experiments are easier to inspect, iterate on, and faithfully transfer into final papers, reducing common failure modes such as partial runs and malformed result reporting. In our internal evaluation on five AI research case studies, using AutoResearchClaw as the baseline, Claw AI Lab is consistently preferred by AI expert judges on idea novelty, experiment completeness, and paper presentation quality. We view Claw AI Lab as an early step toward a new paradigm: autonomous research as usable, interactive, and reliability-aware scientific infrastructure.

AI.tldr: Claw AI Lab is an interactive, lab-style autonomous research platform that turns prompt-driven agents into a steerable multi-agent research environment with tight experiment integration.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous research systems often function as opaque prompt-to-paper pipelines centered on a single agent and linear workflow, making them hard to steer, inspect, and reliably translate into high-quality, reproducible research outputs. The authors aim to create a more usable, interactive, and reliability-aware infrastructure that better matches how real laboratories operate.
AI.method: They design Claw AI Lab as a platform where users can instantiate an entire AI research team from a single prompt, assigning customizable roles and collaborative workflows. The system offers an interactive dashboard for real-time monitoring, artifact inspection, and rollback/resume of research processes. It introduces multiple research modes (exploration, multi-agent discussion, reproduction) and a Claw-Code Harness that links local codebases, datasets, and model checkpoints to executable experiments, feeding outputs back into the research loop to guide subsequent steps.
AI.result: In internal evaluations across five AI research case studies, and using AutoResearchClaw as a baseline, expert AI judges consistently prefer Claw AI Lab along dimensions such as idea novelty, completeness of experiments, and quality of paper presentation. The Claw-Code Harness also improves experiment execution integration, completion rates, and the integrity and inspectability of results, addressing issues like partial runs and malformed result reporting.
AI.conclusion: Claw AI Lab demonstrates that autonomous research systems can be re-framed as interactive, lab-like infrastructure rather than monolithic prompt-to-paper pipelines. By enabling multi-agent collaboration, human steering, and tight integration with executable experiments and artifacts, it serves as an early step toward more usable, reliable, and reproducible autonomous research platforms.

title: WorkstreamBench: Evaluating LLM Agents on End-to-End Spreadsheet Tasks in Finance
summary: LLM agents are increasingly expected to carry out end-to-end workflows, producing complete artifacts from high-level user instructions. To meet enterprise needs, frontier AI labs have developed agents that can construct entire spreadsheets from scratch. This is especially relevant in finance, where core workflows such as financial modeling, forecasting, and scenario analysis are commonly conducted through spreadsheets. Yet, existing spreadsheet benchmarks do not measure this advanced capability, focusing instead on question-answering or single-formula edits. To address this gap, we provide one of the first evaluations of agents on end-to-end spreadsheet tasks, focusing on economically critical financial workflows such as modeling and scenario analysis. Since deliverables therein are routinely reviewed and revised by multiple stakeholders, judging their quality necessarily involves high-level criteria such as readability or ease of modification. To reflect the multidimensional nature of solution quality, we develop an evaluation taxonomy comprising three dimensions: Accuracy, Formula, and Format, each comprising fine-grained criteria that reflect professional standards. The Claude family leads the benchmark and produces the most professional-looking outputs in our qualitative review, but even the strongest agents frequently fall short of professional finance standards and degrade sharply as the difficulty increases beyond a few chained calculations. This suggests that current agents are not yet able to reliably produce professional-quality spreadsheets at the level of complexity real-world workflows demand.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces one of the first benchmarks for evaluating LLM agents on end-to-end spreadsheet creation for financial workflows and shows that even top models fall short of professional standards as task complexity grows.
AI.motivation: Existing spreadsheet benchmarks mainly test question answering or small formula edits, not full, end-to-end spreadsheet workflows. Enterprises, especially in finance, need agents that can build complete, professional-grade spreadsheets for tasks like financial modeling, forecasting, and scenario analysis. There is a gap between real-world needs and how current systems are evaluated.
AI.method: The authors design a benchmark of realistic end-to-end spreadsheet tasks centered on economically important financial workflows, such as modeling and scenario analysis. They create an evaluation taxonomy with three main dimensions—Accuracy, Formula, and Format—each further broken into fine-grained criteria aligned with professional finance practices. Multiple LLM agents, including frontier models, are tasked with constructing entire spreadsheets from high-level instructions, and their outputs are evaluated both quantitatively (via the taxonomy) and qualitatively (professional appearance and usability).
AI.result: The Claude family of models achieves the strongest performance on the benchmark and generates the most professional-looking spreadsheets in qualitative assessments. However, all evaluated agents, including the strongest ones, frequently fail to meet professional finance standards, especially when the tasks require more than a few chained calculations. Performance degrades noticeably as workflow complexity increases.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM agents, while capable of producing partially functional spreadsheets and sometimes professional-looking ones, are not yet reliable enough to create full, professional-quality spreadsheets for complex, real-world financial workflows. The proposed benchmark and taxonomy highlight significant gaps and can guide future improvements in agent capabilities and evaluation methods.

title: Is Capability a Liability? More Capable Language Models Make Worse Forecasts When It Matters Most
summary: We document inverse scaling in LLMs on forecasting problems whose underlying time series exhibit superlinear growth and tail risk of regime change, a structure common in finance and epidemiology. On these tasks, more capable models produce worse distributional forecasts. The pattern appears on ForecastBench-Sim (FBSim), a contamination-free, simulated-world benchmark we release, in forecasting synthetic SIR epidemics with a matched linear control, and replicates in real-world datasets on COVID-19, measles, housing markets, and hyperinflation. A per-quantile decomposition shows the failure concentrates at the upper tail, which more capable models shift upward to track aggressive extrapolations of growth, while the lower tail stays put. A within-family study of Llama-3.1 shows that both model scale and post-training independently contribute to this effect. Domain knowledge does not reliably rescue calibration. This inverse scaling does not appear on single-threshold metrics common in LLM forecasting benchmarks, reversing the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on identical outputs. Single-threshold scoring at conventional cutoffs misses the upper-tail cost; tail-inclusive scoring reverses the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on the same outputs. We recommend that LLM forecasting evaluations use continuous (and unbounded) measures of accuracy alongside bounded binary threshold metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper finds that larger, more capable LLMs can actually perform worse at forecasting when time series exhibit superlinear growth and regime-change risk, mainly by overestimating upper-tail outcomes; it introduces a new benchmark, analyzes why this happens, and recommends better evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether increasing LLM capability always improves forecasting performance, especially on realistic time series (finance, epidemiology) that combine fast growth with the possibility of abrupt regime changes, and to test if current evaluation metrics may hide important failure modes.
AI.method: The authors build ForecastBench-Sim (FBSim), a simulated benchmark with contamination-free synthetic time series including SIR epidemics and matched linear controls. They evaluate multiple LLMs, including a within-family scale/post-training study on Llama-3.1, using distributional and per-quantile forecast evaluations. They compare performance on synthetic data and several real-world datasets (COVID-19, measles, housing, hyperinflation), and contrast tail-inclusive scoring with conventional single-threshold metrics.
AI.result: They observe inverse scaling: more capable LLMs produce worse distributional forecasts on time series with superlinear growth and tail risk of regime change, in both synthetic and real-world datasets. Errors concentrate in the upper tail: advanced models shift upper quantiles upward, chasing aggressive growth extrapolations, while lower quantiles remain similar. Both increased model size and post-training independently worsen this pattern in Llama-3.1. Domain knowledge prompts do not reliably fix miscalibration. Standard single-threshold metrics fail to detect this, and can even suggest improved accuracy where tail-aware metrics show degradation.
AI.conclusion: LLM capability does not monotonically improve forecasting quality in settings with superlinear growth and regime-change risk; in fact, larger and more fine-tuned models can be systematically overconfident in the upper tail. Common single-threshold metrics hide these failures and can invert conclusions about capability vs. accuracy. Evaluations of LLM forecasting should incorporate continuous, tail-sensitive, and unbounded scoring rules, not just bounded binary thresholds, to reliably measure and compare model performance.

title: Forecasting Scientific Progress with Artificial Intelligence
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly embedded in scientific discovery, yet whether it can anticipate scientific progress remains unclear. To study this question, we introduce a temporally grounded evaluation framework for forecasting scientific progress under controlled knowledge constraints. We present CUSP (Cutoff-conditioned Unseen Scientific Progress), a multi-disciplinary and event-level benchmark that evaluates scientific forecasting in AI systems through feasibility assessment, mechanistic reasoning, generative solution design, and temporal prediction. Across 4,760 scientific events, we observe systematic and domain-dependent limitations in current frontier models. While models can identify plausible research directions from competing candidates, they fail to reliably predict whether scientific advances will be realized and systematically misestimate when they will occur. Performance is highly heterogeneous across domains, with the timing of AI progress more predictable than advances in biology, chemistry, and physics. Performance is largely insensitive to whether events occur before or after the training cutoff, suggesting these limitations cannot be explained solely by knowledge exposure in training data. Under controlled information access, additional pre-cutoff knowledge improves performance but does not close the gap to full-information settings, which becomes more pronounced for high-citation advances. Models also exhibit systematic overconfidence and strong response biases, indicating unreliable uncertainty estimation. Taken together, current AI systems fall short as predictive tools for scientific progress. Access to prior knowledge does not translate into reliable forecasting, and performance benefits more from post-event information than from forward-looking prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CUSP, a benchmark to test whether AI models can forecast future scientific progress, and finds that current models systematically fail at reliable prediction and timing of scientific advances across domains.
AI.motivation: Although AI is widely used in research, it is not clear if AI can actually anticipate which scientific breakthroughs will happen and when. Existing evaluations mostly test knowledge recall or reasoning on known results, not forward-looking forecasting under realistic knowledge constraints. The authors want to rigorously measure AI’s ability to predict scientific progress itself, across disciplines and time, and to disentangle the role of training data exposure from genuine forecasting ability.
AI.method: The authors build CUSP (Cutoff-conditioned Unseen Scientific Progress), a large, multi-disciplinary benchmark of 4,760 concrete scientific “events” (e.g., specific advances or results), each associated with a publication time and citation impact. They design tasks that require models to: (1) assess feasibility of candidate research directions, (2) reason about underlying mechanisms, (3) propose generative solution ideas, and (4) predict whether and when specific advances occur. They enforce temporal cutoffs and controlled access to pre- and post-cutoff information to separate effects of raw knowledge exposure from predictive generalization. They then test frontier AI models under different knowledge access regimes and analyze performance patterns, biases, and calibration.
AI.result: Models can often tell which future-looking directions are plausible among alternatives, but they perform poorly at predicting which advances are actually realized and at estimating the timing of those advances. Forecasting performance varies significantly by domain: AI progress is relatively more predictable, whereas biology, chemistry, and physics are much harder. Model performance is similar for events occurring before and after the training data cutoff, implying that simple lack of exposure to specific facts is not the main limitation. Providing more pre-cutoff context yields only modest gains and does not approach performance under full-information settings, especially for highly cited breakthroughs. Models are notably overconfident, miscalibrated, and show strong response biases in their predictions.
AI.conclusion: Current AI systems are not reliable tools for forecasting scientific progress. Even when given substantial prior knowledge, they struggle to predict which advances will occur and when, particularly for influential results and in non-AI domains. Their gains from post hoc information exceed their forward-looking predictive power, and their uncertainty estimates are unreliable. Progress in AI-for-science will require methods that go beyond pattern-matching over historical literature and specifically target robust, calibrated forecasting of novel scientific developments.

title: Beyond the Org Chart: AI and the Transformation of Invisible Work
summary: An increasing number of news and research articles report that AI adoption is allowing professionals to blur and extend the boundaries of their corporate roles. With the goal of understanding how work processes might be changing in an AI-forward company, we interviewed 24 product-focused individuals at a large technology firm about how AI has impacted their own work, their work within their product team, and their professional interactions. Our conversations suggest that AI is not only changing formal role responsibilities and collaborations between those roles, but also changing informal cultural practices like professional mentoring that are key to helping professionals settle in their positions, stay engaged with their work, and grow their careers. Some of these changes are positive, such as smoother collaboration between peers, but other changes are more nuanced and put the typical career growth opportunities, like receiving feedback from professional networks and promoting leadership and mentorship, at risk. We propose steps that AI companies can take to make the invisible work more visible. Additionally, we propose efforts that individuals and leaders can take to support their colleagues through AI transformation while preserving healthy company cultures that support diverse thinking, collaboration, and informal interactions.

AI.tldr: Study of how AI adoption changes product professionals’ roles, team dynamics, and informal culture (e.g., mentoring) in a large tech firm, with recommendations to preserve healthy, collaborative workplaces.
AI.motivation: AI tools are rapidly being integrated into knowledge work, but most discussion focuses on productivity and task automation, not on how AI reshapes professional roles, collaboration, mentoring, and career development inside organizations. The authors want to uncover these less visible social and cultural changes in an AI-forward company.
AI.method: Qualitative study: semi-structured interviews with 24 product-focused employees at a large technology company. Participants were asked how AI has affected their individual work, their product team’s work, and their broader professional interactions. The authors thematically analyzed the interview data to characterize changes in roles, collaboration, and culture.
AI.result: AI adoption was found to alter not only formal responsibilities and cross-role collaboration, but also informal cultural practices such as mentoring, feedback seeking, and networking. Some impacts were beneficial (e.g., smoother peer collaboration, possibly more efficient workflows), while others were ambiguous or negative, including reduced visibility of certain types of ‘invisible work’ and potential erosion of traditional career growth mechanisms like mentorship and leadership development opportunities.
AI.conclusion: AI transformations in organizations have complex socio-cultural effects beyond efficiency. To avoid undermining career development and healthy culture, AI companies should introduce practices and structures that surface invisible contributions and protect mentoring and feedback channels. Leaders and individual contributors alike need to take deliberate steps to sustain diverse thinking, collaboration, and informal interactions as AI reshapes work processes and role boundaries.

title: AMEL: Accumulated Message Effects on LLM Judgments
comment: 19 pages, 14 figures, 6 tables. Single author. Code, data (75,898 deduplicated API responses), and analysis pipeline at https://github.com/chutapp/amel
summary:
Large language models are routinely used as automated evaluators: to review code, moderate content, or score outputs, often with many items passing through one conversation. We ask whether the polarity of prior conversation history biases subsequent judgments, an effect we call the accumulated message effect on LLM judgments (AMEL). Across 75,898 API calls to 11 models from 4 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and four open-source models), we present identical test items in isolation or following histories saturated with predominantly positive or negative evaluations.
  Models shift toward the conversation's prevailing polarity (d = -0.17, p < 10^-46). The effect concentrates on items where the model is genuinely uncertain at baseline (d = -0.34 for high-entropy items, vs d = -0.15 when the baseline is deterministic). Bias does not grow with context length: 5 prior turns and 50 produce the same shift (Spearman |r| < 0.01; OLS slope p = 0.80). And there is a negativity asymmetry: paired per item, negative histories induce 1.62x more bias than positive (t = 13.46, p < 10^-39, n = 2,481). Scaling helps but does not solve it (Anthropic: Haiku -0.22 to Opus -0.17; OpenAI: Nano -0.34 to GPT-5.2 -0.17).
  Three follow-ups narrow the mechanism. The token probability distribution shifts continuously, not at a threshold. The negativity asymmetry has both token-level and semantic components, though attributing the balance is exploratory at our sample sizes. Position does not matter: five biased turns anywhere in a 50-turn history produce the same shift. The simplest fix for evaluation pipelines is a fresh context per item; when batching is unavoidable, balancing the history helps.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how prior conversation history biases large language models’ subsequent evaluations, defining and measuring the “accumulated message effect on LLM judgments” (AMEL).
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used as automated evaluators (code review, content moderation, scoring), often processing many items in a single conversation. If their judgments are biased by earlier turns in the dialogue (e.g., long streaks of positive or negative evaluations), this threatens reliability, fairness, and repeatability of LLM-based evaluation pipelines. The authors want to quantify whether such history-dependent bias exists, how large it is, and what factors affect it (uncertainty, context length, polarity, and model scale).
AI.method: They run 75,898 API calls across 11 models from four providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, plus several open-source models). Identical test items are presented either (a) in isolation or (b) after conversation histories engineered to have predominantly positive or negative evaluations. They compare the resulting judgments, with attention to baseline uncertainty (entropy of model predictions), context length (5 vs 50 prior turns), polarity asymmetry (positive vs negative histories), and model size. Follow-up experiments analyze token-level probability shifts, the role of semantics vs lexical/token effects in negativity asymmetry, and whether the position of biased turns in long histories matters.
AI.result: (1) Models’ judgments systematically shift toward the prevailing polarity of the conversation history with a moderate standardized effect size (overall d ≈ -0.17, highly significant). (2) The bias is strongest on items where the model is uncertain at baseline (d ≈ -0.34 for high-entropy items vs ≈ -0.15 for deterministic ones). (3) Longer context does not increase the magnitude of bias: 5 vs 50 prior turns yield essentially the same shift (negligible Spearman correlation and non-significant OLS slope). (4) Negative histories induce substantially more bias than positive ones—about 1.62× as large—showing a pronounced negativity asymmetry. (5) Larger, more capable models reduce but do not eliminate the effect across both Anthropic and OpenAI families. (6) The distribution over next-token probabilities drifts smoothly rather than exhibiting a discrete threshold. (7) Both token-level and semantic factors contribute to the negativity asymmetry, though disentangling them remains tentative. (8) The position of biased messages within a long history does not matter: five biased turns anywhere in a 50-turn context produce similar bias.
AI.conclusion: LLM evaluations are path-dependent: prior conversation polarity systematically biases subsequent judgments, especially on ambiguous items, with a stronger effect from negative than positive histories. The effect does not accumulate with longer context and persists even in larger models. For practical evaluation pipelines, the safest mitigation is to evaluate each item in a fresh context; when batching is necessary, enforcing balanced histories (e.g., mixing positive and negative content) can reduce bias. The work underscores that LLM-based evaluation is not context-invariant and requires careful prompt and pipeline design to avoid systematic drift.

title: Parametric Modular Answer Set Programs Made Declarative
comment: To appear in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming
summary: In this paper, we explore the concept of modularity in first-order answer set programming (ASP). We introduce a new formalism called parametric modular logic programs, which allows defining subprograms with parameters and intensionality statements. We demonstrate how this formalism can capture the semantics of clingo-programs with collective control, a feature that enables structuring and instantiating subprograms. We provide theoretical foundations for modular ASP, illustrate its usefulness, and connect to traditional non-modular ASP.

AI.tldr: The paper defines a formal framework for modularity in first-order answer set programming via parametric modular logic programs and shows how it captures clingo’s collective control and relates to standard ASP.
AI.motivation: ASP programs can grow large and complex, and current practice (e.g., in clingo) offers pragmatic mechanisms for structuring code, such as subprograms and collective control, without a fully developed formal semantic foundation. There is a need for a rigorous notion of modularity in first-order ASP that supports parameterized subprograms, clarifies their semantics, and relates them cleanly to traditional non-modular ASP, so that modular engineering of ASP knowledge bases becomes principled and analyzable.
AI.method: The authors introduce a new formalism called parametric modular logic programs. In this framework, programs are decomposed into subprograms with explicitly declared parameters and intensionality statements, specifying which predicates are defined within a module. They then define a formal semantics for these modular programs and show how clingo-programs with collective control can be translated into this formalism. The paper develops the theoretical underpinnings—such as semantic equivalence and correspondence results—linking modular and non-modular ASP, supported by examples that illustrate how real clingo constructs are represented.
AI.result: The paper establishes a formal semantic framework for modularity in first-order ASP via parametric modular logic programs, proves that clingo-programs using collective control can be represented within this framework, and shows correspondence results that connect the new modular semantics to the standard semantics of non-modular ASP. It demonstrates, through examples, that common structuring patterns in clingo can be captured and reasoned about modularly.
AI.conclusion: Parametric modular logic programs provide a principled way to express and analyze modularity in first-order ASP. They give a clear semantics to clingo’s collective control and similar structuring features, while remaining compatible with traditional ASP. This lays a theoretical foundation for modular ASP programming and supports more systematic, scalable development of complex ASP applications.

title: Can AI Make Conflicts Worse? An Alignment Failure in LLM Deployment Across Conflict Contexts
comment: Preprint. 8 pages, 2 figures. Code and evaluation framework: https://github.com/akryshtal/conflict-sensitivity-eval-bloom
summary: AI models are already deployed in societies affected by armed conflict, and journalists, humanitarian workers, governments and ordinary citizens rely on them for information or for their work processes. No established practice exists for checking whether their outputs can make those conflicts worse. We tested nine model configurations from four providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, xAI) on 90 multi-turn scenarios designed to surface misaligned behaviour in conflict contexts: false equivalence between documented atrocities, denial of genocide, and failure to recognise ethnic slurs, among others. When such outputs feed into journalism, humanitarian reporting, or public debate, they can deepen divisions in fragile societies. Failure rates span 6\% to 47\% between the best and worst performing models, which makes model choice a safety question in its own right and when users pushed for ``balance'' in cases where international courts have already assigned responsibility, five of nine configurations failed 80 to 100 percent of the time. We release the first evaluation framework for this domain and propose adding it to alignment evaluation portfolios.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how current AI models behave in armed-conflict contexts, showing substantial failure rates that can exacerbate harm, and introduces a new benchmark for this domain.
AI.motivation: AI systems are already being used by people operating in or reporting on armed conflicts, but there is no standard way to test whether these models respond safely and accurately in such sensitive, high-stakes contexts. Misaligned outputs—such as denying atrocities, equating perpetrators and victims, or missing hate speech—can inflame tensions and worsen conflicts. The authors want to quantify these risks and provide a systematic evaluation tool.
AI.method: The authors design 90 multi-turn dialogue scenarios that mimic real-world conflict-related interactions (e.g., questions about atrocities, genocide, ethnic slurs, and requests for misleading ‘balance’). They then evaluate nine different model configurations from four providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, xAI) on these scenarios, measuring how often each model produces misaligned or harmful responses according to predefined criteria. This forms an evaluation framework tailored to conflict contexts.
AI.result: Across the scenarios, failure rates vary substantially across models, from as low as 6% to as high as 47% depending on the configuration. In scenarios where users explicitly push for ‘balanced’ treatments of events for which international courts have already assigned responsibility, a majority of models (five out of nine configurations) fail in 80–100% of cases, producing misleading or harmful ‘both-sides’ responses.
AI.conclusion: Model behaviour in armed-conflict contexts differs markedly across providers and configurations, and these differences have direct safety implications: simply choosing a different model can substantially change the risk of harmful outputs. The authors argue that their conflict-focused evaluation suite should become part of standard AI alignment assessment, helping developers identify and mitigate misaligned behaviour before deployment in sensitive real-world settings.

title: Beyond Acoustic Emotion Recognition: Multimodal Pathos Analysis in Political Speech Using LLM-Based and Acoustic Emotion Models
summary: We investigate whether acoustic emotion recognition models can serve as proxies for the Pathos dimension in political speech analysis, as operationalised by the TRUST multi-agent large language model (LLM) pipeline. Using a Bundestag plenary speech by Felix Banaszak (51 segments, 245 s) as a case study, we compare three analysis modalities: (1) emotion2vec_plus_large, an acoustic speech emotion recognition (SER) model whose continuous Arousal and Valence values are derived via post-hoc Russell Circumplex projection; (2) Gemini 2.5 Flash, an LLM analysing the full speech audio together with its transcript in an open-ended, context-aware fashion; and (3) TRUST-Pathos scores from a three-advocate LLM supervisor ensemble. Spearman rank correlations reveal that Gemini Valence correlates strongly with TRUST-Pathos (rho = +0.664, p < 0.001), whereas emotion2vec Valence does not (rho = +0.097, p = 0.499). We further demonstrate, via a systematic quality evaluation of the Berlin Database of Emotional Speech (EMO-DB) using Gemini in an open-ended annotation paradigm, that standard SER benchmark corpora suffer from acted speech, cultural bias, and category incompatibility. Our results suggest that LLM-based multimodal analysis captures semantically defined political emotion substantially better than acoustic models alone, while acoustic features remain informative for low-level Arousal estimation. Future work will extend this approach to video-based analysis incorporating facial expression and gaze.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether acoustic speech emotion recognition (SER) models can approximate Pathos (emotional appeal) in political speech and finds that multimodal LLM analysis aligns much better with a Pathos benchmark than acoustic-only models.
AI.motivation: Pathos, or emotional appeal, is central to understanding political speeches, but is hard to quantify automatically. Traditional acoustic SER models are widely used yet may not capture the semantically rich, context-dependent emotions in political discourse. With the advent of multimodal LLMs and specialized political rhetoric pipelines (like TRUST), it is important to know whether simpler acoustic models are adequate proxies for Pathos, or whether richer multimodal LLMs are needed.
AI.method: Using a 245-second German Bundestag speech (51 segments) as a case study, the authors compare three modalities: (1) an acoustic SER model (emotion2vec_plus_large) that outputs continuous Arousal and Valence via Russell Circumplex projection; (2) a multimodal LLM (Gemini 2.5 Flash) that analyzes both audio and transcript in an open-ended, context-aware way; and (3) TRUST-Pathos scores produced by a three-advocate LLM ensemble. They compute Spearman rank correlations between each modality’s Valence estimates and TRUST-Pathos. Additionally, they systematically evaluate Gemini’s open-ended emotion annotations on EMO-DB to diagnose quality issues in standard SER benchmarks.
AI.result: Gemini’s Valence scores correlate strongly and significantly with TRUST-Pathos (rho = 0.664, p < 0.001), indicating substantial alignment with a rhetorically informed Pathos measure. In contrast, emotion2vec’s Valence shows no meaningful correlation with TRUST-Pathos (rho = 0.097, p = 0.499). The EMO-DB analysis using Gemini reveals limitations of conventional SER datasets, including acted speech artifacts, cultural bias, and mismatches between discrete emotion labels and more nuanced categories used in practice.
AI.conclusion: Acoustic SER models, at least in the tested configuration, are poor proxies for semantically defined Pathos in political speech, though they still provide useful low-level Arousal cues. Multimodal LLM-based analysis that integrates audio and text better captures the complex emotional meaning relevant to political rhetoric. The authors advocate moving beyond standard acoustic benchmarks toward richer, context-sensitive multimodal methods and plan to extend their framework to include video features such as facial expressions and gaze.

title: Towards a General Intelligence and Interface for Wearable Health Data
summary: While ubiquitous wearable sensors capture a wealth of behavioral and physiological information, effectively transforming these signals into personalized health insights is challenging. Specifically, converting low-level sensor data into representations capable of characterizing higher-level states is difficult due to high phenotypic diversity and variation in individual baseline health, physiology, and lifestyle factors. Moreover, collecting wearable data paired with health outcome annotations is laborious and expensive, and retrospective annotation remains practically unfeasible, contributing to a scarcity of data with high-quality labels. To overcome these limitations, we propose a foundation model for wearable health that is pretrained on more than one trillion minutes of unlabeled sensor signals drawn from a large cohort of five million participants. We demonstrate that the joint scaling of model capacity and pretraining data volume leads to systematic improvements in performance, as evaluated on a diverse set of 35 health prediction tasks, spanning cardiovascular, metabolic, sleep, and mental health, as well as lifestyle choices and demographic factors. We find that this population scale representation unlocks label-efficient few-shot learning and generative capabilities for robust daily metric estimation. To further leverage this learned representation, we deploy a classroom of LLM agents to autonomously search the space of downstream predictive heads built on the model embeddings, showing broad performance improvements that increase with LLM model capacity. Finally, we show how integrating these downstream predictors into a Personal Health Agent can support model responses that are more relevant, contextually aware, and safe, and we validate this via 1,860 ratings from a cohort of clinicians.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a large-scale foundation model for wearable-sensor health data, pretrained on over one trillion minutes from 5 million people, which enables strong, label-efficient performance across 35 diverse health prediction tasks and improves when combined with LLM-based head search and a Personal Health Agent interface validated by clinicians.
AI.motivation: Wearable devices collect massive amounts of low-level sensor signals, but turning these into accurate, personalized, high-level health insights is hard because of individual variability and the scarcity and cost of well-labeled health outcome data. Existing models struggle to generalize across people and tasks, and cannot fully exploit unlabeled population-scale data.
AI.method: The authors pretrain a large foundation model on more than one trillion minutes of unlabeled wearable sensor data from five million participants, learning generic representations of human physiology and behavior. They then evaluate scaling laws by increasing both model capacity and data volume and testing on 35 downstream health tasks. On top of the learned embeddings, they use a set of LLM agents to autonomously design and search over downstream prediction heads. They also integrate these predictors into a Personal Health Agent system that uses the model outputs to generate health guidance, and assess its responses with clinician ratings.
AI.result: Scaling both model size and pretraining data improves performance systematically across 35 downstream tasks related to cardiovascular, metabolic, sleep, mental health, lifestyle, and demographics. The learned representation supports label-efficient few-shot learning and enables generative modeling for robust daily metric estimation. LLM-driven head search further boosts performance, with gains increasing as the LLMs grow in capacity. The resulting Personal Health Agent produces responses that clinicians rate as more relevant, contextually aware, and safe, based on 1,860 evaluations.
AI.conclusion: A population-scale foundation model for wearable sensor data can convert unlabeled signals into powerful, general-purpose health representations, enabling strong performance and label efficiency across many tasks. Augmenting this representation with LLM-designed prediction heads and integrating it into a Personal Health Agent yields practically useful, clinically validated improvements in the quality and safety of health-related responses. This paradigm suggests a scalable path for leveraging massive unlabeled wearable data to support personalized digital health applications.

title: Advancing Mathematics Research with AI-Driven Formal Proof Search
comment: The first three authors and the last author have equal contributions. The first three authors are in random order
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly excel at mathematical reasoning, but their unreliability limits their utility in mathematics research. A mitigation is using LLMs to generate formal proofs in languages like Lean. We perform the first large-scale evaluation of this method's ability to solve open problems. Our most capable agent autonomously resolved 9 of 353 open Erdős problems at the per-problem cost of a few hundred dollars, proved 44/492 OEIS conjectures, and is being deployed in combinatorics, optimization, graph theory, algebraic geometry, and quantum optics research. A basic agent alternating LLM-based generation with Lean-based verification replicated the Erdős successes but proved costlier on the hardest problems. These findings demonstrate the power of AI-aided formal proof search and shed light on the agent designs that enable it.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates large language model (LLM) agents that search for and verify formal proofs (in Lean) to tackle real open mathematical problems, showing they can autonomously solve a nontrivial number of Erdős and OEIS conjectures.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly good at mathematical reasoning but remain unreliable and untrusted for serious mathematics research. Formal proof assistants like Lean can verify correctness but require significant human effort to construct proofs. The paper aims to test whether combining LLMs with formal verification can reliably attack open problems at scale, and to understand which agent designs make this practical and cost-effective.
AI.method: The authors build autonomous proof-search agents that use powerful LLMs to generate candidate Lean proofs and Lean to mechanically verify them. They design multiple agent configurations, including a more capable system and a basic one that alternates between LLM-based proof generation and Lean-based checking. They then run large-scale experiments on two benchmarks of genuine open problems: 353 open Erdős problems and 492 conjectures from the OEIS, measuring solved instances, computational/monetary cost, and behavior across mathematical domains. They compare performance and cost-efficiency across agent designs.
AI.result: The most capable agent autonomously solved 9 of 353 open Erdős problems at a per-problem cost of a few hundred dollars, and proved 44 of 492 OEIS conjectures. It has also begun to be practically deployed on problems in several mathematical subfields, including combinatorics, optimization, graph theory, algebraic geometry, and quantum optics. A simpler baseline agent, which repeatedly alternates between LLM generation and Lean verification, managed to replicate the Erdős problem successes but at higher cost on the hardest instances. This establishes that AI-driven formal proof search can nontrivially contribute to open mathematical research problems, with meaningful differences between agent designs in efficiency.
AI.conclusion: AI agents that combine LLM-based proof generation with formal verification in systems like Lean can autonomously resolve a nontrivial number of genuine open problems in mathematics, at a cost that is feasible relative to research value. The study shows that careful agent design significantly impacts efficiency and scalability, and it positions AI-aided formal proof search as a powerful emerging tool for mathematicians across multiple disciplines.

title: LCGuard: Latent Communication Guard for Safe KV Sharing in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems increasingly rely on intermediate communication to coordinate complex tasks. While most existing systems communicate through natural language, recent work shows that latent communication, particularly through transformer key-value (KV) caches, can improve efficiency and preserve richer task-relevant information. However, KV caches also encode contextual inputs, intermediate reasoning states, and agent-specific information, creating an opaque channel through which sensitive content may propagate across agents without explicit textual disclosure. To address this, we introduce \textbf{LCGuard} (Latent Communication Guard), a framework for safe KV-based latent communication in multi-agent LLM systems. LCGuard treats shared KV caches as latent working memory and learns representation-level transformations before cache artifacts are transmitted across agents. We formalize representation-level sensitive information leakage operationally through reconstruction: a shared cache artifact is unsafe if an adversarial decoder can recover agent-specific sensitive inputs from it. This leads to an adversarial training formulation in which the adversary learns to reconstruct sensitive inputs, while LCGuard learns transformations that preserve task-relevant semantics and reduce reconstructable information. Empirical evaluations across multiple model families and multi-agent benchmarks show that LCGuard consistently reduces reconstruction-based leakage and attack success rates while maintaining competitive task performance compared to standard KV-sharing baselines.

AI.tldr: LCGuard is a framework that makes KV-cache based latent communication between LLM agents safer by transforming shared representations to minimize reconstructable sensitive information while preserving task performance.
AI.motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems often share information via natural language, but KV-cache based latent communication is emerging as a more efficient, information-rich alternative. However, KV caches contain contextual inputs, intermediate reasoning states, and agent-specific data, creating an opaque channel where sensitive information may silently leak between agents. Existing safety approaches focus on text-level filtering and do not address this latent channel, creating a gap for methods that explicitly manage and bound representation-level leakage in KV sharing.
AI.method: LCGuard models shared KV caches as latent working memory and applies learnable representation-level transformations to cache artifacts before they are transmitted to other agents. The authors define sensitive information leakage operationally via reconstructability: a cache artifact is deemed unsafe if an adversarial decoder can recover sensitive agent-specific inputs from it. They formulate an adversarial training setup where (1) an adversary network is trained to reconstruct sensitive inputs from transformed caches, and (2) LCGuard is trained to learn cache transformations that hinder this reconstruction while preserving task-relevant semantics and downstream performance.
AI.result: Across multiple LLM families and multi-agent benchmarks, LCGuard substantially reduces reconstruction-based leakage from shared KV caches and lowers the success rates of attacks that attempt to recover sensitive information, compared to standard KV-sharing baselines. At the same time, it maintains competitive task performance, indicating that its transformations effectively obscure sensitive content without significantly degrading coordination or task success in multi-agent settings.
AI.conclusion: Representation-level safety mechanisms are necessary when using KV-cache based latent communication in LLM multi-agent systems. LCGuard demonstrates that adversarially trained transformations over KV caches can meaningfully reduce sensitive information leakage under reconstruction-based threat models while preserving the utility of latent communication. This suggests a practical path toward safer, privacy-aware multi-agent architectures that go beyond text-only safety filters.

title: Gated DeltaNet-2: Decoupling Erase and Write in Linear Attention
comment: Gated DeltaNet-2 technical report; code at https://github.com/NVlabs/GatedDeltaNet-2
summary: Linear attention replaces the unbounded cache of softmax attention with a fixed-size recurrent state, reducing sequence mixing to linear time and decoding to constant memory. The hard part is not just what to forget, but how to edit this compressed memory without scrambling existing associations. Delta-rule models subtract the current read before writing a new value, and Kimi Delta Attention (KDA) sharpens forgetting with channel-wise decay. But the active edit still uses a single scalar gate to control two different things: how much old content to erase on the key side and how much new content to commit on the value side. We introduce Gated DeltaNet-2, which generalizes both Gated DeltaNet and KDA by inheriting adaptive forgetting and channel-wise decay while addressing their shared limitation, the scalar tie between erasing and writing. Gated Delta Rule-2 separates these roles with a channel-wise erase gate b_t and a channel-wise write gate w_t, reducing to KDA when both gates collapse to the same scalar and to Gated DeltaNet when the decay also collapses. We derive a fast-weight update view, a chunkwise WY algorithm with channel-wise decay absorbed into asymmetric erase factors, and a gate-aware backward pass that preserves efficient parallel training. At 1.3B parameters trained on 100B FineWeb-Edu tokens, Gated DeltaNet-2 achieves the strongest overall results among Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet, KDA, and Mamba-3 variants across language modeling, commonsense reasoning, and retrieval. Its advantage is most pronounced on long-context RULER needle-in-a-haystack benchmarks, where it improves the evaluated multi-key retrieval setting and remains strong in both recurrent and hybrid settings. Code is available at https://github.com/NVlabs/GatedDeltaNet-2.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Gated DeltaNet-2, a new linear attention architecture that decouples forgetting and writing with separate channel-wise gates, improving efficiency and long-context performance over prior delta-rule and Mamba-style models.
AI.motivation: Linear attention offers constant-memory decoding and linear-time sequence mixing by using a fixed-size recurrent state instead of a growing key-value cache. However, existing delta-rule based linear attention models struggle to precisely modify this compressed memory: they typically use a single scalar gate to jointly control how much old information is erased and how much new information is written. This coupling limits their ability to selectively update memory without damaging useful stored associations, which is particularly problematic for long-context tasks and fine-grained retrieval.
AI.method: The authors introduce Gated DeltaNet-2, a linear attention model that generalizes Gated DeltaNet and Kimi Delta Attention (KDA). The core innovation is Gated Delta Rule-2, which uses separate channel-wise erase (b_t) and write (w_t) gates, instead of a single scalar gate, to independently control forgetting and writing in the recurrent state. The model also retains channel-wise decay for sharper, adaptive forgetting. The paper provides: (1) a fast-weight update interpretation of the mechanism; (2) a chunkwise WY algorithm in which channel-wise decay is folded into asymmetric erase factors for efficient computation; and (3) a gate-aware backward pass that keeps training parallel and efficient.
AI.result: With 1.3B parameters trained on 100B FineWeb-Edu tokens, Gated DeltaNet-2 outperforms Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet, KDA, and Mamba-3 variants on a suite of benchmarks including language modeling, commonsense reasoning, and retrieval. The gains are largest on long-context RULER needle-in-a-haystack tasks, especially in multi-key retrieval and across both recurrent-only and hybrid (attention + recurrence) settings.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling erasure and writing through channel-wise gates in a linear attention framework yields more precise and powerful memory editing, while preserving the computational and memory benefits of linear attention. Gated DeltaNet-2 establishes a new state of the art among related models, particularly for long-context retrieval, and its accompanying algorithms (fast-weight view, chunkwise WY with decay, and gate-aware backprop) make it practical for large-scale training and deployment.

title: MOSS: Self-Evolution through Source-Level Rewriting in Autonomous Agent Systems
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Preprint. Code: https://github.com/dav-joy-thon/MOSS
summary: Autonomous agentic systems are largely static after deployment: they do not learn from user interactions, and recurring failures persist until the next human-driven update ships a fix. Self-evolving agents have emerged in response, but all confine evolution to text-mutable artifacts -- skill files, prompt configurations, memory schemas, workflow graphs -- and leave the agent harness untouched. Since routing, hook ordering, state invariants, and dispatch live in code rather than in any text artifact, an entire class of structural failure is physically unreachable from the text layer. We argue that source-level adaptation is a fundamentally more general medium: it is Turing-complete, a strict superset of every text-mutable scope, takes effect deterministically rather than through base-model compliance, and does not erode under long-context drift. We present MOSS, a system that performs self-rewriting at the source level on production agentic substrates. Each evolution is anchored to an automatically curated batch of production-failure evidence and proceeds through a deterministic multi-stage pipeline; code modification is delegated to a pluggable external coding-agent CLI while MOSS retains stage ordering and verdicts. Candidates are verified by replaying the batch against the candidate image in ephemeral trial workers, then promoted via user-consent-gated, in-place container swap with health-probe-gated rollback. On OpenClaw, MOSS lifts a four-task mean grader score from 0.25 to 0.61 in a single cycle without human intervention.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MOSS, a system that enables production autonomous agents to self-evolve by rewriting their own source code, not just text-config artifacts, yielding large performance gains without human intervention.
AI.motivation: Most deployed autonomous agents are static after release and cannot learn from real-world interaction failures until humans manually patch them. Existing "self-evolving" approaches only modify text-based configurations (skills, prompts, workflows) and cannot touch the core agent harness code where many structural failures reside (routing, dispatch, invariants). This leaves a major class of persistent errors unfixable by current self-evolution frameworks. The authors are motivated to build a system that can adapt the actual source code of agentic systems safely and deterministically based on production failure evidence.
AI.method: The authors propose MOSS, a self-rewriting framework that operates at the source-code level of production agent systems. Each evolution cycle starts from an automatically collected batch of production failure traces. A deterministic multi-stage pipeline orchestrates the evolution: MOSS delegates concrete code-editing to an external, pluggable coding-agent CLI, while it controls stage ordering and decisions. It generates candidate code revisions, then verifies them by replaying the curated failure batch against each candidate in isolated, ephemeral trial workers. Successful candidates can be promoted via a user-consent-gated, in-place container swap, protected by health probes and rollback mechanisms to maintain system stability.
AI.result: In experiments on OpenClaw (presumably a benchmark or production-like agent platform), applying a single MOSS self-evolution cycle improved the mean grader score across four tasks from 0.25 to 0.61. This demonstrates that source-level self-rewriting can substantially enhance agent performance on real tasks without any human-crafted fixes in the loop, given only production failure evidence.
AI.conclusion: Source-level adaptation is more expressive and reliable than limiting self-evolution to text-mutable artifacts, because it is Turing-complete, includes all configuration scopes as a subset, behaves deterministically rather than relying on base-model compliance, and is robust to long-context drift. MOSS shows that it is practical to deploy self-rewriting at the source level in production agentic systems, with automated failure-driven evolution, verification via replay, and safe deployment (consent-gated promotion and rollback). The system can meaningfully and autonomously improve agent performance, suggesting that future autonomous agents should be designed for safe source-level self-modification rather than only prompt- or config-level evolution.
